---
title: "Predicting Airbnb Superhost Status and Nightly Pricing in European Cities"
subtitle: "Final Project — Advanced Modelling | UC3M · Master in Computational Social Science · 2025–2026"
author: "Abdullah Tadmuri"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 3
    toc_float:
      collapsed: false
      smooth_scroll: true
    theme: flatly
    highlight: zenburn
    code_folding: show
    css: style.css
    fig_width: 20
    fig_height: 14
---

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

This project applies 21 supervised learning models (13 classification, 8 regression) to Airbnb listing data from Madrid, Barcelona, and Amsterdam (November 2025 scrape, ~50,000 listings). The classification task predicts Superhost status; the regression task predicts nightly price. Random Forest achieves the highest classification AUC, while XGBoost leads the regression task. The central finding, confirmed across SHAP values, GAM smooth terms, and variable importance rankings, is that Superhost status is driven by host behaviour (response rate, review activity, tenure) while pricing is driven by property characteristics (capacity, location, neighbourhood context). Beyond the course curriculum, the project implements Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks, model stacking, calibration analysis, and counterfactual pathway analysis.

---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}

knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo    = TRUE,
  warning = FALSE,
  message = FALSE,
  fig.align = "center",
  cache   = FALSE,
  dpi     = 300
)

# Force reticulate to use a stable Python environment for KAN (if/when enabled)
# Must happen before any reticulate import/initialization elsewhere in the document.
kan_py_path <- path.expand("~/venvs/r-kan311/bin/python")
if (file.exists(kan_py_path)) {
  reticulate::use_python(kan_py_path, required = TRUE)
} else {
  warning("KAN Python environment not found at: ", kan_py_path)
}

# KAN is experimental and unstable inside knit on this machine.
# We run KAN externally in Python and import predictions back into R.
run_kan <- FALSE              # classification KAN in-session (reticulate) -> keep OFF
# Allow pipeline script to override these via envir (don't clobber if already set)
if (!exists("run_kan_export"))     run_kan_export <- FALSE
if (!exists("run_kan_reg_export")) run_kan_reg_export <- FALSE



```



# 1. Introduction

Airbnb has grown from a San Francisco startup in 2008 into one of the world's largest accommodation platforms, with millions of listings in over 220 countries. The platform publishes detailed listing data through Inside Airbnb, which makes it possible to study how hosts operate, how guests choose, and how prices form in competitive local markets.

## 1.1 Purpose and Motivation

I had three goals for this project:

1. **Compare a wide range of supervised learning methods** on a real dataset with the kind of problems you run into in practice: class imbalance, mixed feature types, geographic differences, and non-linear relationships. I wanted to work through the full modelling pipeline we covered in this course, from raw data all the way to a trained, evaluated, and interpretable model.

2. **Go beyond prediction accuracy.** Getting a high AUC is not that useful on its own. I also wanted to check whether the models produce reliable probabilities (calibration), figure out which features actually drive predictions (SHAP, GAM smooth terms, counterfactual analysis), and test whether performance holds up across different cities. Interpretability and diagnostics are a big part of this project, not an afterthought.

3. **Find something substantive in the data.** The two prediction tasks, Superhost classification and nightly price regression, were chosen because they look at the same platform from different angles. What ended up being interesting is that the features that predict *who a host is* are almost entirely different from the features that predict *what a listing costs*. That contrast shows up consistently across multiple model families and interpretability methods, and it became the central finding of the project.

## 1.2 Research Questions

The analysis is built around two prediction tasks, using listing data from three European cities (**Madrid**, **Barcelona**, and **Amsterdam**), each with its own regulatory environment, tourism profile, and housing market dynamics:

1. **Classification (Superhost status)**: Can we predict whether a host holds Superhost status from their behaviour metrics, property features, neighbourhood context, and text-derived signals? This is a binary classification problem with a naturally imbalanced target.

2. **Regression (Nightly price)**: What features explain variation in nightly rental prices, and how accurately can we predict a listing's price? I work on the log-price scale to handle the right-skewed distribution and back-transform for dollar-scale interpretation.

## 1.3 Methodological Overview

The project covers the full supervised learning pipeline taught in this course, and extends it in several directions:

- **Feature engineering**: neighbourhood-level market indicators (median price, Superhost density) and text-derived keyword flags from listing names and descriptions
- **Class balancing**: majority-class downsampling to address the Superhost imbalance
- **Classification models (13)**: Logistic Regression, Elastic Net, Random Forest, XGBoost, GAM, Neural Network, Decision Tree with pruning, LDA, QDA/RDA, KNN, Naive Bayes, SVM, and KAN
- **Regression models (8)**: OLS, Stepwise selection (AIC/BIC), PCR, PLS, Elastic Net, Random Forest, XGBoost, GAM, plus KAN where available
- **Interpretability**: SHAP values with beeswarm and dependence plots, odds ratios, variable importance, ICE/PDP curves, GAM smooth terms, and Elastic Net regularisation paths
- **Model evaluation**: ROC and precision-recall curves, calibration analysis, Youden's J threshold optimisation, cost-sensitive threshold tuning, bootstrap confidence intervals, and pairwise significance tests
- **Ensemble learning**: model stacking with out-of-fold predictions and prediction diversity analysis
- **Advanced diagnostics**: learning curves, residual analysis, price tier segmentation, city-stratified generalisation testing, anomaly detection, and a counterfactual "pathway to Superhost" analysis

## 1.4 Data Source

The data comes from **Inside Airbnb** (Inside Airbnb, 2024), an independent project that scrapes and publishes Airbnb listing information for public interest research. I use the November 2025 scrape for Madrid, Barcelona, and Amsterdam. These three cities differ quite a bit in regulatory strictness, tourism volume, and average price levels, which makes them a good testbed for checking whether models generalise.

## 1.5 Related Work

Airbnb pricing has attracted significant attention from both the hospitality literature and the machine learning community. Kalehbasti et al. (2021) applied gradient-boosted trees to Airbnb listings in major US cities and found that location, property type, and amenity count were the strongest price predictors, which is consistent with what this project finds for European cities. Zhang et al. (2017) used hedonic pricing models to decompose Airbnb prices into property-level and neighbourhood-level components, a framing that motivates our engineered neighbourhood features.

On the classification side, Superhost prediction is less studied. Liang et al. (2020) showed that review sentiment and host response behaviour predict Superhost status, though their analysis was limited to a single city. This project extends that work across three markets and a broader model zoo.

Methodologically, the comparison of linear and non-linear models on tabular data echoes the findings of Grinsztajn et al. (2022), who showed that tree-based models consistently outperform neural networks on medium-sized structured datasets, a result our leaderboard confirms. For interpretability, we follow the multi-method triangulation approach advocated by Molnar (2022), combining global feature importance, local SHAP explanations, and smooth term visualisations to build confidence in the identified predictive signals.

---

# 2. Setup

## 2.1 Load Required Packages

```{r packages, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# ── Core data manipulation ──────────────────────────────────────
library(tidyverse)
library(janitor)
library(scales)
library(patchwork)
library(ggrepel)

# ── Machine learning — core ─────────────────────────────────────
library(caret)
library(glmnet)
library(ranger)
library(xgboost)

# ── Machine learning — advanced ─────────────────────────────────
library(mgcv)          # Generalized Additive Models
library(nnet)          # Neural networks
library(naivebayes)    # Naive Bayes for caret
library(caretEnsemble) # OOF prediction interface for stacking
library(rpart)         # Decision trees
library(rpart.plot)    # Tree plotting
# NOTE: MASS is NOT loaded here — it masks dplyr::select(). Loaded locally
#   in model-lda-class and detached after model-qda-class.
# NOTE: maps is NOT loaded here — it masks purrr::map(). ggplot2::map_data()
#   calls maps:: directly without requiring library(maps).
library(kernlab)       # SVM
library(car)           # VIF diagnostics
library(pls)           # PCR / PLS regression

# ── Interpretability ────────────────────────────────────────────
library(pROC)
library(vip)
library(pdp)
library(broom)
library(shapviz)       # SHAP value visualisation

# ── Visualisation & reporting ───────────────────────────────────
# maps: NOT loaded globally — ggplot2::map_data() calls maps:: directly.
# Loading maps globally masks purrr::map().
library(corrplot)
library(gridExtra)
library(grid)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
# library(leaflet)     # Replaced with static ggplot maps (Section 4.6)

# ── KAN via Python (optional — graceful fallback) ────────────────
suppressWarnings(suppressMessages(library(reticulate)))

# ── Global seed for reproducibility ────────────────────────────
set.seed(42)

# Helper: styled kable
styled_kable <- function(df, caption = NULL, ...) {
  df |>
    kable(caption = caption, ...) |>
    kable_styling(
      bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed", "responsive"),
      full_width = FALSE, position = "center"
    )
}

# Helper: theme for all ggplots
theme_airbnb <- function(base_size = 18) {
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_size) +
    theme(
      plot.title         = element_text(face = "bold", colour = "#1B4F72", size = rel(1.3),
                                        margin = margin(b = 10)),
      plot.title.position = "plot",
      plot.subtitle      = element_text(colour = "#5D6D7E", size = rel(0.95),
                                        margin = margin(b = 14)),
      plot.caption       = element_text(colour = "#95A5A6", size = rel(0.75), hjust = 1,
                                        margin = margin(t = 10)),
      plot.margin        = margin(16, 18, 12, 14),
      axis.text          = element_text(colour = "#5D6D7E", size = rel(0.85)),
      axis.title         = element_text(colour = "#2C3E50", face = "bold", size = rel(1.0)),
      axis.title.x       = element_text(margin = margin(t = 12)),
      axis.title.y       = element_text(margin = margin(r = 12)),
      panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
      panel.grid.major.x = element_line(colour = "#EAECEE", linewidth = 0.4),
      panel.grid.major.y = element_line(colour = "#EAECEE", linewidth = 0.4),
      legend.position    = "bottom",
      legend.text        = element_text(size = rel(0.85), colour = "#2C3E50"),
      legend.title       = element_text(size = rel(0.9), face = "bold", colour = "#1B4F72"),
      legend.key.size    = unit(0.7, "cm"),
      legend.background  = element_rect(fill = adjustcolor("white", alpha.f = 0.95), colour = NA),
      legend.margin      = margin(t = 6, b = 6),
      legend.key         = element_rect(fill = NA, colour = NA),
      legend.spacing.x   = unit(0.4, "cm"),
      strip.text         = element_text(face = "bold", colour = "#1B4F72", size = rel(1.0),
                                        margin = margin(b = 6, t = 6)),
      strip.background   = element_rect(fill = "#F2F4F8", colour = NA),
      plot.background    = element_rect(fill = "white", colour = NA),
      panel.background   = element_rect(fill = "white", colour = NA),
      panel.spacing      = unit(1.5, "lines")
    )
}

# Blue-themed color palette (matching dashboard CSS)
pal_blue <- c("#1B4F72", "#2E86C1", "#48C9B0", "#F39C12", "#E74C3C", "#8E44AD",
              "#27AE60", "#D35400", "#2C3E50", "#16A085", "#C0392B", "#7D3C98",
              "#2980B9")
pal_class <- c("No" = "#2E86C1", "Yes" = "#1B4F72")  # superhost
pal_city  <- c("Madrid" = "#1B4F72", "Barcelona" = "#2E86C1", "Amsterdam" = "#48C9B0")
```

---

# 3. Data Collection

We load compressed CSV files for each city and combine them into a single data frame. All file paths are relative to the project folder, so the analysis is fully portable and reproducible on any machine.

> **Data update (November 2025):** Raw data refreshed from [Inside Airbnb](https://insideairbnb.com/get-the-data/) — Madrid scraped 2025-11-19, Barcelona 2025-11-17, Amsterdam 2025-11-11. This is the **most recent scrape with complete price data** for all three cities. The 2025 schema adds new columns: estimated occupancy (`estimated_occupancy_l365d`), review counts last year (`number_of_reviews_ly`), and end-of-year availability (`availability_eoy`). Price coverage is ~55–79% (inactive listings lack pricing; filtered out). Note: December 2025 (Barcelona) and January 2026 (all cities) scrapes were evaluated but rejected — Inside Airbnb removed price, host_since, instant_bookable, and host_response_rate fields from these newer scrapes. Old 2024 data backed up in `airbnb_data/old_2024_backup/`.

```{r load-data}
data_dir <- "airbnb_data"   # relative path — works on any machine

madrid    <- read_csv(file.path(data_dir, "madrid_listings.csv.gz"),    show_col_types = FALSE) |> mutate(city = "Madrid")
barcelona <- read_csv(file.path(data_dir, "barcelona_listings.csv.gz"), show_col_types = FALSE) |> mutate(city = "Barcelona")
amsterdam <- read_csv(file.path(data_dir, "amsterdam_listings.csv.gz"), show_col_types = FALSE) |> mutate(city = "Amsterdam")

df_raw <- bind_rows(madrid, barcelona, amsterdam)

# ── Data vintage assertion (Nov 2025 update) ────────────────────────────────
scrape_date <- max(as.Date(df_raw$last_scraped), na.rm = TRUE)
stopifnot("Data must be from 2025 or later" = year(scrape_date) >= 2025)
cat("Data scraped:", as.character(scrape_date), "\n")
cat("Schema columns:", ncol(df_raw), "\n")

cat("Combined dataset dimensions:\n")
cat("  Observations:", format(nrow(df_raw), big.mark = ","), "\n")
cat("  Variables:   ", ncol(df_raw), "\n\n")

df_raw |>
  count(city) |>
  mutate(pct = percent(n / sum(n), accuracy = 0.1)) |>
  styled_kable(caption = "Observations by city", col.names = c("City", "n", "%"))
```

---

# 4. Data Preparation

Raw Airbnb data is messy: prices arrive as character strings with dollar signs, booleans are encoded as "t"/"f", and many fields need extraction or imputation. The cleaning pipeline below parses column types, engineers geographic and text features, computes neighbourhood-level metrics, handles missing values, and produces the final modelling dataset.

## 4.1 Parse Raw Columns

```{r parse-columns}

df <- df_raw |>
  mutate(
    price               = str_remove_all(price, "[$,]") |> as.numeric(),
    host_response_rate  = str_remove(host_response_rate,  "%") |> as.numeric(),
    host_acceptance_rate= str_remove(host_acceptance_rate,"%") |> as.numeric(),

    host_is_superhost = case_when(
      host_is_superhost == TRUE  | host_is_superhost == "t" ~ "Yes",
      host_is_superhost == FALSE | host_is_superhost == "f" ~ "No",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),

    host_identity_verified = as.integer(host_identity_verified == TRUE | host_identity_verified == "t"),
    host_has_profile_pic   = as.integer(host_has_profile_pic   == TRUE | host_has_profile_pic   == "t"),
    instant_bookable       = as.integer(instant_bookable       == TRUE | instant_bookable       == "t"),

    bathrooms_n      = str_extract(bathrooms_text, "\\d+\\.?\\d*") |> as.numeric(),
    host_since_date  = as.Date(host_since),
    host_tenure_days = as.numeric(scrape_date - host_since_date),

    # ── New 2025 schema columns ─────────────────────────────────────────────
    # Occupancy estimate (days occupied last 365 days)
    estimated_occupancy_l365d = if ("estimated_occupancy_l365d" %in% names(df_raw))
      as.numeric(estimated_occupancy_l365d)
    else NA_real_,
    # Review count last year (review velocity signal)
    number_of_reviews_ly = if ("number_of_reviews_ly" %in% names(df_raw))
      as.numeric(number_of_reviews_ly)
    else NA_real_,
    # End-of-year availability (seasonal supply signal).
    # NOTE: availability_eoy is present in the Amsterdam scrape but absent in
    # Madrid and Barcelona; it is NA for those cities and imputed to the training
    # median before model fitting.
    availability_eoy = if ("availability_eoy" %in% names(df_raw))
      as.numeric(availability_eoy)
    else NA_real_
  )

cat("Parsing complete.\n")
cat("  New columns parsed:",
    sum(!is.na(df$estimated_occupancy_l365d)), "non-NA occupancy estimates,",
    sum(!is.na(df$number_of_reviews_ly)), "non-NA review_ly values\n")
```

## 4.2 Feature Engineering

### 4.2.1 Geographic & Amenity Features

```{r feature-engineering}
# Haversine distance (km) between two lat/lon points
haversine <- function(lat1, lon1, lat2, lon2) {
  lat1 <- lat1 * pi / 180; lon1 <- lon1 * pi / 180
  lat2 <- lat2 * pi / 180; lon2 <- lon2 * pi / 180
  dlat <- lat2 - lat1; dlon <- lon2 - lon1
  a <- sin(dlat/2)^2 + cos(lat1)*cos(lat2)*sin(dlon/2)^2
  6371 * 2 * atan2(sqrt(a), sqrt(1-a))
}

city_centers <- tribble(
  ~city,       ~center_lat, ~center_lon,
  "Madrid",     40.4168,    -3.7038,
  "Barcelona",  41.3874,     2.1686,
  "Amsterdam",  52.3676,     4.9041
)

df <- df |>
  left_join(city_centers, by = "city") |>
  mutate(
    dist_to_center   = haversine(latitude, longitude, center_lat, center_lon),
    n_amenities      = str_count(amenities, ",") + 1L,
    has_wifi         = as.integer(str_detect(tolower(amenities), "wifi|internet")),
    has_kitchen      = as.integer(str_detect(tolower(amenities), "kitchen")),
    has_ac           = as.integer(str_detect(tolower(amenities), "air conditioning|a/c")),
    has_parking      = as.integer(str_detect(tolower(amenities), "parking")),
    has_pool         = as.integer(str_detect(tolower(amenities), "pool")),
    has_elevator     = as.integer(str_detect(tolower(amenities), "elevator|lift")),
    has_washer       = as.integer(str_detect(tolower(amenities), "washer|washing machine")),
    has_dryer        = as.integer(str_detect(tolower(amenities), "dryer")),
    name_nchar       = nchar(coalesce(name, "")),
    is_entire_home   = as.integer(room_type == "Entire home/apt"),
    is_professional_host = as.integer(host_total_listings_count > 3),
    log_price        = log1p(price),
    host_is_superhost_n = if_else(host_is_superhost == "Yes", 1L, 0L)
  ) |>
  select(-center_lat, -center_lon)

top_property_types <- df |>
  count(property_type, sort = TRUE) |>
  slice_head(n = 5) |>
  pull(property_type)

df <- df |>
  mutate(
    property_type_simple = if_else(property_type %in% top_property_types,
                                   property_type, "Other") |> as.factor()
  )

cat("Base feature engineering complete.\n")
```

### 4.2.2 Neighbourhood Intelligence Features

<div class="callout-insight">
Neighbourhood context is one of the most powerful yet often overlooked signals in Airbnb modelling. I encode three market-context features per neighbourhood: the local median price (a pricing benchmark), the local Superhost density (a quality signal), and the local listing count (competition intensity). These are computed **only from the training data** in the modelling phase to prevent leakage, but computed here on the full dataset for EDA.
</div>

```{r neighbourhood-features}
# Use neighbourhood_cleansed as primary; fall back to neighbourhood
neigh_col <- if ("neighbourhood_cleansed" %in% names(df)) "neighbourhood_cleansed" else "neighbourhood"

df <- df |>
  rename(neighbourhood_use = all_of(neigh_col)) |>
  mutate(neighbourhood_use = coalesce(neighbourhood_use, "Unknown"))

# Neighbourhood-level aggregates (will be recalculated leakage-safe in modelling)
neigh_stats <- df |>
  filter(!is.na(price), price >= 10, price <= 2000) |>
  group_by(city, neighbourhood_use) |>
  summarise(
    neigh_median_price   = median(price, na.rm = TRUE),
    neigh_superhost_rate = mean(host_is_superhost == "Yes", na.rm = TRUE),
    neigh_listing_count  = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

df <- df |>
  left_join(neigh_stats, by = c("city", "neighbourhood_use")) |>
  mutate(
    neigh_median_price   = replace_na(neigh_median_price,   median(price, na.rm = TRUE)),
    neigh_superhost_rate = replace_na(neigh_superhost_rate, 0.3),
    neigh_listing_count  = replace_na(neigh_listing_count,  1L)
  )

cat("Neighbourhood features added.\n")
cat("  Unique neighbourhoods:", n_distinct(df$neighbourhood_use), "\n")
```

### 4.2.3 Text / NLP Features

```{r text-features}
# Keyword-based semantic features from listing name & description
df <- df |>
  mutate(
    listing_name_lc = tolower(coalesce(name, "")),
    listing_desc_lc = tolower(coalesce(description, "")),

    # Prestige signals
    name_luxury    = as.integer(str_detect(listing_name_lc,
                       "luxury|luxurious|premium|exclusive|villa|penthouse|palace")),
    name_cozy      = as.integer(str_detect(listing_name_lc, "cozy|cosy|charming|cute|lovely|gem")),
    name_modern    = as.integer(str_detect(listing_name_lc, "modern|new|renovated|brand new|design")),
    name_central   = as.integer(str_detect(listing_name_lc, "central|center|centre|downtown|heart")),
    name_view      = as.integer(str_detect(listing_name_lc, "view|vista|panoramic|sea view|ocean view")),
    name_spacious  = as.integer(str_detect(listing_name_lc, "spacious|large|big|huge|bright")),
    name_budget    = as.integer(str_detect(listing_name_lc, "budget|cheap|affordable|economy")),

    # Description quality signals
    desc_length    = nchar(listing_desc_lc),
    desc_quality   = case_when(
      nchar(listing_desc_lc) > 500 ~ 3L,  # detailed
      nchar(listing_desc_lc) > 150 ~ 2L,  # moderate
      nchar(listing_desc_lc) >  20 ~ 1L,  # minimal
      TRUE                          ~ 0L   # absent
    ),
    desc_has_rules     = as.integer(str_detect(listing_desc_lc, "no party|no smoking|quiet|rules")),
    desc_has_transport = as.integer(str_detect(listing_desc_lc, "metro|subway|bus|transport|train|airport"))
  ) |>
  select(-listing_name_lc, -listing_desc_lc)

cat("Text / NLP features added.\n")
```

## 4.3 Define Feature Sets

```{r define-features}
class_features <- c(
  # Host
  "host_response_rate", "host_acceptance_rate", "host_identity_verified",
  "host_has_profile_pic", "host_tenure_days", "host_total_listings_count",
  "is_professional_host",
  # Property
  "room_type", "property_type_simple", "accommodates", "bedrooms", "beds",
  "bathrooms_n", "minimum_nights", "maximum_nights",
  # Availability
  "availability_30", "availability_365",
  # Reviews (counts only — no scores to avoid leakage)
  "number_of_reviews", "number_of_reviews_ltm", "reviews_per_month",
  # Amenities
  "n_amenities", "has_wifi", "has_kitchen", "has_ac", "has_parking",
  "has_pool", "has_elevator", "has_washer", "has_dryer",
  # Location
  "dist_to_center", "name_nchar", "is_entire_home", "instant_bookable", "city",
  # Neighbourhood intelligence (NEW)
  "neigh_median_price",
  # NOTE: neigh_superhost_rate is a neighbourhood-level aggregate of the target variable.
  # This is a form of target encoding; SHAP values (Section 8.5) show it is not a dominant
  # driver — host behaviour features outrank it across all model families.
  "neigh_superhost_rate", "neigh_listing_count",
  # Text / NLP (NEW)
  "name_luxury", "name_cozy", "name_modern", "name_central", "name_view",
  "name_spacious", "name_budget", "desc_length", "desc_quality",
  "desc_has_rules", "desc_has_transport",
  # 2025 schema additions (demand & supply signals)
  "estimated_occupancy_l365d",  # demand intensity (days occupied last year)
  "number_of_reviews_ly",       # review velocity last year
  "availability_eoy",           # seasonal supply signal
  # Price (not leakage for superhost)
  "price"
)

reg_features <- c(
  # Host
  "host_is_superhost_n", "host_response_rate", "host_acceptance_rate",
  "host_identity_verified", "host_has_profile_pic", "host_tenure_days",
  "host_total_listings_count", "is_professional_host",
  # Property
  "room_type", "property_type_simple", "accommodates", "bedrooms", "beds",
  "bathrooms_n", "minimum_nights", "maximum_nights",
  # Availability
  "availability_30", "availability_365",
  # Reviews (including scores — no leakage for price)
  "number_of_reviews", "number_of_reviews_ltm", "reviews_per_month",
  "review_scores_rating", "review_scores_cleanliness",
  "review_scores_location", "review_scores_value",
  # Amenities
  "n_amenities", "has_wifi", "has_kitchen", "has_ac", "has_parking",
  "has_pool", "has_elevator", "has_washer", "has_dryer",
  # Location
  "dist_to_center", "name_nchar", "is_entire_home", "instant_bookable", "city",
  # Neighbourhood intelligence (NEW)
  "neigh_median_price", "neigh_superhost_rate", "neigh_listing_count",
  # Text / NLP (NEW)
  "name_luxury", "name_cozy", "name_modern", "name_central", "name_view",
  "name_spacious", "name_budget", "desc_length", "desc_quality",
  "desc_has_rules", "desc_has_transport",
  # 2025 schema additions (demand & supply signals)
  "estimated_occupancy_l365d",  # demand intensity (days occupied last year)
  "number_of_reviews_ly",       # review velocity last year
  "availability_eoy"            # seasonal supply signal
)

cat("Classification features:", length(class_features), "\n")
cat("Regression features:    ", length(reg_features), "\n")
```

## 4.4 Handle Missing Data

```{r missing-data}
missing_summary <- df |>
  select(all_of(c(class_features, reg_features, "host_is_superhost", "log_price"))) |>
  summarise(across(everything(), ~sum(is.na(.)))) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "variable", values_to = "missing_count") |>
  mutate(missing_pct = missing_count / nrow(df) * 100) |>
  filter(missing_count > 0) |>
  arrange(desc(missing_pct))

ggplot(missing_summary |> slice_head(n = 25),
       aes(x = reorder(variable, missing_pct), y = missing_pct)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#1B4F72", alpha = 0.88, width = 0.7) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(missing_pct, 1), "%")),
            hjust = -0.12, size = 5.5, colour = "#2C3E50", fontface = "bold") +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15)),
                     labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%")) +
  labs(title = "Missing Data Summary",
       subtitle = "Top 25 variables by missingness rate · imputation deferred to after train/test split",
       x = NULL, y = "Missing (%)",
       caption = "Source: raw Airbnb listings data") +
  theme_airbnb() +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        panel.grid.major.y = element_blank())

# Filter extremes and missing targets
df_clean <- df |>
  filter(!is.na(host_is_superhost), !is.na(price),
         price >= 10, price <= 2000)

cat("\nRows removed:", format(nrow(df) - nrow(df_clean), big.mark = ","), "\n")
cat("Final dataset:", format(nrow(df_clean), big.mark = ","), "observations\n\n")

# Identify numeric columns for later imputation (AFTER train-test split)
num_cols <- df_clean |> select(where(is.numeric)) |> names()

# NOTE: Imputation is deferred to AFTER the train-test split (Section 6)
# to prevent test-set median values from leaking into training preprocessing.
# See the train-test-split chunk for the imputation code.

# Categorical: NA → "Unknown"
df_clean <- df_clean |>
  mutate(
    room_type              = replace_na(room_type, "Unknown"),
    property_type_simple   = fct_na_value_to_level(property_type_simple, "Unknown"),
    city                   = as.factor(city),
    host_is_superhost      = factor(host_is_superhost, levels = c("No", "Yes")),
    neighbourhood_use      = replace_na(neighbourhood_use, "Unknown")
  )

cat("Imputation complete.\n")
```

## 4.5 Final Dataset Summary

```{r final-summary}
cat("Final dataset:\n")
cat("  n:", format(nrow(df_clean), big.mark = ","), " (requirement: > 1,000)\n")
cat("  p (class):", length(class_features), " (requirement: > 20)\n")
cat("  p (reg):  ", length(reg_features), " (requirement: > 20)\n\n")

df_clean |>
  select(price, log_price, host_tenure_days, accommodates,
         bedrooms, n_amenities, dist_to_center, neigh_median_price,
         estimated_occupancy_l365d, number_of_reviews_ly, availability_eoy) |>
  summarise(across(everything(),
    list(Mean = ~mean(., na.rm=TRUE), SD = ~sd(., na.rm=TRUE),
         Median = ~median(., na.rm=TRUE),
         Min  = ~min(., na.rm=TRUE),  Max = ~max(., na.rm=TRUE)))) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "stat", values_to = "value") |>
  separate(stat, into = c("variable", "metric"), sep = "_(?=[^_]+$)") |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = metric, values_from = value) |>
  styled_kable(digits = 2, caption = "Summary Statistics for Key Continuous Variables",
               format.args = list(big.mark = ","))

# Save processed data with relative path
write_csv(df_clean, "airbnb_processed.csv")
cat("\nProcessed dataset saved to: airbnb_processed.csv\n")
```

<div class="stat-grid">
<div class="stat-card">
<div class="stat-number">`r format(nrow(df_clean), big.mark=",")`</div>
<div class="stat-label">Observations</div>
</div>
<div class="stat-card">
<div class="stat-number">`r length(class_features)`</div>
<div class="stat-label">Class Features</div>
</div>
<div class="stat-card">
<div class="stat-number">`r length(reg_features)`</div>
<div class="stat-label">Reg Features</div>
</div>
<div class="stat-card">
<div class="stat-number">3</div>
<div class="stat-label">Cities</div>
</div>
<div class="stat-card">
<div class="stat-number">13</div>
<div class="stat-label">Models (Class)</div>
</div>
<div class="stat-card">
<div class="stat-number">8</div>
<div class="stat-label">Models (Reg)</div>
</div>
</div>

---

## 4.6 Listing Geography — City Maps

```{r city-maps, fig.width=17, fig.height=8}

# Static ggplot maps with country coastline boundaries -------------------------
# No external API or tiles needed — renders reliably in knitted HTML

set.seed(42)
map_data <- df_clean |>
  filter(!is.na(latitude), !is.na(longitude))

# Sample per city for performance (keeps proportions)
map_sample <- map_data |>
  split(~city) |>
  map_df(~ slice_sample(.x, n = min(2000, nrow(.x))))

# Country boundaries for background context
europe_map <- map_data("world", region = c("Spain", "Netherlands",
                                            "France", "Belgium", "Germany",
                                            "Portugal", "Italy", "Switzerland",
                                            "Luxembourg", "UK", "Ireland"))

# City centre coords for labels
city_centres <- tibble(
  city = c("Madrid", "Barcelona", "Amsterdam"),
  lon  = c(-3.7038, 2.1734, 4.9041),
  lat  = c(40.4168, 41.3851, 52.3676)
)

# Compute bounding box per city (with padding)
city_bounds <- map_sample |>
  group_by(city) |>
  summarise(
    xmin = min(longitude) - 0.02, xmax = max(longitude) + 0.02,
    ymin = min(latitude)  - 0.02, ymax = max(latitude)  + 0.02,
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# One faceted map per city with proper coord limits
city_plots <- purrr::map(city_bounds$city, function(cname) {
  bounds <- city_bounds |> filter(city == cname)
  pts    <- map_sample  |> filter(city == cname)
  centre <- city_centres |> filter(city == cname)

  ggplot() +
    # Country coastline / boundary fill
    geom_polygon(data = europe_map,
                 aes(x = long, y = lat, group = group),
                 fill = "#E8EDF5", colour = "#B0BEC5", linewidth = 0.3) +
    # Listing points
    geom_point(data = pts,
               aes(x = longitude, y = latitude, colour = price),
               alpha = 0.5, size = 0.8) +
    scale_colour_viridis_c(option = "plasma", trans = "log10",
                           labels = dollar, name = "Price",
                           breaks = c(30, 100, 300, 1000),
                           guide = guide_colourbar(barwidth = 12, barheight = 0.7,
                                                    title.position = "top",
                                                    label.theme = element_text(size = 10, colour = "#2C3E50"))) +
    # City label
    annotate("text", x = centre$lon, y = bounds$ymax - 0.005,
             label = cname, fontface = "bold", colour = "#1B4F72",
             size = 5.5, hjust = 0.5, vjust = 1) +
    coord_quickmap(xlim = c(bounds$xmin, bounds$xmax),
                   ylim = c(bounds$ymin, bounds$ymax)) +
    labs(x = NULL, y = NULL) +
    theme_airbnb() +
    theme(axis.text  = element_text(size = 8, colour = "#7F8C8D"),
          plot.margin = margin(2, 2, 2, 2))
})

# Combine with shared legend
(city_plots[[1]] + city_plots[[2]] + city_plots[[3]]) +
  plot_layout(guides = "collect") +
  plot_annotation(
    title    = "Airbnb Listing Locations by City",
    subtitle = "Point colour = nightly price (log scale) · Sampled for readability",
    theme    = theme(plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", colour = "#1B4F72", size = 20),
                     plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "#5D6D7E", size = 14))
  ) &
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

---

# 5. Exploratory Data Analysis

Before fitting any models, I wanted to get a sense of what the data actually looks like. Here I examine the distributions of both target variables (Superhost status and nightly price), spatial and neighbourhood-level patterns, and the main bivariate relationships that will guide feature selection.

## 5.1 Target Distributions

```{r superhost-distribution}
superhost_balance <- df_clean |>
  count(host_is_superhost) |>
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n) * 100,
         label = paste0(format(n, big.mark=","), "\n(", round(pct,1), "%)"))

ggplot(superhost_balance, aes(x = host_is_superhost, y = n, fill = host_is_superhost)) +
  geom_col(alpha = 0.9, width = 0.55, colour = "white", linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = label), vjust = -0.35, size = 6.5, fontface = "bold",
            colour = "#2C3E50", lineheight = 0.9) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = pal_class) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.2))) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Superhost Status",
       subtitle = "Class imbalance — handled by majority-class downsampling in modelling",
       x = "Superhost Status", y = "Count",
       caption = "Imbalance ratio shown in parentheses") +
  theme_airbnb() +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        panel.grid.major.x = element_blank())
```

```{r price-distribution}
p1 <- ggplot(df_clean, aes(x = price)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), bins = 65, fill = "#1B4F72",
                 alpha = 0.7, colour = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
  geom_density(colour = "#E74C3C", linewidth = 1.4, adjust = 1.5) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = dollar) +
  labs(title = "Raw Price", subtitle = "Right-skewed · red curve = kernel density",
       x = "Price (USD)", y = "Density") +
  theme_airbnb() +
  theme(panel.grid.major.x = element_blank())

p2 <- ggplot(df_clean, aes(x = log_price)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), bins = 65, fill = "#2E86C1",
                 alpha = 0.7, colour = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
  geom_density(colour = "#E74C3C", linewidth = 1.4, adjust = 1.5) +
  labs(title = "Log-Transformed Price", subtitle = "Approximately normal · red curve = kernel density",
       x = "log(Price + 1)", y = "Density") +
  theme_airbnb() +
  theme(panel.grid.major.x = element_blank())

p1 + p2 + plot_annotation(
  title    = "Distribution of Nightly Price",
  subtitle = "Log transformation corrects the right skew for regression modelling",
  theme    = theme(plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", colour = "#1B4F72", size = 22),
                   plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "#5D6D7E", size = 16))
)
```

```{r price-by-city}
ggplot(df_clean, aes(x = city, y = price, fill = city)) +
  geom_violin(alpha = 0.55, colour = NA, trim = FALSE, scale = "width") +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.15, outlier.alpha = 0.12, outlier.size = 0.8,
               colour = "#2C3E50", fill = "white", alpha = 0.7) +
  stat_summary(fun = median, geom = "text",
               aes(label = dollar(after_stat(y), accuracy = 1)),
               vjust = -1.2, size = 5.5, fontface = "bold", colour = "#2C3E50") +
  scale_y_log10(labels = dollar,
                breaks = c(10, 25, 50, 100, 250, 500, 1000, 2000)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = pal_city) +
  labs(title = "Nightly Price Distribution by City",
       subtitle = "Log scale · violin shows density shape · boxplot shows quartiles · labels = median",
       x = NULL, y = "Price (USD, log scale)") +
  theme_airbnb() +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        panel.grid.major.x = element_blank())
```

## 5.2 Neighbourhood Analysis

```{r neighbourhood-analysis, fig.height=12, fig.width=16}
top_neighs <- neigh_stats |>
  group_by(city) |>
  slice_max(neigh_median_price, n = 10) |>
  ungroup()

ggplot(top_neighs, aes(x = reorder(neighbourhood_use, neigh_median_price),
                       y = neigh_median_price, fill = city)) +
  geom_col(alpha = 0.88, width = 0.75, colour = "white", linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = dollar(neigh_median_price, accuracy = 1)),
            hjust = -0.08, size = 4.5, colour = "#2C3E50", fontface = "bold") +
  coord_flip() +
  facet_wrap(~city, scales = "free_y", ncol = 1) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = pal_city) +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = function(x) str_trunc(x, 25)) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.18)), labels = dollar) +
  labs(title = "Top 10 Neighbourhoods by Median Nightly Price",
       subtitle = "By city — neighbourhood intelligence used as model features",
       x = NULL, y = "Median Price (USD)") +
  theme_airbnb() +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
        axis.text.y = element_text(size = 11))
```

```{r neigh-superhost-rate, fig.height=12, fig.width=16}
neigh_stats |>
  group_by(city) |>
  filter(neigh_listing_count >= 10) |>
  slice_max(neigh_superhost_rate, n = 8) |>
  ungroup() |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(neighbourhood_use, neigh_superhost_rate),
             y = neigh_superhost_rate, fill = city)) +
  geom_col(alpha = 0.88, width = 0.75, colour = "white", linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = percent(neigh_superhost_rate, accuracy = 1)),
            hjust = -0.08, size = 4.5, colour = "#2C3E50", fontface = "bold") +
  coord_flip() +
  facet_wrap(~city, scales = "free_y", ncol = 1) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = pal_city) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.2))) +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = function(x) str_trunc(x, 25)) +
  labs(title = "Top Neighbourhoods by Superhost Density",
       subtitle = "Minimum 10 listings per neighbourhood",
       x = NULL, y = "Superhost Rate") +
  theme_airbnb() +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
        axis.text.y = element_text(size = 11))
```

## 5.3 Key Bivariate Relationships

```{r superhost-by-city}
df_clean |>
  group_by(city, host_is_superhost) |>
  summarise(n = n(), .groups = "drop") |>
  group_by(city) |>
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n) * 100) |>
  filter(host_is_superhost == "Yes") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(city, pct), y = pct, fill = city)) +
  geom_col(alpha = 0.9, width = 0.5, colour = "white", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(pct,1),"%")), vjust = -0.35, size = 5.5,
            fontface = "bold", colour = "#2C3E50") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = pal_city) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.18)),
                     labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%")) +
  labs(title = "Superhost Rate by City",
       subtitle = "Percentage of hosts with Superhost badge by market",
       x = NULL, y = "Superhost Rate (%)") +
  theme_airbnb() +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        panel.grid.major.x = element_blank())
```

```{r text-features-eda}
text_summary <- df_clean |>
  summarise(
    pct_luxury  = mean(name_luxury)  * 100,
    pct_cozy    = mean(name_cozy)    * 100,
    pct_modern  = mean(name_modern)  * 100,
    pct_central = mean(name_central) * 100,
    pct_view    = mean(name_view)    * 100,
    pct_budget  = mean(name_budget)  * 100
  ) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "keyword", values_to = "pct") |>
  mutate(keyword = str_remove(keyword, "pct_") |> str_to_title())

p_text1 <- ggplot(text_summary, aes(x = reorder(keyword, pct), y = pct, fill = keyword)) +
  geom_col(alpha = 0.88, show.legend = FALSE, width = 0.7, colour = "white", linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(pct,1),"%")), hjust = -0.12, size = 5,
            colour = "#2C3E50", fontface = "bold") +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.2)),
                     labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%")) +
  labs(title = "NLP Keyword Prevalence", subtitle = "% of listings with keyword in name",
       x = NULL, y = "% of listings") +
  theme_airbnb() +
  theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_blank())

# Superhost rate by luxury keyword
p_text2 <- df_clean |>
  pivot_longer(c(name_luxury, name_cozy, name_modern, name_central, name_view, name_budget),
               names_to = "keyword", values_to = "present") |>
  filter(present == 1) |>
  mutate(keyword = str_remove(keyword, "name_") |> str_to_title()) |>
  group_by(keyword) |>
  summarise(superhost_rate = mean(host_is_superhost == "Yes") * 100, n = n(),
            .groups = "drop") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(keyword, superhost_rate), y = superhost_rate, fill = keyword)) +
  geom_col(alpha = 0.88, show.legend = FALSE, width = 0.7, colour = "white", linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(superhost_rate,1),"%")), hjust = -0.12, size = 5,
            colour = "#2C3E50", fontface = "bold") +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.22)),
                     labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%")) +
  labs(title = "Superhost Rate by Name Keyword",
       subtitle = "Among listings with that keyword",
       x = NULL, y = "Superhost Rate (%)") +
  theme_airbnb() +
  theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_blank())

p_text1 + p_text2
```

## 5.4 Correlation Heatmap

```{r correlation-heatmap, fig.width=18, fig.height=14}
cor_vars <- c("price","accommodates","bedrooms","beds","bathrooms_n",
              "minimum_nights","number_of_reviews","reviews_per_month",
              "review_scores_rating","host_response_rate","host_acceptance_rate",
              "host_tenure_days","n_amenities","dist_to_center",
              "neigh_median_price","neigh_superhost_rate","desc_length")

cor_vars <- cor_vars[cor_vars %in% names(df_clean)]
cor_mat   <- df_clean |> select(all_of(cor_vars)) |> select(where(is.numeric)) |>
  cor(use = "pairwise.complete.obs")
cor_mat[is.na(cor_mat)] <- 0

corrplot(cor_mat, method = "color", type = "upper", order = "hclust",
         tl.col = "#2C3E50", tl.cex = 1.1, tl.srt = 45,
         addCoef.col = "#2C3E50", number.cex = 0.8, number.font = 2,
         cl.cex = 1.0, cl.align.text = "l",
         col = colorRampPalette(c("#2E86C1","#EBF5FB","white","#FDEDEC","#E74C3C"))(200),
         title = "Pearson Correlation Matrix — Numeric Features",
         mar = c(0,0,2.5,0))
```

## 5.5 Geospatial Patterns

```{r geospatial-price, fig.width=18, fig.height=10}
set.seed(42)
df_sample <- df_clean |> group_by(city) |> slice_sample(n = 2000) |> ungroup()

ggplot(df_sample, aes(x = longitude, y = latitude, colour = price)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.50, size = 1.6, shape = 16) +
  facet_wrap(~city, scales = "free") +
  scale_colour_viridis_c(option = "plasma", trans = "log10", labels = dollar,
                         breaks = c(30, 100, 300, 1000),
                         guide = guide_colourbar(barwidth = 16, barheight = 0.9,
                                                 title.position = "top",
                                                 label.theme = element_text(size = 12, colour = "#2C3E50"))) +
  labs(title = "Listing Locations Coloured by Nightly Price",
       subtitle = "Price hotspots concentrate near city centres and tourist districts · log colour scale",
       x = "Longitude", y = "Latitude", colour = "Nightly Price") +
  theme_airbnb() +
  theme(axis.text = element_text(size = 12, colour = "#95A5A6"))
```

```{r geospatial-superhost, fig.width=18, fig.height=11}
ggplot(df_sample, aes(x = longitude, y = latitude, colour = host_is_superhost)) +
  geom_point(data = ~filter(., host_is_superhost == "No"),
             alpha = 0.40, size = 1.2, shape = 16) +
  geom_point(data = ~filter(., host_is_superhost == "Yes"),
             alpha = 0.75, size = 2.0, shape = 16) +
  facet_wrap(~city, scales = "free") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("No" = "#95A5A6", "Yes" = "#1B4F72"),
                      labels = c("No" = "Non-Superhost", "Yes" = "Superhost")) +
  labs(title = "Superhost Geographic Distribution",
       subtitle = "Superhosts distributed citywide — behaviour matters more than location",
       x = "Longitude", y = "Latitude", colour = NULL) +
  theme_airbnb() +
  theme(axis.text = element_text(size = 12, colour = "#95A5A6"),
        legend.position = "bottom",
        legend.text = element_text(size = 14))
```

---

# 6. Train/Test Split & Class Balancing

The data is split 80/20 into training and test sets. All preprocessing after this point (imputation, neighbourhood feature recomputation, class balancing) is fitted on the training data only and then applied to the test set. This prevents data leakage so the test set stays genuinely unseen.

```{r train-test-split}
set.seed(42)

# ── Split strategy ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# When KAN CSVs are pre-generated (run_kan_export = FALSE), we must use the
# SAME train/test partition that produced those CSVs.  The Python pipeline saved
# 0-based row indices in _train_indices.csv / _test_indices.csv.  If the row
# count in df_clean matches the sum of those indices we adopt them directly;
# otherwise we fall back to caret::createDataPartition and KAN predictions will
# be regenerated on the next pipeline run.
kan_idx_path <- "_test_indices.csv"

if (!isTRUE(run_kan_export) && file.exists(kan_idx_path) &&
    file.exists("_train_indices.csv")) {

  test_idx_0  <- readr::read_csv(kan_idx_path,          show_col_types = FALSE)$idx
  train_idx_0 <- readr::read_csv("_train_indices.csv",  show_col_types = FALSE)$idx
  expected_n  <- length(test_idx_0) + length(train_idx_0)

  if (expected_n == nrow(df_clean)) {
    # Row counts match → safe to reuse Python split (0-based → 1-based)
    # IMPORTANT: use BOTH index vectors explicitly to preserve Python's row
    # ordering.  df_clean[-train_idx, ] would return test rows in sequential
    # order, but kan_pred_test.csv is in Python's (shuffled) order.
    train_idx <- train_idx_0 + 1L
    test_idx  <- test_idx_0  + 1L
    cat("Using pre-generated split indices for KAN compatibility",
        "(n =", nrow(df_clean), ", test =", length(test_idx), ")\n")
  } else {
    warning("df_clean has ", nrow(df_clean), " rows but pre-generated indices ",
            "sum to ", expected_n, ".  Falling back to createDataPartition.")
    train_idx <- createDataPartition(df_clean$host_is_superhost,
                                     p = 0.8, list = FALSE)
    test_idx  <- setdiff(seq_len(nrow(df_clean)), as.integer(train_idx))
  }
} else {
  train_idx <- createDataPartition(df_clean$host_is_superhost,
                                   p = 0.8, list = FALSE)
  test_idx  <- setdiff(seq_len(nrow(df_clean)), as.integer(train_idx))
}

train_df  <- df_clean[train_idx, ]
test_df   <- df_clean[test_idx,  ]

# Persist split indices (0-based) so Python KAN scripts use the identical partition
readr::write_csv(tibble::tibble(idx = as.integer(train_idx) - 1L), "_train_indices.csv")
readr::write_csv(tibble::tibble(idx = as.integer(test_idx)  - 1L), "_test_indices.csv")

# Impute numeric predictors: fit medians on TRAIN only, apply to both sets
# This prevents test-set information from leaking into training preprocessing.
impute_medians <- sapply(train_df[, num_cols, drop = FALSE],
                         function(x) median(x, na.rm = TRUE))

train_df <- train_df |>
  mutate(across(all_of(num_cols),
                ~replace(., is.na(.), impute_medians[cur_column()])))
test_df <- test_df |>
  mutate(across(all_of(num_cols),
                ~replace(., is.na(.), impute_medians[cur_column()])))

cat("Imputation medians computed on training set and applied to both splits.\n")
cat("Training set:", format(nrow(train_df), big.mark=","), "observations\n")
cat("Test set:    ", format(nrow(test_df),  big.mark=","), "observations\n\n")

cat("Class balance — Train:\n")
prop.table(table(train_df$host_is_superhost)) |> round(3) |> print()
cat("\nClass balance — Test:\n")
prop.table(table(test_df$host_is_superhost)) |> round(3) |> print()
```


```{r recompute-neighbourhood-features}
# Recompute neighbourhood features on TRAIN only (leakage-safe)
train_neigh_stats <- train_df |>
  filter(!is.na(price), price >= 10, price <= 2000) |>
  group_by(city, neighbourhood_use) |>
  summarise(
    neigh_median_price   = median(price, na.rm = TRUE),
    neigh_superhost_rate = mean(host_is_superhost == "Yes", na.rm = TRUE),
    neigh_listing_count  = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Global fallbacks from TRAIN only
global_neigh_price <- median(train_df$price, na.rm = TRUE)
global_sh_rate     <- mean(train_df$host_is_superhost == "Yes", na.rm = TRUE)

train_df <- train_df |>
  select(-neigh_median_price, -neigh_superhost_rate, -neigh_listing_count) |>
  left_join(train_neigh_stats, by = c("city", "neighbourhood_use")) |>
  mutate(
    neigh_median_price   = replace_na(neigh_median_price, global_neigh_price),
    neigh_superhost_rate = replace_na(neigh_superhost_rate, global_sh_rate),
    neigh_listing_count  = replace_na(neigh_listing_count, 1L)
  )

test_df <- test_df |>
  select(-neigh_median_price, -neigh_superhost_rate, -neigh_listing_count) |>
  left_join(train_neigh_stats, by = c("city", "neighbourhood_use")) |>
  mutate(
    neigh_median_price   = replace_na(neigh_median_price, global_neigh_price),
    neigh_superhost_rate = replace_na(neigh_superhost_rate, global_sh_rate),
    neigh_listing_count  = replace_na(neigh_listing_count, 1L)
  )
```

> **A note on `neigh_superhost_rate`**: This feature computes the proportion of Superhosts in each neighbourhood, which constitutes a form of target encoding (the feature is derived from the target variable). To mitigate leakage, we compute this statistic on the training set only and map it to the test set by neighbourhood. However, the train-set statistic still encodes target information at the group level, which can inflate classification performance. As a robustness check, we verified that removing `neigh_superhost_rate` from the classification feature set reduces Random Forest AUC by approximately 0.01--0.02, confirming that it contributes modestly but is not responsible for the bulk of predictive performance.

```{r class-balancing}
# Prepare classification data
train_class <- train_df |>
  select(all_of(class_features), host_is_superhost) |>
  rename(is_superhost = host_is_superhost) |>
  na.omit()

test_class <- test_df |>
  select(all_of(class_features), host_is_superhost) |>
  rename(is_superhost = host_is_superhost) |>
  na.omit()

cat("Classification data after na.omit():\n")
cat("  Train:", format(nrow(train_class), big.mark=","), "rows (dropped",
    format(nrow(train_df) - nrow(train_class), big.mark=","), ")\n")
cat("  Test: ", format(nrow(test_class), big.mark=","), "rows (dropped",
    format(nrow(test_df) - nrow(test_class), big.mark=","), ")\n")

# Downsample majority class to balance — much faster than upSample on large datasets
# Applied only to training data to prevent leakage into test set
set.seed(42)
train_class_balanced <- downSample(
  x      = train_class |> select(-is_superhost),
  y      = train_class$is_superhost,
  yname  = "is_superhost"
)

cat("Original class distribution:\n")
table(train_class$is_superhost) |> print()
cat("\nAfter downsampling (balanced):\n")
table(train_class_balanced$is_superhost) |> print()
cat("Balanced training set size:", nrow(train_class_balanced), "\n")
```

---

# 7. Classification — Predicting Superhost Status

The goal here is to fit thirteen classification models to predict Superhost status, following the model comparison workflow we practised in class. The models range from logistic regression and LDA through KNN, SVM, and decision trees to Random Forest, XGBoost, and KAN. Each is evaluated with 5-fold cross-validation on the balanced training set, then tested on the held-out data with its natural class distribution. After comparing the models, I examine calibration, SHAP explanations, threshold optimisation, and model stacking.

## 7.1 Cross-Validation Control

```{r classification-control}
ctrl_class <- trainControl(
  method          = "cv",
  number          = 5,
  classProbs      = TRUE,
  summaryFunction = twoClassSummary,
  savePredictions = "final",
  verboseIter     = FALSE
)

cat("5-fold CV configured for classification (metric: ROC-AUC)\n")
cat("Training on downsampled (balanced) data:", nrow(train_class_balanced), "obs\n")
cat("Evaluating on original test set:", nrow(test_class), "obs\n")
```

## 7.2 Baseline: Majority Class

```{r baseline-classification}
# Baseline computed on TEST SET — training class distribution is artificially balanced
# after downsampling, making train-based baseline uninformative. The test set preserves
# the natural class distribution and gives a meaningful benchmark.
majority_class <- names(which.max(table(test_class$is_superhost)))
baseline_acc   <- max(table(test_class$is_superhost)) / nrow(test_class)
cat("Baseline accuracy (majority class on TEST set =", majority_class, "):",
    round(baseline_acc, 4), "\n")
cat("(Note: downsampled training data is 50/50; using test-set natural distribution as baseline)\n")
```

## 7.3 Model C1: Logistic Regression

```{r model-glm}
set.seed(42)
m_glm <- train(is_superhost ~ ., data = train_class_balanced,
               method = "glm", family = "binomial",
               trControl = ctrl_class, metric = "ROC")
cat("GLM — CV ROC:", round(max(m_glm$results$ROC), 4), "\n")
```

## 7.4 Model C2: Elastic Net

```{r model-enet-class}
set.seed(42)
m_enet_c <- train(is_superhost ~ ., data = train_class_balanced,
                  method = "glmnet", trControl = ctrl_class,
                  metric = "ROC", tuneLength = 12)
cat("Elastic Net — best alpha:", round(m_enet_c$bestTune$alpha, 3),
    "| lambda:", round(m_enet_c$bestTune$lambda, 4),
    "| CV ROC:", round(max(m_enet_c$results$ROC), 4), "\n")
```

### Elastic Net Regularization Path (Classification)

The regularisation path shows how each feature's coefficient changes as the penalty increases. Features that keep non-zero coefficients even under heavy penalisation are the most reliably predictive ones. Features that collapse to zero early are either redundant or carry weak signal.

```{r enet-path-class, fig.width=18, fig.height=11}
# Extract coefficient matrix across lambda grid (best alpha from CV)
best_alpha_c <- m_enet_c$bestTune$alpha
enet_fit_c   <- glmnet::glmnet(
  x      = model.matrix(is_superhost ~ ., data = train_class_balanced)[, -1],
  y      = train_class_balanced$is_superhost,
  family = "binomial",
  alpha  = best_alpha_c
)

# Top 15 features by final absolute coefficient
final_coefs_c <- coef(enet_fit_c, s = m_enet_c$bestTune$lambda)
top15_c <- names(sort(abs(final_coefs_c[-1, 1]), decreasing = TRUE))[1:15]

path_mat_c <- as.matrix(t(coef(enet_fit_c)[c(top15_c), ]))
path_df_c  <- as.data.frame(path_mat_c) |>
  mutate(log_lambda = log(enet_fit_c$lambda)) |>
  pivot_longer(-log_lambda, names_to = "Variable", values_to = "Coefficient") |>
  mutate(Variable = Variable |> str_replace_all("[._]", " ") |> str_squish())

ggplot(path_df_c, aes(x = log_lambda, y = Coefficient,
                       colour = Variable, group = Variable)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, colour = "#EAECEE", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2, alpha = 0.9) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = log(m_enet_c$bestTune$lambda),
             linetype = "dashed", colour = "#E74C3C", linewidth = 1.2) +
  annotate("label",
           x    = log(m_enet_c$bestTune$lambda),
           y    = max(abs(path_df_c$Coefficient)) * 0.95,
           label = paste0("Best λ = ", round(m_enet_c$bestTune$lambda, 4)),
           colour = "#E74C3C", size = 5, fontface = "bold", hjust = -0.05, fill = "white",
           label.size = 0.3, label.padding = unit(0.2, "lines")) +
  labs(title = "Elastic Net Regularization Path — Classification (Top 15 Features)",
       subtitle = paste0("α = ", round(best_alpha_c, 3),
                         " · Features shrink to zero as regularisation penalty increases (→ left)"),
       x = "log(λ)", y = "Coefficient", colour = NULL) +
  theme_airbnb() +
  theme(legend.position = "right",
        legend.text = element_text(size = 11),
        legend.key.height = unit(1.0, "lines"),
        legend.key.width  = unit(1.2, "lines"))
```

Elastic Net blends Ridge (α = 0, which shrinks coefficients but drops none) and Lasso (α = 1, which can zero out features entirely). The CV-selected α = `r round(m_enet_c$bestTune$alpha, 3)` means the model prefers `r if (m_enet_c$bestTune$alpha > 0.7) "a Lasso-dominant strategy, aggressively eliminating weak features and retaining only the strongest predictors" else if (m_enet_c$bestTune$alpha < 0.3) "a Ridge-dominant strategy, keeping most features with moderate shrinkage, which suggests many predictors carry complementary information" else "a balanced strategy, performing moderate variable selection while keeping groups of correlated features together"`. At λ = `r round(m_enet_c$bestTune$lambda, 4)`, `r sum(coef(m_enet_c$finalModel, m_enet_c$bestTune$lambda) != 0) - 1` features survive with non-zero coefficients. The rest are zeroed out, meaning they did not add enough predictive value to justify keeping.

## 7.5 Model C3: Random Forest

```{r model-rf-class}


set.seed(42)
m_rf_c <- train(
  is_superhost ~ ., data = train_class_balanced,
  method = "ranger",
  trControl = ctrl_class,
  metric = "ROC",
  tuneLength = 3,
  importance = "impurity",
  num.trees = 200
)

cat("Random Forest — best mtry:", m_rf_c$bestTune$mtry,
    "| CV ROC:", round(max(m_rf_c$results$ROC), 4), "\n")
```

## 7.6 Model C4: XGBoost

```{r model-xgb-class}
# # Dummy-encode for XGBoost (requires all-numeric input)


# Dummy-encode for XGBoost (xgbTree requires numeric matrix input)
dummies_class <- caret::dummyVars(
  is_superhost ~ .,
  data = train_class_balanced,
  fullRank = TRUE,
  na.action = na.pass
)

x_train_xgb_c <- predict(dummies_class, newdata = train_class_balanced)

# Keep only finite columns; reuse this same mask on the test set later
good_cols_c <- apply(x_train_xgb_c, 2, function(col) all(is.finite(col)))
x_train_xgb_c <- x_train_xgb_c[, good_cols_c, drop = FALSE]

y_train_xgb_c <- train_class_balanced$is_superhost

# Constrained tuning grid (fast / development-friendly)
xgb_grid_c <- expand.grid(
  nrounds          = c(100, 200, 300),
  max_depth        = c(3, 5, 7),
  eta              = c(0.01, 0.1),
  gamma            = c(0),
  colsample_bytree = c(0.8),
  min_child_weight = c(1),
  subsample        = c(0.8)
)

set.seed(42)
m_xgb_c <- train(
  x = x_train_xgb_c,
  y = y_train_xgb_c,
  method = "xgbTree",
  trControl = ctrl_class,
  metric = "ROC",
  tuneGrid = xgb_grid_c
)

cat("XGBoost — CV ROC:", round(max(m_xgb_c$results$ROC), 4), "\n")
cat("Best params:\n")
print(m_xgb_c$bestTune)


```

## 7.7 Model C5: Generalized Additive Model (GAM)

I included the GAM because it lets each numeric predictor follow a flexible non-linear curve (via penalised splines) while keeping the model additive and interpretable. Unlike tree ensembles, you can actually visualise how each feature relates to the outcome.

```{r model-gam-class}

# GAM (classification) -------------------------------------------------------
# Fast/stable implementation using mgcv::bam() instead of caret::train(method = "gam")

# 1) Build numeric-only training data
gam_num_cols_all_c <- names(train_class_balanced)[sapply(train_class_balanced, is.numeric)]
gam_train_c <- train_class_balanced[, c(gam_num_cols_all_c, "is_superhost"), drop = FALSE]

cat("GAM training rows:", nrow(gam_train_c), "\n")
cat("GAM numeric predictors (raw):", length(gam_num_cols_all_c), "\n")

# 2) Binary outcome for mgcv
y_raw <- gam_train_c$is_superhost
if (is.factor(y_raw) || is.character(y_raw)) {
  # preserve original labels for downstream evaluation
  y_chr_orig  <- as.character(y_raw)
  y_chr_lower <- tolower(y_chr_orig)

  if ("yes" %in% y_chr_lower) {
    pos_idx <- which(y_chr_lower == "yes")[1]
    gam_positive_class_c <- y_chr_orig[pos_idx]   # preserves "Yes" if original had caps
    gam_train_c$is_superhost_bin <- as.integer(y_chr_orig == gam_positive_class_c)
  } else {
    levs <- unique(y_chr_orig)
    if (length(levs) != 2) stop("is_superhost is not binary; cannot fit binomial GAM.")
    gam_positive_class_c <- levs[2]
    gam_train_c$is_superhost_bin <- as.integer(y_chr_orig == gam_positive_class_c)
    warning("Using second class level as positive: ", gam_positive_class_c)
  }
} else if (is.logical(y_raw)) {
  gam_train_c$is_superhost_bin <- as.integer(y_raw)
  gam_positive_class_c <- "TRUE"
} else if (is.numeric(y_raw) || is.integer(y_raw)) {
  vals <- sort(unique(stats::na.omit(y_raw)))
  if (!all(vals %in% c(0, 1))) stop("Numeric is_superhost must be coded 0/1.")
  gam_train_c$is_superhost_bin <- as.integer(y_raw)
  gam_positive_class_c <- "1"
} else {
  stop("Unsupported is_superhost type for GAM classification.")
}

# 3) Use a targeted numeric subset (much more stable than smoothing everything)
gam_candidate_vars_c <- c(
  "price",
  "availability_365",
  "number_of_reviews",
  "reviews_per_month",
  "host_tenure_days",
  "n_amenities",
  "bedrooms",
  "beds",
  "bathrooms_n",
  "accommodates",
  "minimum_nights",
  # 2025 additions
  "estimated_occupancy_l365d",
  "number_of_reviews_ly",
  "availability_eoy"
)

gam_num_cols_c <- intersect(gam_candidate_vars_c, names(gam_train_c))
cat("Targeted GAM predictors:\n")
print(gam_num_cols_c)

if (length(gam_num_cols_c) < 3) {
  stop("Too few targeted GAM predictors found. Check variable names.")
}

# 4) Drop NZV / invalid columns
nzv_idx <- caret::nearZeroVar(gam_train_c[, gam_num_cols_c, drop = FALSE])
if (length(nzv_idx) > 0) {
  removed_nzv <- gam_num_cols_c[nzv_idx]
  gam_num_cols_c <- gam_num_cols_c[-nzv_idx]
  cat("Removed near-zero variance predictors:", paste(removed_nzv, collapse = ", "), "\n")
}

valid_pred <- vapply(gam_num_cols_c, function(v) {
  x <- gam_train_c[[v]]
  sum(!is.na(x)) > 1 && stats::var(x, na.rm = TRUE) > 0 && all(is.finite(x[!is.na(x)]))
}, logical(1))

if (!all(valid_pred)) {
  removed_bad <- gam_num_cols_c[!valid_pred]
  gam_num_cols_c <- gam_num_cols_c[valid_pred]
  cat("Removed invalid predictors:", paste(removed_bad, collapse = ", "), "\n")
}

# Keep final columns
gam_train_c <- gam_train_c[, c(gam_num_cols_c, "is_superhost", "is_superhost_bin"), drop = FALSE]

# Extra diagnostics
cat("Any NA in GAM training data? ", anyNA(gam_train_c[, c(gam_num_cols_c, "is_superhost_bin")]), "\n")

# 5) Formula
smooth_terms <- paste0("s(", gam_num_cols_c, ", k = 5)")
gam_formula_c <- stats::as.formula(
  paste("is_superhost_bin ~", paste(smooth_terms, collapse = " + "))
)

cat("Fitting mgcv::bam with", length(gam_num_cols_c), "smooth terms...\n")

# 6) Fit GAM with visible error if it fails
set.seed(42)
t0 <- Sys.time()

m_gam_c <- tryCatch({
  cat("Starting mgcv::bam()...\n")
  model <- mgcv::bam(
    formula  = gam_formula_c,
    data     = gam_train_c,
    family   = stats::binomial(link = "logit"),
    method   = "fREML",
    discrete = TRUE
  )
  cat("mgcv::bam completed in",
      round(as.numeric(difftime(Sys.time(), t0, units = "mins")), 2),
      "minutes\n")
  model
}, error = function(e) {
  cat("\n[ERROR] mgcv::bam failed:\n", conditionMessage(e), "\n\n", sep = "")
  cat("Falling back to GLM.\n")
  model <- stats::glm(
    is_superhost_bin ~ .,
    data   = gam_train_c[, c(gam_num_cols_c, "is_superhost_bin"), drop = FALSE],
    family = stats::binomial()
  )
  cat("GLM fallback completed in",
      round(as.numeric(difftime(Sys.time(), t0, units = "mins")), 2),
      "minutes\n")
  model
})

# 7) Diagnostics
cat("m_gam_c class:\n")
print(class(m_gam_c))

if (inherits(m_gam_c, "gam")) {
  cat("mgcv effective degrees of freedom (sum):", round(sum(m_gam_c$edf), 2), "\n")
}

# 8) Save metadata for downstream chunks
gam_num_cols_used_c <- gam_num_cols_c
gam_positive_class_used_c <- gam_positive_class_c
cat("GAM positive class label:", gam_positive_class_used_c, "\n")

```

### GAM Smooth Terms — Classification

The point of the GAM is that it does not force a straight-line relationship between each predictor and the outcome. Instead it learns smooth curves via penalised splines. The plots below show these learned shapes for the most non-linear predictors. Where the effective degrees of freedom (edf) exceed 1, the relationship is genuinely curved.

```{r gam-smooth-class, fig.width=20, fig.height=14}



# GAM smooth-term plot (classification) -----------------------------------------
# Works for mgcv::bam / mgcv::gam objects; skips gracefully for GLM fallback.

if (inherits(m_gam_c, "gam")) {
  
  gam_sum <- summary(m_gam_c)
  
  if (is.null(gam_sum$s.table)) {
    cat("Smooth-term plot skipped: GAM summary has no smooth table.\n")
    
  } else {
    
    s_tab <- as.data.frame(gam_sum$s.table)
    s_tab$term <- rownames(gam_sum$s.table)
    
    edf_matches <- grep("^edf", names(s_tab), value = TRUE)
    if (length(edf_matches) == 0) stop("No edf column found in GAM smooth table.")
    s_tab$edf_value <- s_tab[[edf_matches[1]]]
    
    top_terms <- s_tab |>
      dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(edf_value)) |>
      dplyr::slice_head(n = min(6, nrow(s_tab))) |>
      dplyr::pull(term)
    
    base_row <- gam_train_c |>
      dplyr::select(dplyr::all_of(gam_num_cols_used_c)) |>
      dplyr::summarise(dplyr::across(dplyr::everything(), ~mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE)))
    
    smooth_df <- purrr::map_df(top_terms, function(term_label) {
      
      # Parse mgcv smooth term labels like "s(price)" without regex
      var_name <- term_label
      if (startsWith(var_name, "s(") && substring(var_name, nchar(var_name), nchar(var_name)) == ")") {
        var_name <- substring(var_name, 3, nchar(var_name) - 1)
      }
      
      if (!var_name %in% names(base_row)) return(NULL)
      
      x_vec <- gam_train_c[[var_name]]
      if (all(is.na(x_vec))) return(NULL)
      
      x_min <- min(x_vec, na.rm = TRUE)
      x_max <- max(x_vec, na.rm = TRUE)
      if (!is.finite(x_min) || !is.finite(x_max) || x_min == x_max) return(NULL)
      
      grid_vals <- seq(x_min, x_max, length.out = 200)
      
      nd <- base_row[rep(1, length(grid_vals)), , drop = FALSE]
      nd[[var_name]] <- grid_vals
      
      pred <- predict(m_gam_c, newdata = nd, type = "link", se.fit = TRUE)
      
      edf_here <- s_tab |>
        dplyr::filter(term == term_label) |>
        dplyr::pull(edf_value)
      
      edf_here <- if (length(edf_here) == 0 || is.na(edf_here[1])) NA_real_ else edf_here[1]
      edf_lab  <- if (is.na(edf_here)) "edf = NA" else paste0("edf = ", round(edf_here, 2))
      
      tibble::tibble(
        x    = grid_vals,
        fit  = as.numeric(pred$fit),
        se   = as.numeric(pred$se.fit),
        term = paste0(var_name, " (", edf_lab, ")")
      )
    })
    
    if (nrow(smooth_df) > 0) {
      ggplot2::ggplot(smooth_df, ggplot2::aes(x = x, y = fit)) +
        ggplot2::geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "#EAECEE",
                            linewidth = 0.6) +
        ggplot2::geom_ribbon(
          ggplot2::aes(ymin = fit - 2 * se, ymax = fit + 2 * se),
          fill = "#1B4F72", alpha = 0.15
        ) +
        ggplot2::geom_line(colour = "#1B4F72", linewidth = 1.1) +
        ggplot2::facet_wrap(~term, scales = "free_x", ncol = 3) +
        ggplot2::labs(
          title = "GAM Smooth Terms — Classification",
          subtitle = "Partial effects on log-odds of Superhost · shaded band = ±2 SE · edf > 1 = non-linear",
          x = NULL,
          y = "Partial effect (log-odds)"
        ) +
        theme_airbnb()
    } else {
      cat("No valid smooth terms could be parsed for plotting.\n")
    }
  }
  
} else if (inherits(m_gam_c, "glm")) {
  cat("Smooth-term plot skipped: GAM chunk fell back to GLM, so there are no spline smooths to plot.\n")
  
} else {
  cat("Smooth-term plot skipped: model object is neither mgcv GAM/BAM nor GLM.\n")
  print(class(m_gam_c))
}


```

Each panel shows how one continuous predictor affects the log-odds of Superhost status, holding everything else at its training-set average. The number in parentheses (the effective degrees of freedom, edf) indicates how non-linear the relationship is: edf close to 1 means basically linear, edf above 3 means there is real curvature with threshold effects or diminishing returns.

The shaded bands (plus/minus 2 SE) widen at the edges where observations are sparse. The middle portion of each curve is the most reliable part to interpret.

These GAM curves are worth comparing with the SHAP dependence plots in Section 7.22. When both methods agree on the shape of a relationship, even though they work very differently, that is good evidence the pattern is real and not just an artefact of one algorithm.

## 7.8 Model C6: Neural Network

I included a single-hidden-layer neural network (via `nnet`) to see whether a learned feature-weighting scheme outperforms the additive structure of the GAM. It is a simple architecture by modern standards, but it gives us a non-linear baseline between the interpretable models and the ensembles.

```{r model-nn-class}
# nn_grid_c <- expand.grid(size = c(5, 10), decay = c(0.01, 0.1))
# set.seed(42)
# m_nn_c <- train(is_superhost ~ ., data = train_class_balanced,
#                 method = "nnet", trControl = ctrl_class,
#                 metric = "ROC", tuneGrid = nn_grid_c,
#                 MaxNWts = 5000, trace = FALSE, maxit = 200)
# cat("Neural Network — best size:", m_nn_c$bestTune$size,
#     "| decay:", m_nn_c$bestTune$decay,
#     "| CV ROC:", round(max(m_nn_c$results$ROC, na.rm=TRUE), 4), "\n")

dummies_nn_c <- caret::dummyVars(
  is_superhost ~ .,
  data = train_class_balanced,
  fullRank = TRUE,
  na.action = na.pass
)

x_train_nn_c <- predict(dummies_nn_c, newdata = train_class_balanced)
x_train_nn_c <- as.data.frame(x_train_nn_c)

# Keep finite columns only (reuse this mask on test data later)
good_cols_nn_c <- sapply(x_train_nn_c, function(col) all(is.finite(col)))
x_train_nn_c <- x_train_nn_c[, good_cols_nn_c, drop = FALSE]

# Scale predictors (important for nnet)
pp_nn_c <- caret::preProcess(x_train_nn_c, method = c("center", "scale"))
x_train_nn_c_scaled <- predict(pp_nn_c, x_train_nn_c)

# Validate: zero-variance columns can produce NaN/Inf after centering+scaling
stopifnot("Post-scaling NaN/Inf detected in NN features" =
            all(is.finite(as.matrix(x_train_nn_c_scaled))))

y_train_nn_c <- train_class_balanced$is_superhost

nn_grid_c <- expand.grid(
  size  = c(5, 10),
  decay = c(0.01, 0.1)
)

set.seed(42)
m_nn_c <- train(
  x = x_train_nn_c_scaled,
  y = y_train_nn_c,
  method = "nnet",
  trControl = ctrl_class,
  metric = "ROC",
  tuneGrid = nn_grid_c,
  trace = FALSE,
  maxit = 200,
  MaxNWts = 20000
)

cat("Neural Network — best size:", m_nn_c$bestTune$size,
    "| decay:", m_nn_c$bestTune$decay,
    "| CV ROC:", round(max(m_nn_c$results$ROC, na.rm = TRUE), 4), "\n")

```

## 7.9 Model C7: Decision Tree (with Pruning)

Following the workflow from Notebook 3.3, I grow a full decision tree and then prune it back using cross-validation. The accuracy will not beat the ensembles, but a single tree is the most interpretable model here: you can trace every prediction as a chain of if-then rules.

```{r model-tree-class, fig.width=18, fig.height=12}
set.seed(42)
m_tree_c <- rpart(
  is_superhost ~ ., data = train_class_balanced,
  method = "class",
  control = rpart.control(cp = 0.001, minsplit = 20, maxdepth = 15)
)

# Cross-validation pruning: select cp with minimum xerror
cp_table <- as.data.frame(m_tree_c$cptable)
best_cp  <- cp_table$CP[which.min(cp_table$xerror)]

# Apply 1-SE rule: smallest tree within 1 SE of the minimum xerror
min_xerror   <- min(cp_table$xerror)
min_xstd     <- cp_table$xstd[which.min(cp_table$xerror)]
cp_1se       <- cp_table$CP[cp_table$xerror <= min_xerror + min_xstd][1]
cp_prune     <- max(best_cp, cp_1se)

m_tree_pruned <- prune(m_tree_c, cp = cp_prune)

# Tree visualisation
rpart.plot(m_tree_pruned,
           type = 4, extra = 104, under = TRUE,
           col = "#1B4F72", shadow.col = "grey85",
           main = "Pruned Decision Tree — Superhost Classification",
           sub = paste0("CP = ", round(cp_prune, 5),
                        " · ", sum(m_tree_pruned$frame$var == "<leaf>"), " terminal nodes"),
           cex = 0.7)

# Pruning curve
ggplot(cp_table, aes(x = CP, y = xerror)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = xerror - xstd, ymax = xerror + xstd),
              fill = "#2E86C1", alpha = 0.12) +
  geom_line(colour = "#1B4F72", linewidth = 1.4) +
  geom_point(colour = "#2E86C1", size = 4.5, shape = 21, fill = "white", stroke = 1.2) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = min_xerror + min_xstd, linetype = "dashed", colour = "#E74C3C",
             linewidth = 1.0) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = cp_prune, linetype = "dotted", colour = "#27AE60",
             linewidth = 1.0) +
  annotate("text", x = cp_prune * 1.5, y = max(cp_table$xerror) * 0.95,
           label = paste0("Pruned CP = ", round(cp_prune, 5)), colour = "#27AE60", size = 5.5) +
  labs(title = "Cost-Complexity Pruning Curve",
       subtitle = "Red dashed = 1-SE threshold · Green = selected CP",
       x = "Complexity Parameter (CP)", y = "Cross-Validation Error") +
  theme_airbnb()

cat("Decision Tree — terminal nodes:", sum(m_tree_pruned$frame$var == "<leaf>"),
    "| CP:", round(cp_prune, 5), "\n")
```

The first split usually lands on `host_response_rate` or `reviews_per_month`, which lines up with what the black-box models also show: host responsiveness is the single most informative feature. Accuracy is lower than the ensembles, but every prediction traces a readable path, which is useful for explaining the logic.

## 7.10 Model C8: LDA (Linear Discriminant Analysis)

LDA is the Bayesian counterpart to logistic regression. As Professor Morala covered in the classification lecture, it assumes Gaussian class distributions with shared covariance, which gives linear decision boundaries. It often performs comparably to logistic regression when the normality assumption roughly holds.

```{r model-lda-class}
library(MASS)  # lda/qda — detached after QDA chunk to prevent masking dplyr::select()
set.seed(42)
# LDA requires numeric features. Use caret's preProcess to handle near-zero-variance
# columns that arise from dummy encoding (which can cause singular within-class covariance).
m_lda_c <- tryCatch({
  train(is_superhost ~ ., data = train_class_balanced,
        method = "lda", trControl = ctrl_class,
        metric = "ROC",
        preProcess = c("nzv", "center", "scale"))
}, error = function(e) {
  cat("LDA failed:", conditionMessage(e), "\n")
  cat("Attempting LDA2 (stepwise LDA via caret) ...\n")
  tryCatch({
    train(is_superhost ~ ., data = train_class_balanced,
          method = "lda2", trControl = ctrl_class,
          metric = "ROC",
          preProcess = c("nzv", "corr", "center", "scale"))
  }, error = function(e2) {
    cat("LDA2 also failed:", conditionMessage(e2), "\n")
    # Final fallback: use MASS::lda directly on numeric-only subset
    lda_num_cols <- names(train_class_balanced)[sapply(train_class_balanced, is.numeric)]
    train_num <- train_class_balanced[, c(lda_num_cols, "is_superhost")]
    train(is_superhost ~ ., data = train_num,
          method = "lda", trControl = ctrl_class,
          metric = "ROC",
          preProcess = c("nzv", "center", "scale"))
  })
})

cat("LDA — CV ROC:", round(max(m_lda_c$results$ROC), 4), "\n")
```

## 7.11 Model C9: QDA (Quadratic Discriminant Analysis)

QDA relaxes LDA's shared-covariance assumption, letting each class have its own covariance structure. This creates curved decision boundaries, but the trade-off is more parameters and a higher risk of overfitting, especially with many dummy-encoded features.

```{r model-qda-class}
set.seed(42)

# QDA requires non-singular within-class covariance matrices.
# With many dummy features, some columns may be near-constant within one class.
# We use caret's built-in preProcessing to remove near-zero-variance and center/scale.
m_qda_c <- tryCatch({
  train(is_superhost ~ ., data = train_class_balanced,
        method = "qda", trControl = ctrl_class,
        metric = "ROC",
        preProcess = c("nzv", "center", "scale"))
}, error = function(e) {
  cat("QDA failed (likely singular covariance):", conditionMessage(e), "\n")
  cat("Falling back to regularised QDA (method = 'stepQDA') ...\n")
  # Fallback: Regularised Discriminant Analysis approximating QDA
  tryCatch({
    train(is_superhost ~ ., data = train_class_balanced,
          method = "rda", trControl = ctrl_class,
          metric = "ROC",
          preProcess = c("nzv", "center", "scale"),
          tuneGrid = expand.grid(gamma = c(0, 0.01, 0.1), lambda = c(0, 0.1, 0.5)))
  }, error = function(e2) {
    cat("RDA also failed:", conditionMessage(e2), "\n")
    NULL
  })
})

if (!is.null(m_qda_c)) {
  cat("QDA/RDA — CV ROC:", round(max(m_qda_c$results$ROC), 4), "\n")
} else {
  cat("QDA/RDA — could not be fitted (too many near-collinear features).\n")
}

# Detach MASS to prevent MASS::select() from masking dplyr::select() downstream
if ("package:MASS" %in% search()) detach("package:MASS", unload = TRUE)
```

## 7.12 Model C10: k-Nearest Neighbours (KNN)

KNN just looks at the *k* most similar training observations and takes a majority vote. No parametric assumptions at all. I was not expecting KNN to perform this well given the mixed feature types, but features are centred and scaled, and the tuned *k* controls the bias-variance trade-off.

```{r model-knn-class}
set.seed(42)
m_knn_c <- train(is_superhost ~ ., data = train_class_balanced,
                 method = "knn", trControl = ctrl_class,
                 metric = "ROC",
                 tuneGrid = expand.grid(k = c(5, 11, 21, 31, 51)),
                 preProcess = c("center", "scale"))
cat("KNN — best k:", m_knn_c$bestTune$k,
    "| CV ROC:", round(max(m_knn_c$results$ROC), 4), "\n")
```

## 7.13 Model C11: Naive Bayes

Naive Bayes assumes conditional independence between features, which is clearly unrealistic here, but the course introduced this as a useful probabilistic baseline. It gives per-feature likelihood ratios, so you can see exactly how much each variable shifts the odds.

```{r model-nb-class}
set.seed(42)
```
