```{r model-nb-class}
m_nb_c <- train(is_superhost ~ ., data = train_class_balanced,
                method = "naive_bayes", trControl = ctrl_class,
                metric = "ROC", tuneLength = 5)
cat("Naive Bayes — CV ROC:", round(max(m_nb_c$results$ROC, na.rm = TRUE), 4), "\n")
```

## 7.14 Model C12: Support Vector Machine (SVM)

```{r model-svm-class}
set.seed(42)
m_svm_c <- train(
  is_superhost ~ ., data = train_class_balanced,
  method = "svmRadial",
  trControl = ctrl_class,
  metric = "ROC",
  tuneLength = 5,
  preProcess = c("center", "scale")
)
cat("SVM (Radial) — best sigma:", round(m_svm_c$bestTune$sigma, 5),
    "| C:", round(m_svm_c$bestTune$C, 3),
    "| CV ROC:", round(max(m_svm_c$results$ROC), 4), "\n")
```

```{r kan-export-for-python}

# Export KAN-ready matrices for external Python training
# (stable alternative to running KAN via reticulate inside knitr)

if (!exists("run_kan_export")) run_kan_export <- FALSE

if (!isTRUE(run_kan_export)) {
  
  cat("KAN export skipped (run_kan_export = FALSE).\n")
  cat("Set run_kan_export <- TRUE in setup to regenerate x_kan_train.csv / y_kan_train.csv / x_kan_test.csv.\n")
  
} else {
  
  # Build dummy-encoded matrices (same logic as KAN chunk)
  dum_kan_export <- caret::dummyVars(
    is_superhost ~ .,
    data = train_class_balanced,
    fullRank = TRUE,
    na.action = na.pass
  )
  
  x_kan_train_export <- predict(dum_kan_export, newdata = train_class_balanced)
  x_kan_test_export  <- predict(dum_kan_export, newdata = test_class)
  
  # Keep only finite columns based on TRAIN (reuse same mask for test)
  good_cols_kan_export <- apply(x_kan_train_export, 2, function(col) all(is.finite(col)))
  x_kan_train_export <- x_kan_train_export[, good_cols_kan_export, drop = FALSE]
  x_kan_test_export  <- x_kan_test_export[,  good_cols_kan_export, drop = FALSE]
  
  if (ncol(x_kan_train_export) == 0) {
    stop("KAN export matrix has zero valid columns after finite-value filtering.")
  }
  
  # Remove near-zero variance columns before scaling (safer for NN / avoids scale issues)
  nzv_idx_kan_export <- caret::nearZeroVar(as.data.frame(x_kan_train_export))
  if (length(nzv_idx_kan_export) > 0) {
    x_kan_train_export <- x_kan_train_export[, -nzv_idx_kan_export, drop = FALSE]
    x_kan_test_export  <- x_kan_test_export[,  -nzv_idx_kan_export, drop = FALSE]
  }
  
  if (ncol(x_kan_train_export) == 0) {
    stop("KAN export matrix has zero columns after removing near-zero variance predictors.")
  }
  
  # Scale using training data parameters only
  pp_kan_export <- caret::preProcess(as.data.frame(x_kan_train_export), method = c("center", "scale"))
  x_kan_train_export <- predict(pp_kan_export, as.data.frame(x_kan_train_export)) |> as.matrix()
  x_kan_test_export  <- predict(pp_kan_export, as.data.frame(x_kan_test_export))  |> as.matrix()
  
  # Post-scaling finite check (important)
  finite_after_scale <- apply(x_kan_train_export, 2, function(col) all(is.finite(col)))
  if (!all(finite_after_scale)) {
    bad_cols <- colnames(x_kan_train_export)[!finite_after_scale]
    x_kan_train_export <- x_kan_train_export[, finite_after_scale, drop = FALSE]
    x_kan_test_export  <- x_kan_test_export[,  finite_after_scale, drop = FALSE]
    cat("Dropped non-finite columns after scaling:", paste(bad_cols, collapse = ", "), "\n")
  }
  
  if (ncol(x_kan_train_export) == 0) {
    stop("KAN export matrix has zero columns after post-scaling finite check.")
  }
  
  # Binary outcome (robust positive class handling)
  if (!is.factor(train_class_balanced$is_superhost) || !is.factor(test_class$is_superhost)) {
    stop("is_superhost must be a factor in both train and test for KAN export.")
  }
  
  levs_kan_export <- levels(train_class_balanced$is_superhost)
  if (length(levs_kan_export) != 2) stop("KAN export expects binary factor outcome.")
  
  pos_lab_kan_export <- levs_kan_export[2]
  
  y_kan_train_export <- as.integer(train_class_balanced$is_superhost == pos_lab_kan_export)
  
  # Save to CSV for Python
  readr::write_csv(as.data.frame(x_kan_train_export), "x_kan_train.csv")
  readr::write_csv(tibble::tibble(y = y_kan_train_export), "y_kan_train.csv")
  readr::write_csv(as.data.frame(x_kan_test_export), "x_kan_test.csv")
  
  # Save feature names (useful for debugging / reproducibility)
  readr::write_csv(
    tibble::tibble(feature = colnames(x_kan_train_export)),
    "x_kan_feature_names.csv"
  )
  
  # Optional metadata for reproducibility/checks
  kan_meta <- tibble::tibble(
    key = c("n_train", "n_test", "n_features", "positive_class"),
    value = c(
      nrow(x_kan_train_export),
      nrow(x_kan_test_export),
      ncol(x_kan_train_export),
      pos_lab_kan_export
    )
  )
  readr::write_csv(kan_meta, "kan_export_meta.csv")
  
  cat("KAN export files written to:", normalizePath(".", winslash = "/"), "\n")
  cat(" - x_kan_train.csv (", nrow(x_kan_train_export), " x ", ncol(x_kan_train_export), ")\n", sep = "")
  cat(" - y_kan_train.csv (", length(y_kan_train_export), " rows)\n", sep = "")
  cat(" - x_kan_test.csv  (", nrow(x_kan_test_export), " x ", ncol(x_kan_test_export), ")\n", sep = "")
  cat(" - x_kan_feature_names.csv (", ncol(x_kan_train_export), " features)\n", sep = "")
  cat("Positive class for KAN:", pos_lab_kan_export, "\n")
}
```

## 7.15 Model C13: Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN)

<div class="callout-kan">
**Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs)** are a 2024 neural architecture that replace fixed activation functions with learnable B-spline transformations on each edge. The idea comes from the Kolmogorov-Arnold representation theorem: any continuous multivariate function can be decomposed into sums and compositions of univariate functions, and KANs learn those univariate functions directly.

I included KAN as an experimental benchmark via Python (`pykan`). Because it is trained externally (not through `caret`), it does not participate in the cross-validation comparison and is evaluated on the test set only. Due to KAN's quadratic memory scaling with grid resolution, training uses a random subsample of up to 6,000 observations for classification and 5,000 for regression; the full test set is used for evaluation in both cases.
</div>

```{r model-kan-class}

# KAN is run externally in Python (kan_train.py) because reticulate + pykan
# caused native crashes (segfaults) during R Markdown rendering on this machine.
# This chunk initializes placeholders only. Predictions are imported below.

kan_pred_test <- NULL
kan_available <- FALSE
kan_auc_test  <- NA_real_

cat("KAN training is executed externally in Python (kan_train.py).\n")
cat("This chunk intentionally skips in-session KAN fitting for render stability.\n")
cat("Expected file: kan_pred_test.csv in the project working directory.\n\n")
cat("To generate KAN predictions before knitting:\n")
cat("  1. Ensure kan_train.py and kan_reg_train.py are in the project directory\n")
cat("  2. Run: python kan_train.py       (classification predictions)\n")
cat("  3. Run: python kan_reg_train.py   (regression predictions)\n")
cat("  4. This produces kan_pred_test.csv and kan_reg_pred_test.csv\n")
cat("  5. If files are absent, KAN is excluded from downstream analyses gracefully.\n")
```


```{r kan-import-preds}


# Import external KAN predictions generated by kan_train.py

kan_pred_path <- "kan_pred_test.csv"

if (file.exists(kan_pred_path)) {
  
  kan_df <- readr::read_csv(kan_pred_path, show_col_types = FALSE)
  
  if (!"kan_pred_test" %in% names(kan_df)) {
    stop("kan_pred_test.csv must contain a column named 'kan_pred_test'.")
  }
  
  kan_pred_test <- as.numeric(kan_df$kan_pred_test)
  
  # Length check — warn and skip if stale predictions don't match current split
  if (length(kan_pred_test) != nrow(test_class)) {
    warning(
      "KAN prediction length mismatch: expected ", nrow(test_class),
      ", got ", length(kan_pred_test),
      ". Skipping KAN (re-run kan_train.py to regenerate)."
    )
    kan_available <- FALSE
    kan_pred_test <- NULL
  }
  
  if (is.null(kan_pred_test)) {
    # Length mismatch — skip remainder
  } else {

  # Finite check (fail fast on NaN/Inf)
  if (any(!is.finite(kan_pred_test))) {
    stop("KAN predictions contain non-finite values (NA/NaN/Inf).")
  }

  # Bound to [0, 1] for safety
  kan_pred_test <- pmin(pmax(kan_pred_test, 0), 1)

  # Optional consistency check against export metadata (if available)
  meta_path <- "kan_export_meta.csv"
  if (file.exists(meta_path)) {
    meta_df <- readr::read_csv(meta_path, show_col_types = FALSE)
    if (all(c("key", "value") %in% names(meta_df))) {
      meta_list <- stats::setNames(meta_df$value, meta_df$key)
      
      if ("n_test" %in% names(meta_list)) {
        n_test_meta <- suppressWarnings(as.integer(meta_list[["n_test"]]))
        if (!is.na(n_test_meta) && n_test_meta != nrow(test_class)) {
          warning(
            "kan_export_meta.csv reports n_test = ", n_test_meta,
            " but current test_class has ", nrow(test_class), " rows."
          )
        }
      }
      
      if ("positive_class" %in% names(meta_list)) {
        cat("KAN export positive class (metadata):", meta_list[["positive_class"]], "\n")
      }
    }
  }
  
  # Compute held-out test AUC
  kan_auc_test <- as.numeric(
    pROC::auc(
      pROC::roc(
        response  = test_class$is_superhost,
        predictor = kan_pred_test,
        levels    = levels(test_class$is_superhost),
        direction = "<",
        quiet     = TRUE
      )
    )
  )
  
  kan_available <- TRUE

  cat("Imported external KAN predictions from:", kan_pred_path, "\n")
  cat("KAN test AUC (held-out test set):", round(kan_auc_test, 4), "\n")
  cat("Note: KAN was trained externally on a subsample and is not CV-comparable to caret models.\n")

  } # end if (!is.null(kan_pred_test))

} else {
  
  cat("KAN predictions file not found:", kan_pred_path, "\n")
  cat("Working directory during render:", normalizePath(".", winslash = "/"), "\n")
  cat("KAN will be excluded from downstream comparison tables/plots.\n")
}
```



---

## 7.16 Classification Model Comparison

```{r classification-cv-comparison}

# CV comparison for caret-trained classifiers only --------------------------------
# Note: m_gam_c is an mgcv::bam()/glm object (not a caret::train object), so it
# cannot be included in caret::resamples(). We compare caret models via CV here
# and report GAM on the held-out test set separately below.

class_models_caret <- list(
  "Logistic Regression" = m_glm,
  "Elastic Net"         = m_enet_c,
  "Random Forest"       = m_rf_c,
  "XGBoost"             = m_xgb_c,
  "Neural Network"      = m_nn_c,
  "LDA"                 = m_lda_c,
  "KNN"                 = m_knn_c,
  "Naive Bayes"         = m_nb_c,
  "SVM"                 = m_svm_c
)

# Add QDA/RDA if it was successfully fitted
if (exists("m_qda_c") && !is.null(m_qda_c)) {
  class_models_caret[["QDA"]] <- m_qda_c
}

class_resamples <- caret::resamples(class_models_caret)

# 1) CV summary table (caret models only)
summary(class_resamples)$statistics$ROC |>
  as.data.frame() |>
  tibble::rownames_to_column("Model") |>
  dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(Mean)) |>
  styled_kable(
    digits = 4,
    caption = "5-Fold CV ROC-AUC: Caret Classification Models (GAM reported separately)",
    col.names = c("Model","Min","Q1","Median","Mean","Q3","Max","NA's")
  )

# 2) CV distribution plot (caret models only)
cv_vals_class <- as.data.frame(class_resamples$values)

cv_vals_class |>
  tidyr::pivot_longer(
    cols = -dplyr::all_of("Resample"),
    names_to = "model_metric",
    values_to = "value"
  ) |>
  tidyr::separate(model_metric, into = c("Model", "Metric"), sep = "~") |>
  dplyr::filter(Metric == "ROC") |>
  ggplot2::ggplot(ggplot2::aes(
    x = reorder(Model, value, FUN = median),
    y = value,
    fill = Model
  )) +
  ggplot2::geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.8, outlier.alpha = 0.35, outlier.size = 1.5,
                        colour = "#5D6D7E", width = 0.65, linewidth = 0.6) +
  ggplot2::coord_flip() +
  ggplot2::scale_fill_manual(values = pal_blue) +
  ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::number_format(accuracy = 0.001)) +
  ggplot2::labs(
    title = "5-Fold CV ROC-AUC Distribution by Model",
    subtitle = "Caret-trained models · box = IQR · line = median · ordered by median performance",
    x = NULL,
    y = "ROC-AUC (higher is better)"
  ) +
  theme_airbnb() +
  ggplot2::theme(legend.position = "none",
                 panel.grid.major.y = element_blank())

# 3) GAM held-out test ROC (reported separately because it is not a caret model)
if (!is.factor(test_class$is_superhost)) {
  stop("test_class$is_superhost must be a factor for ROC evaluation.")
}

needed_gam_cols <- c(gam_num_cols_used_c, "is_superhost")
missing_gam_cols <- setdiff(needed_gam_cols, names(test_class))
if (length(missing_gam_cols) > 0) {
  stop("Missing GAM test columns: ", paste(missing_gam_cols, collapse = ", "))
}

gam_test_c <- test_class[, needed_gam_cols, drop = FALSE]

# Predict positive-class probability from mgcv GAM / GLM fallback
# (Both mgcv::bam and glm return probabilities with type = 'response' here.)
p_gam_test <- predict(m_gam_c, newdata = gam_test_c, type = "response")
p_gam_test <- pmin(pmax(as.numeric(p_gam_test), 0), 1)

levs_test <- levels(test_class$is_superhost)
if (length(levs_test) != 2) {
  stop("Classification outcome must have exactly 2 levels.")
}

gam_roc_obj <- pROC::roc(
  response  = test_class$is_superhost,
  predictor = p_gam_test,
  levels    = levs_test,
  direction = "<",
  quiet     = TRUE
)

gam_test_auc_tbl <- tibble::tibble(
  Model = paste0("GAM (", class(m_gam_c)[1], ")"),
  `Held-out Test ROC-AUC` = as.numeric(pROC::auc(gam_roc_obj))
)

styled_kable(
  gam_test_auc_tbl,
  digits = 4,
  caption = "GAM Held-out Test ROC-AUC (reported separately from caret CV resamples)"
)
```

### Pairwise Significance Tests (CV Resamples)

When two models differ by a fraction of an AUC point, is that a real difference or just noise from the particular fold assignments? I use paired t-tests on fold-level ROC values to answer this, applying Holm correction for multiple comparisons as covered in the course's regularisation module.

```{r class-cv-significance}

# Pairwise CV significance tests for caret-trained classification models only
# (GAM via mgcv::bam/glm is excluded because it is not a caret::train object)

cv_vals <- as.data.frame(class_resamples$values)
roc_cols <- grep("~ROC$", names(cv_vals), value = TRUE)

if (length(roc_cols) < 2) {
  stop("Need at least two ROC columns in class_resamples$values to run pairwise tests.")
}

roc_df <- cv_vals[, c("Resample", roc_cols), drop = FALSE]
clean_model_names <- sub("~ROC$", "", roc_cols)
names(roc_df)[match(roc_cols, names(roc_df))] <- clean_model_names

pair_idx <- utils::combn(clean_model_names, 2, simplify = FALSE)

pairwise_tbl <- purrr::map_dfr(pair_idx, function(pair) {
  m1 <- pair[1]
  m2 <- pair[2]

  x <- roc_df[[m1]]
  y <- roc_df[[m2]]

  keep <- is.finite(x) & is.finite(y)
  x <- x[keep]
  y <- y[keep]

  if (length(x) < 2) {
    return(tibble::tibble(
      Comparison = paste0(m1, " - ", m2),
      Mean_Diff  = NA_real_,
      p_value    = NA_real_
    ))
  }

  tt <- stats::t.test(x, y, paired = TRUE)

  tibble::tibble(
    Comparison = paste0(m1, " - ", m2),
    Mean_Diff  = mean(x - y),
    p_value    = tt$p.value
  )
})

pairwise_tbl |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    p_value_holm = stats::p.adjust(p_value, method = "holm"),
    Direction = dplyr::case_when(
      Mean_Diff > 0 ~ "\u2191",
      Mean_Diff < 0 ~ "\u2193",
      TRUE ~ "="
    ),
    Significant  = dplyr::if_else(!is.na(p_value_holm) & p_value_holm < 0.05, "Yes", ""),
    Mean_Diff    = round(Mean_Diff, 5),
    p_value      = round(p_value, 4),
    p_value_holm = round(p_value_holm, 4)
  ) |>
  dplyr::arrange(p_value_holm, p_value) |>
  dplyr::select(Comparison, Mean_Diff, Direction, p_value, p_value_holm, Significant) |>
  styled_kable(
    caption = "Pairwise paired t-test on 5-Fold CV ROC-AUC: Caret Classification Models (Holm-adjusted p-values; GAM excluded; \u2191 = row model better)",
    col.names = c("Comparison (row \u2212 col)", "Mean \u0394AUC", "Direction", "p-value", "Holm p-value", "Holm p < 0.05")
  )

```

### Interpretation — What Drives Superhost Status?

Random Forest (AUC = 0.937) and XGBoost (AUC = 0.910) clearly outperform the linear baselines. The gap is nearly 0.10 AUC units, which tells me the relationship between listing features and Superhost status is quite **non-linear**: threshold effects and feature interactions matter more than simple additive contributions.

Looking at the results across models, a few things stand out from the SHAP values, GAM smooth terms, and variable importance rankings:

1. **Review activity is the strongest signal.** Features like `reviews_per_month`, `number_of_reviews_ltm`, and `number_of_reviews_ly` consistently rank at the top. This makes sense because Airbnb's Superhost criteria require maintaining a minimum number of stays and a high response rate. The relationship is non-linear: review velocity matters more at the low end (few to moderate reviews) than at the high end.

2. **Host tenure and professionalism separate top hosts.** Longer-tenured hosts (`host_tenure_days`) and those with verified identity and profile photos are more likely to be Superhosts. The GAM smooth terms show a sharp increase in Superhost probability during the first 1 to 2 years, which then plateaus, suggesting the badge reflects accumulated reputation rather than perpetual improvement.

3. **Location context matters but does not dominate.** Neighbourhood-level features (`neigh_superhost_rate`, `neigh_median_price`) do contribute, reflecting spatial clustering of quality hosts. But city-level differences are modest. The drivers of Superhost status seem more universal than city-specific. I initially thought location would matter more for Superhost, but the data tells a different story.

Naive Bayes performs respectably despite its independence assumption, which suggests individual features carry strong marginal signals on their own. KNN's performance shows that Superhosts are locally separable in feature space, meaning similar listings tend to share Superhost status.

## 7.17 Test Set Performance

```{r classification-test-performance}



# Test-set evaluation for all classification models --------------------------------

# 0) Align factor levels in test set to training set (critical for caret predict())
align_factor_levels <- function(newdata, template_data) {
  out <- newdata
  common_cols <- intersect(names(out), names(template_data))
  
  for (nm in common_cols) {
    if (is.factor(template_data[[nm]])) {
      out[[nm]] <- factor(as.character(out[[nm]]), levels = levels(template_data[[nm]]))
    }
  }
  out
}

test_class_aligned <- align_factor_levels(test_class, train_class_balanced)

# 1) XGBoost test matrix (must match training dummy columns)
test_class_xgb <- predict(dummies_class, newdata = test_class_aligned)[, good_cols_c, drop = FALSE] |>
  as.data.frame()
test_class_xgb$is_superhost <- test_class_aligned$is_superhost

# 1b) Neural-network test matrix (must match columns used when m_nn_c was trained)
nn_coefnames <- NULL
if (!is.null(m_nn_c$coefnames)) {
  nn_coefnames <- m_nn_c$coefnames
} else if (!is.null(m_nn_c$finalModel$xNames)) {
  nn_coefnames <- m_nn_c$finalModel$xNames
}

if (is.null(nn_coefnames)) {
  stop("Could not recover predictor column names for Neural Network model (m_nn_c).")
}

nn_dummy_mat <- predict(dummies_class, newdata = test_class_aligned) |>
  as.data.frame()

# Add any missing NN columns (e.g., levels seen in train but absent in test)
missing_nn_cols <- setdiff(nn_coefnames, names(nn_dummy_mat))
if (length(missing_nn_cols) > 0) {
  for (cc in missing_nn_cols) nn_dummy_mat[[cc]] <- 0
}

# Keep exact predictor order expected by the NN model
test_class_nn <- nn_dummy_mat[, nn_coefnames, drop = FALSE]

# Scale test data using the SAME preProcess object fitted on training (pp_nn_c)
# The NN was trained on centered+scaled features — test must match.
test_nn_predictors <- test_class_nn[, intersect(names(x_train_nn_c), names(test_class_nn)), drop = FALSE]
test_nn_predictors_scaled <- predict(pp_nn_c, test_nn_predictors)
stopifnot(all(is.finite(as.matrix(test_nn_predictors_scaled))))
test_class_nn <- test_nn_predictors_scaled
test_class_nn$is_superhost <- test_class_aligned$is_superhost

# 2) GAM-safe test frame (must match numeric predictors used in GAM fit)
needed_gam_cols <- c(gam_num_cols_used_c, "is_superhost")
missing_gam_cols <- setdiff(needed_gam_cols, names(test_class_aligned))
if (length(missing_gam_cols) > 0) {
  stop("Missing GAM test columns: ", paste(missing_gam_cols, collapse = ", "))
}
gam_test_c <- test_class_aligned[, needed_gam_cols, drop = FALSE]

# Predict GAM probabilities (mgcv::bam or glm fallback both support type='response')
p_gam_test <- predict(m_gam_c, newdata = gam_test_c, type = "response")
p_gam_test <- pmin(pmax(as.numeric(p_gam_test), 0), 1)

# 3) Generic evaluator
eval_class_model <- function(model, test_data, model_name, prob_vec = NULL) {
  actual <- test_data$is_superhost
  
  if (!is.factor(actual) || length(levels(actual)) != 2) {
    stop("Outcome must be a binary factor for classification evaluation.")
  }
  
  if (!is.null(prob_vec)) {
    pred_prob <- as.numeric(prob_vec)
    pred_prob <- pmin(pmax(pred_prob, 0), 1)
    pred_class <- factor(
      dplyr::if_else(pred_prob >= 0.5, levels(actual)[2], levels(actual)[1]),
      levels = levels(actual)
    )
  } else {
    pred_prob <- predict(model, newdata = test_data, type = "prob")[, levels(actual)[2]]
    pred_class <- predict(model, newdata = test_data)
  }
  
  roc_obj <- pROC::roc(
    response  = actual,
    predictor = pred_prob,
    levels    = levels(actual),
    direction = "<",
    quiet     = TRUE
  )
  
  cm <- caret::confusionMatrix(pred_class, actual, positive = levels(actual)[2])
  
  list(
    model     = model_name,
    auc       = as.numeric(pROC::auc(roc_obj)),
    accuracy  = as.numeric(cm$overall["Accuracy"]),
    precision = as.numeric(cm$byClass["Precision"]),
    recall    = as.numeric(cm$byClass["Recall"]),
    f1        = as.numeric(cm$byClass["F1"]),
    roc_obj   = roc_obj
  )
}

# 4) Safe wrapper (better error messages)
safe_eval <- function(...) {
  args <- list(...)
  model_name_here <- if (length(args) >= 3) args[[3]] else "Unknown"
  
  tryCatch(
    eval_class_model(...),
    error = function(e) {
      stop("Failed while evaluating model '", model_name_here, "': ", conditionMessage(e))
    }
  )
}

# 5) Evaluate models
test_results_class <- list(
  safe_eval(m_glm,    test_class_aligned, "Logistic Regression"),
  safe_eval(m_enet_c, test_class_aligned, "Elastic Net"),
  safe_eval(m_rf_c,   test_class_aligned, "Random Forest"),
  safe_eval(m_xgb_c,  test_class_xgb,     "XGBoost"),
  safe_eval(NULL,     gam_test_c,         "GAM", prob_vec = p_gam_test),
  safe_eval(m_nn_c,   test_class_nn,      "Neural Network"),
  safe_eval(m_knn_c,  test_class_aligned, "KNN"),
  safe_eval(m_nb_c,   test_class_aligned, "Naive Bayes"),
  safe_eval(m_svm_c,  test_class_aligned, "SVM")
)

# Add LDA if successfully trained
if (!is.null(m_lda_c)) {
  test_results_class[[length(test_results_class) + 1]] <-
    safe_eval(m_lda_c, test_class_aligned, "LDA")
}

# Add QDA/RDA if successfully trained
if (!is.null(m_qda_c)) {
  test_results_class[[length(test_results_class) + 1]] <-
    safe_eval(m_qda_c, test_class_aligned, "QDA/RDA")
}

# Add Decision Tree (rpart — predict returns matrix, extract positive class column)
tree_probs_test <- predict(m_tree_pruned, newdata = test_class_aligned, type = "prob")
tree_prob_pos <- tree_probs_test[, levels(test_class_aligned$is_superhost)[2]]
test_results_class[[length(test_results_class) + 1]] <-
  safe_eval(NULL, test_class_aligned, "Decision Tree", prob_vec = tree_prob_pos)

# Add KAN results if available (imported externally)
if (exists("kan_available") && isTRUE(kan_available) &&
    exists("kan_pred_test") && !is.null(kan_pred_test)) {
  test_results_class[[length(test_results_class) + 1]] <-
    safe_eval(NULL, test_class_aligned, "KAN", prob_vec = kan_pred_test)
}

# 6) Performance table
test_perf_class <- purrr::map_df(test_results_class, function(x) {
  tibble::tibble(
    Model     = x$model,
    AUC       = x$auc,
    Accuracy  = x$accuracy,
    Precision = x$precision,
    Recall    = x$recall,
    F1        = x$f1
  )
}) |>
  dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(AUC))

# Enhanced leaderboard: bold best, add CI and significance
# best_class_idx_tbl: position within sorted test_perf_class (always row 1 after arrange)
best_class_idx_tbl <- which.max(test_perf_class$AUC)
best_model_name_tbl <- test_perf_class$Model[best_class_idx_tbl]

# Compute DeLong p-values (best vs all others)
delong_pvals_all <- sapply(seq_along(test_results_class), function(i) {
  if (test_results_class[[i]]$model == best_model_name_tbl) return(NA_real_)
  tryCatch(
    pROC::roc.test(
      test_results_class[[which(sapply(test_results_class, `[[`, "model") == best_model_name_tbl)]]$roc_obj,
      test_results_class[[i]]$roc_obj,
      method = "delong")$p.value,
    error = function(e) NA_real_
  )
})
names(delong_pvals_all) <- sapply(test_results_class, function(x) x$model)

# Holm-adjust DeLong p-values for multiple comparisons
delong_pvals_holm <- stats::p.adjust(delong_pvals_all, method = "holm")
names(delong_pvals_holm) <- names(delong_pvals_all)

test_perf_class |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    Sig = dplyr::case_when(
      Model == best_model_name_tbl ~ "---",
      !is.na(delong_pvals_holm[Model]) & delong_pvals_holm[Model] < 0.01 ~ "**",
      !is.na(delong_pvals_holm[Model]) & delong_pvals_holm[Model] < 0.05 ~ "*",
      TRUE ~ ""
    ),
    dplyr::across(where(is.numeric), ~round(.x, 4)),
    Model = dplyr::if_else(
      Model == best_model_name_tbl,
      paste0("**", Model, "**"), Model
    )
  ) |>
  styled_kable(caption = "Test Set Performance: All Classification Models (** = Holm-adj. DeLong p < 0.01 vs best; * = p < 0.05)")

# 7) DeLong test: best vs second-best by test AUC
ranked_idx <- order(purrr::map_dbl(test_results_class, "auc"), decreasing = TRUE)
best_idx   <- ranked_idx[1]
second_idx <- ranked_idx[2]

best_class_name <- test_results_class[[best_idx]]$model
roc_best_dl     <- test_results_class[[best_idx]]$roc_obj
roc_second_dl   <- test_results_class[[second_idx]]$roc_obj

delong_test <- pROC::roc.test(roc_best_dl, roc_second_dl, method = "delong")

cat("\nDeLong AUC test:\n")
cat("  Best:   ", test_results_class[[best_idx]]$model,
    " | AUC = ", round(test_results_class[[best_idx]]$auc, 4), "\n", sep = "")
cat("  Second: ", test_results_class[[second_idx]]$model,
    " | AUC = ", round(test_results_class[[second_idx]]$auc, 4), "\n", sep = "")
cat("  ΔAUC = ",
    round(as.numeric(delong_test$estimate[[1]] - delong_test$estimate[[2]]), 4),
    " | p-value = ", round(delong_test$p.value, 4), "\n\n", sep = "")
```

## 7.18 Cost-Sensitive Threshold Optimisation

In practice, misclassifying a Superhost as a regular host (false negative) is probably more costly than the reverse (false positive). A platform that misses genuine Superhosts risks losing its best hosts to competitors, which is worse than occasionally awarding the badge too early.

```{r cost-sensitive-analysis, fig.width=19, fig.height=9}
# Define asymmetric cost structure
# FN (miss a superhost) = 3× the cost of FP (label non-superhost as super)
cost_fn <- 3
cost_fp <- 1

# Use best model's predicted probabilities
best_idx_cost <- which.max(purrr::map_dbl(test_results_class, "auc"))
best_model_cost <- test_results_class[[best_idx_cost]]$model
best_roc_cost <- test_results_class[[best_idx_cost]]$roc_obj

# Extract probabilities from best model's ROC object
best_probs <- best_roc_cost$predictor
actual_labels <- test_class_aligned$is_superhost
pos_lev <- levels(actual_labels)[2]

# Sweep thresholds to find cost-minimising cutoff
thresholds <- seq(0.05, 0.95, by = 0.01)
cost_df <- purrr::map_df(thresholds, function(tau) {
  pred <- factor(ifelse(best_probs >= tau, pos_lev, levels(actual_labels)[1]),
                 levels = levels(actual_labels))
  cm <- table(Predicted = pred, Actual = actual_labels)
  fn <- if (pos_lev %in% rownames(cm)) sum(actual_labels == pos_lev & pred != pos_lev) else 0
  fp <- if (pos_lev %in% rownames(cm)) sum(actual_labels != pos_lev & pred == pos_lev) else 0
  tibble(threshold = tau, FN = fn, FP = fp,
         total_cost = cost_fn * fn + cost_fp * fp,
         f1 = tryCatch({
           p <- sum(pred == pos_lev & actual_labels == pos_lev) / max(sum(pred == pos_lev), 1)
           r <- sum(pred == pos_lev & actual_labels == pos_lev) / max(sum(actual_labels == pos_lev), 1)
           2 * p * r / max(p + r, 1e-8)
         }, error = function(e) NA_real_))
})

opt_tau <- cost_df$threshold[which.min(cost_df$total_cost)]
default_tau <- 0.5

p_cost <- ggplot(cost_df, aes(x = threshold)) +
  geom_area(aes(y = total_cost), fill = "#1B4F72", alpha = 0.06) +
  geom_line(aes(y = total_cost), linewidth = 1.7, colour = "#1B4F72") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = opt_tau, linetype = "dashed", colour = "#E74C3C", linewidth = 1.3) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = default_tau, linetype = "dotted", colour = "#95A5A6", linewidth = 1.0) +
  geom_point(data = cost_df |> filter(threshold == opt_tau),
             aes(y = total_cost), colour = "#E74C3C", size = 6, shape = 18) +
  annotate("label", x = opt_tau + 0.03, y = max(cost_df$total_cost) * 0.88,
           label = paste0("Optimal: τ = ", opt_tau), colour = "#E74C3C", size = 5, fontface = "bold",
           hjust = 0, fill = "white", label.size = 0.3) +
  annotate("label", x = default_tau + 0.03, y = max(cost_df$total_cost) * 0.76,
           label = "Default: τ = 0.50", colour = "#7F8C8D", size = 5, fontface = "bold",
           hjust = 0, fill = "white", label.size = 0.3) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(title = "Cost-Sensitive Threshold Optimisation",
       subtitle = paste0("Asymmetric costs: FN = ", cost_fn, "× | FP = ", cost_fp,
                         "× · diamond marks optimal operating point"),
       x = "Classification Threshold (τ)", y = "Total Weighted Cost") +
  theme_airbnb()

print(p_cost)

cat("Default threshold (0.50) total cost:", cost_df$total_cost[cost_df$threshold == 0.5], "\n")
cat("Optimal threshold (", opt_tau, ") total cost:", min(cost_df$total_cost), "\n")
cat("Cost reduction:", round((1 - min(cost_df$total_cost) / cost_df$total_cost[cost_df$threshold == 0.5]) * 100, 1), "%\n")
```

<div class="callout-insight">
Shifting the threshold from the default 0.50 to the cost-optimal value noticeably reduces the total weighted misclassification cost. The logic behind this is straightforward: missing a genuine Superhost (a false negative) is more costly to the platform than temporarily over-awarding the badge (a false positive), because the latter self-corrects at the next review cycle while the former may drive a good host away. This kind of cost-sensitive threshold tuning is how statistical models connect to actual business decisions.
</div>

```{r classification-bootstrap-ci}

# 8) Bootstrap 95% CI for best model AUC
set.seed(42)

if (best_class_name == "KAN") {
  if (!exists("kan_pred_test") || is.null(kan_pred_test)) {
    stop("KAN selected as best model, but kan_pred_test is unavailable.")
  }
  best_probs_c <- kan_pred_test
  
} else if (best_class_name == "XGBoost") {
  best_probs_c <- predict(m_xgb_c, newdata = test_class_xgb, type = "prob")[, levels(test_class_aligned$is_superhost)[2]]
  
} else if (best_class_name == "GAM") {
  best_probs_c <- p_gam_test
  
} else {
  best_model_obj <- dplyr::case_when(
    best_class_name == "Logistic Regression" ~ list(m_glm),
    best_class_name == "Elastic Net"         ~ list(m_enet_c),
    best_class_name == "Random Forest"       ~ list(m_rf_c),
    best_class_name == "Neural Network"      ~ list(m_nn_c),
    best_class_name == "KNN"                 ~ list(m_knn_c),
    best_class_name == "Naive Bayes"         ~ list(m_nb_c),
    best_class_name == "SVM"                 ~ list(m_svm_c),
    best_class_name == "LDA"                 ~ list(m_lda_c),
    best_class_name %in% c("QDA/RDA","QDA") ~ list(m_qda_c),
    best_class_name == "Decision Tree"       ~ list(NULL),
    TRUE ~ list(NULL)
  )[[1]]

  if (best_class_name == "Decision Tree") {
    best_probs_c <- predict(m_tree_pruned, newdata = test_class_aligned, type = "prob")[, levels(test_class_aligned$is_superhost)[2]]
  } else if (is.null(best_model_obj)) {
    stop("Could not map best_class_name to a model object: ", best_class_name)
  } else if (best_class_name == "Neural Network") {
    best_probs_c <- predict(best_model_obj, newdata = test_class_nn, type = "prob")[, levels(test_class_aligned$is_superhost)[2]]
  } else {
    best_probs_c <- predict(best_model_obj, newdata = test_class_aligned, type = "prob")[, levels(test_class_aligned$is_superhost)[2]]
  }
}

actual_c <- test_class_aligned$is_superhost

boot_auc_c <- replicate(2000, {
  # Stratified bootstrap: resample within each class to preserve class proportions
  idx_pos <- which(actual_c == levels(actual_c)[2])
  idx_neg <- which(actual_c == levels(actual_c)[1])
  idx_b <- c(sample(idx_pos, replace = TRUE), sample(idx_neg, replace = TRUE))
  suppressMessages(
    as.numeric(
      pROC::auc(
        pROC::roc(
          response  = actual_c[idx_b],
          predictor = best_probs_c[idx_b],
          levels    = levels(actual_c),
          direction = "<"
        )
      )
    )
  )
})

cat("Bootstrap 95% CI — Best Classifier AUC (", best_class_name, "):\n",
    "  [", round(stats::quantile(boot_auc_c, 0.025), 4), ", ",
    round(stats::quantile(boot_auc_c, 0.975), 4), "]\n", sep = "")

```

## 7.19 ROC Curves & Precision-Recall Curves

```{r classification-roc-pr, fig.width=18, fig.height=22}

# ROC and Precision-Recall curves on held-out test set --------------------------

# Color palette (extend if needed)
cols <- c("#1B4F72","#2E86C1","#F39C12","#8E44AD","#27AE60","#2980B9","#E74C3C","#16A085",
           "#D35400","#2C3E50","#C0392B","#7D3C98","#1ABC9C")

if (!exists("test_results_class") || length(test_results_class) == 0) {
  stop("test_results_class not found. Run the classification-test-performance chunk first.")
}

# Build tidy ROC data
roc_df <- purrr::map_df(seq_along(test_results_class), function(i) {
  roc_i <- test_results_class[[i]]$roc_obj
  
  coords_i <- pROC::coords(
    roc_i, "all",
    ret = c("threshold", "sensitivity", "specificity"),
    transpose = FALSE
  )
  
  coords_i <- as.data.frame(coords_i)
  coords_i$fpr <- 1 - coords_i$specificity
  coords_i$model <- test_results_class[[i]]$model
  coords_i$model_label <- paste0(
    test_results_class[[i]]$model,
    " (AUC = ",
    round(test_results_class[[i]]$auc, 3),
    ")"
  )
  coords_i
})

# Keep stable legend order = test_results_class order
roc_labels_order <- purrr::map_chr(test_results_class, ~paste0(.x$model, " (AUC = ", round(.x$auc, 3), ")"))
roc_df$model_label <- factor(roc_df$model_label, levels = unique(roc_labels_order))

p_roc <- ggplot2::ggplot(
  roc_df,
  ggplot2::aes(x = fpr, y = sensitivity, colour = model_label, group = model_label)
) +
  ggplot2::geom_abline(
    slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed",
    colour = "#BDC3C7", linewidth = 1.0
  ) +
  ggplot2::geom_line(linewidth = 1.4, alpha = 0.9) +
  ggplot2::annotate("text", x = 0.75, y = 0.22, label = "Random\nclassifier",
                    colour = "#BDC3C7", size = 5, fontface = "italic") +
  ggplot2::scale_colour_manual(
    values = setNames(cols[seq_len(length(levels(roc_df$model_label)))], levels(roc_df$model_label))
  ) +
  ggplot2::scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::percent, limits = c(0, 1),
                               expand = expansion(mult = 0.01)) +
  ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent, limits = c(0, 1),
                               expand = expansion(mult = 0.01)) +
  ggplot2::labs(
    title = "ROC Curves — Test Set",
    subtitle = "AUC shown in legend · diagonal = random classifier (AUC = 0.50)",
    x = "False Positive Rate (1 − Specificity)",
    y = "True Positive Rate (Sensitivity)",
    colour = NULL
  ) +
  theme_airbnb() +
  ggplot2::theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.text = ggplot2::element_text(size = 13)
  ) +
  ggplot2::guides(colour = ggplot2::guide_legend(ncol = 5, override.aes = list(linewidth = 3)))

# Build tidy Precision-Recall data
pr_df <- purrr::map_df(seq_along(test_results_class), function(i) {
  roc_i <- test_results_class[[i]]$roc_obj
  
  coords_i <- pROC::coords(
    roc_i, "all",
    ret = c("recall", "precision"),
    transpose = FALSE
  )
  
  coords_i <- as.data.frame(coords_i)
  coords_i <- coords_i[is.finite(coords_i$recall) & is.finite(coords_i$precision), , drop = FALSE]
  coords_i <- coords_i[order(coords_i$recall), , drop = FALSE]
  coords_i$model <- test_results_class[[i]]$model
  coords_i
})

pr_models_order <- purrr::map_chr(test_results_class, "model")
pr_df$model <- factor(pr_df$model, levels = unique(pr_models_order))

no_skill <- mean(test_class$is_superhost == levels(test_class$is_superhost)[2])

p_pr <- ggplot2::ggplot(
  pr_df,
  ggplot2::aes(x = recall, y = precision, colour = model, group = model)
) +
  ggplot2::geom_hline(
    yintercept = no_skill, linetype = "dashed",
    colour = "#BDC3C7", linewidth = 1.0
  ) +
  ggplot2::geom_line(linewidth = 1.4, alpha = 0.9) +
  ggplot2::annotate(
    "label",
    x = 0.02, y = min(no_skill + 0.025, 0.98),
    label = paste0("No-skill baseline (", round(no_skill, 2), ")"),
    colour = "#95A5A6", size = 5, fontface = "bold", hjust = 0, fill = "white", label.size = 0.2
  ) +
  ggplot2::scale_colour_manual(
    values = setNames(cols[seq_len(length(levels(pr_df$model)))], levels(pr_df$model))
  ) +
  ggplot2::scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::percent, limits = c(0, 1),
                               expand = expansion(mult = 0.01)) +
  ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent, limits = c(0, 1),
                               expand = expansion(mult = 0.01)) +
  ggplot2::labs(
    title = "Precision-Recall Curves — Test Set",
    subtitle = "Dashed line = no-skill baseline (positive-class prevalence in test set)",
    x = "Recall (Sensitivity)",
    y = "Precision (Positive Predictive Value)",
    colour = NULL
  ) +
  theme_airbnb() +
  ggplot2::theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.text = ggplot2::element_text(size = 13)
  ) +
  ggplot2::guides(colour = ggplot2::guide_legend(ncol = 5, override.aes = list(linewidth = 3)))

p_roc / p_pr
```

## 7.20 Optimal Threshold Selection (Youden's J)

<div class="callout-warning">
The default 0.5 classification threshold is arbitrary. There is no reason a model's optimal operating point should fall exactly at 0.5, especially with imbalanced classes. **Youden's J statistic** finds the threshold that maximises Sensitivity + Specificity − 1, giving equal weight to catching true positives and avoiding false positives. This is a better starting point than the default, though a real deployment would tune the threshold to reflect the specific cost of false positives vs. false negatives.
</div>

```{r optimal-threshold}

# Optimal threshold analysis for the best test-set classifier --------------------

if (!exists("test_results_class") || length(test_results_class) == 0) {
  stop("test_results_class not found. Run the classification-test-performance chunk first.")
}

# Identify best model by held-out test AUC
best_class_idx  <- which.max(purrr::map_dbl(test_results_class, "auc"))
best_class_name <- test_results_class[[best_class_idx]]$model
roc_best        <- test_results_class[[best_class_idx]]$roc_obj

# Youden's J optimal threshold
opt_thresh_df <- pROC::coords(
  roc_best,
  x = "best",
  best.method = "youden",
  ret = c("threshold", "sensitivity", "specificity", "accuracy"),
  transpose = FALSE
) |>
  as.data.frame()

if (nrow(opt_thresh_df) == 0) {
  stop("pROC::coords() returned no optimal threshold row.")
}

opt_thr <- as.numeric(opt_thresh_df$threshold[1])
opt_sen <- as.numeric(opt_thresh_df$sensitivity[1])
opt_spe <- as.numeric(opt_thresh_df$specificity[1])
opt_acc <- as.numeric(opt_thresh_df$accuracy[1])

cat("Best classification model:", best_class_name, "\n")
cat("Optimal threshold (Youden's J):", round(opt_thr, 4), "\n")
cat("  Sensitivity:", round(opt_sen, 4), "\n")
cat("  Specificity:", round(opt_spe, 4), "\n")
cat("  Accuracy:   ", round(opt_acc, 4), "\n")

# Threshold sweep plot
thresh_df <- pROC::coords(
  roc_best,
  x = seq(0.1, 0.9, 0.01),
  input = "threshold",
  ret = c("threshold", "sensitivity", "specificity", "accuracy"),
  transpose = FALSE
) |>
  as.data.frame() |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    threshold   = as.numeric(threshold),
    sensitivity = as.numeric(sensitivity),
    specificity = as.numeric(specificity),
    accuracy    = as.numeric(accuracy),
    youdens_j   = sensitivity + specificity - 1
  ) |>
  tidyr::pivot_longer(
    cols = c(sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, youdens_j),
    names_to = "metric",
    values_to = "value"
  )

ggplot2::ggplot(thresh_df, ggplot2::aes(x = threshold, y = value, colour = metric)) +
  ggplot2::geom_line(linewidth = 1.5, alpha = 0.9) +
  ggplot2::geom_vline(
    xintercept = opt_thr,
    linetype = "dashed",
    colour = "#2C3E50",
    linewidth = 1.2
  ) +
  ggplot2::annotate(
    "label",
    x = min(opt_thr + 0.02, 0.92),
    y = 0.12,
    label = paste0("Optimal threshold = ", round(opt_thr, 3)),
    colour = "#2C3E50",
    size = 5, fontface = "bold",
    hjust = 0,
    fill = "white",
    label.size = 0.3,
    label.padding = ggplot2::unit(0.2, "lines")
  ) +
  ggplot2::scale_colour_manual(
    values = c(
      "sensitivity" = "#1B4F72",
      "specificity" = "#2E86C1",
      "accuracy"    = "#F39C12",
      "youdens_j"   = "#8E44AD"
    ),
    labels = c(
      "sensitivity" = "Sensitivity (TPR)",
      "specificity" = "Specificity (TNR)",
      "accuracy"    = "Accuracy",
      "youdens_j"   = "Youden's J (Sens + Spec − 1)"
    )
  ) +
  ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  ggplot2::labs(
    title = paste("Threshold Sweep —", best_class_name),
    subtitle = "Dashed line = Youden's J optimal threshold · maximises Sensitivity + Specificity − 1",
    x = "Classification Threshold",
    y = "Metric Value",
    colour = NULL
  ) +
  theme_airbnb()

```

## 7.21 Calibration Analysis

Well-calibrated classifiers produce predicted probabilities that match observed frequencies. A model predicting 70% Superhost probability should be correct about 70% of the time.

```{r calibration-plot, fig.width=16, fig.height=14}


# Calibration curves for classification models -----------------------------------

# Required objects from earlier chunks
if (!exists("dummies_class")) stop("dummies_class not found. Run model training chunks first.")
if (!exists("good_cols_c")) stop("good_cols_c not found. Run XGBoost prep/training chunk first.")
if (!exists("m_gam_c")) stop("m_gam_c not found. Run GAM chunk first.")
if (!exists("gam_num_cols_used_c")) stop("gam_num_cols_used_c not found. Run GAM chunk first.")

# test_class_aligned, test_class_xgb, test_class_nn, and gam_test_c
# are reused from the classification-test-performance chunk above.

# GAM probabilities (bam/glm fallback both support type='response')
p_gam_cal <- predict(m_gam_c, newdata = gam_test_c, type = "response")
p_gam_cal <- pmin(pmax(as.numeric(p_gam_cal), 0), 1)

# 4) Collect calibration probabilities for all models
pos_level <- levels(test_class_aligned$is_superhost)[2]

cal_models <- list(
  "Logistic Regression" = predict(m_glm,    newdata = test_class_aligned, type = "prob")[, pos_level],
  "Elastic Net"         = predict(m_enet_c, newdata = test_class_aligned, type = "prob")[, pos_level],
  "Random Forest"       = predict(m_rf_c,   newdata = test_class_aligned, type = "prob")[, pos_level],
  "XGBoost"             = predict(m_xgb_c,  newdata = test_class_xgb,     type = "prob")[, pos_level],
  "GAM"                 = p_gam_cal,
  "Neural Network"      = predict(m_nn_c,   newdata = test_class_nn,      type = "prob")[, pos_level],
  "KNN"                 = predict(m_knn_c,  newdata = test_class_aligned, type = "prob")[, pos_level],
  "Naive Bayes"         = predict(m_nb_c,   newdata = test_class_aligned, type = "prob")[, pos_level],
  "SVM"                 = predict(m_svm_c,  newdata = test_class_aligned, type = "prob")[, pos_level],
  "Decision Tree"       = predict(m_tree_pruned, newdata = test_class_aligned, type = "prob")[, pos_level]
)

# Add LDA/QDA if available
if (!is.null(m_lda_c)) {
  cal_models[["LDA"]] <- predict(m_lda_c, newdata = test_class_aligned, type = "prob")[, pos_level]
}
if (!is.null(m_qda_c)) {
  cal_models[["QDA/RDA"]] <- predict(m_qda_c, newdata = test_class_aligned, type = "prob")[, pos_level]
}

# Add KAN if available
if (exists("kan_available") && isTRUE(kan_available) &&
    exists("kan_pred_test") && !is.null(kan_pred_test)) {
  cal_models[["KAN"]] <- pmin(pmax(as.numeric(kan_pred_test), 0), 1)
}

# 5) Binary actual outcome (positive class = 2nd factor level)
if (!is.factor(test_class_aligned$is_superhost) || length(levels(test_class_aligned$is_superhost)) != 2) {
  stop("test_class_aligned$is_superhost must be a binary factor.")
}
actual_bin <- as.integer(test_class_aligned$is_superhost == pos_level)

# 6) Build calibration deciles by model
cal_df <- purrr::map_df(names(cal_models), function(nm) {
  probs <- as.numeric(cal_models[[nm]])
  
  if (length(probs) != length(actual_bin)) {
    stop("Length mismatch in calibration data for model ", nm,
         ": probs=", length(probs), ", actual=", length(actual_bin))
  }
  
  probs <- pmin(pmax(probs, 0), 1)
  
  tibble::tibble(
    model  = nm,
    prob   = probs,
    actual = actual_bin
  )
}) |>
  dplyr::group_by(model) |>
  dplyr::mutate(decile = dplyr::ntile(prob, 10)) |>
  dplyr::group_by(model, decile) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    mean_pred = mean(prob, na.rm = TRUE),
    obs_rate  = mean(actual, na.rm = TRUE),
    n         = dplyr::n(),
    .groups   = "drop"
  )

# 7) Plot
cols_cal <- c("#1B4F72","#2E86C1","#F39C12","#8E44AD","#27AE60","#2980B9","#E74C3C","#16A085",
              "#D35400","#2C3E50","#C0392B","#7D3C98","#1ABC9C")
model_levels_cal <- unique(cal_df$model)

ggplot2::ggplot(cal_df, ggplot2::aes(x = mean_pred, y = obs_rate, colour = model)) +
  ggplot2::geom_abline(
    slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed",
    colour = "#BDC3C7", linewidth = 1.0
  ) +
  ggplot2::annotate("text", x = 0.85, y = 0.78, label = "Perfect\ncalibration",
                    colour = "#95A5A6", size = 7, fontface = "italic", angle = 38) +
  ggplot2::geom_line(linewidth = 1.3, alpha = 0.85) +
  ggplot2::geom_point(ggplot2::aes(size = n), alpha = 0.8, shape = 16) +
  ggplot2::scale_size_continuous(range = c(4, 8.5), guide = "none") +
  ggplot2::scale_colour_manual(
    values = setNames(cols_cal[seq_len(length(model_levels_cal))], model_levels_cal)
  ) +
  ggplot2::scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::percent, limits = c(0, 1),
                               expand = expansion(mult = 0.01)) +
  ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent, limits = c(0, 1),
                               expand = expansion(mult = 0.01)) +
  ggplot2::labs(
    title = "Calibration Curves — Classification Models",
    subtitle = "Points on the diagonal = perfectly calibrated · point size = bin count (deciles)",
    x = "Mean Predicted Probability",
    y = "Observed Positive-Class Rate",
    colour = NULL
  ) +
  theme_airbnb() +
  ggplot2::theme(legend.text = element_text(size = 14),
                 legend.title = element_text(size = 15)) +
  ggplot2::guides(colour = ggplot2::guide_legend(ncol = 3, override.aes = list(linewidth = 3, size = 4)))

```

A well-calibrated model is one where a predicted probability of 70% actually corresponds to roughly 70% of cases being positive. The calibration curves above show how each model fares on this test.

**Logistic Regression and Elastic Net** track the diagonal most closely, meaning their probability outputs can be taken at face value. When they say a host has a 70% chance of being a Superhost, that estimate is broadly trustworthy.

**Random Forest and XGBoost** tend to be overconfident in the 0.60-0.85 range, predicting higher probabilities than the observed rates justify. Tree ensembles tend to do this: they push predictions toward 0 or 1 rather than sitting in the middle. The flattening of the Random Forest curve in the 0.7-0.9 zone means probability estimates in this range should be treated with caution.

**GAM** leans the other way: it is slightly under-confident for borderline cases (0.30-0.60 range), assigning conservative probabilities. For a recommendation system, this is actually safer since it is better to be cautious about borderline hosts than overconfident.

In practice, this means that if predicted probabilities will feed into downstream decisions (like recommending hosts or setting thresholds), linear models give more reliable probability outputs even if their AUC is marginally lower. Tree-based models discriminate better but would benefit from post-hoc calibration (like Platt scaling) before their probabilities are used for decision-making.

## 7.22 Confusion Matrix (Best Model)

```{r classification-confusion-matrix}

# Confusion matrix for best classifier at optimal threshold ----------------------

if (!exists("best_class_name")) {
  stop("best_class_name not found. Run the test-performance / threshold chunk first.")
}
if (!exists("opt_thr")) {
  stop("opt_thr not found. Run the optimal-threshold chunk first.")
}

# Use aligned test set if available (safer for caret models with factor predictors)
if (exists("test_class_aligned")) {
  test_ref <- test_class_aligned
} else {
  test_ref <- test_class
}

if (!is.factor(test_ref$is_superhost) || length(levels(test_ref$is_superhost)) != 2) {
  stop("test outcome must be a binary factor.")
}

get_best_pred_class <- function() {
  pos_lab <- levels(test_ref$is_superhost)[2]
  neg_lab <- levels(test_ref$is_superhost)[1]

  # ---- get predicted probabilities for the selected best model ----
  if (best_class_name == "XGBoost") {

    if (!exists("test_class_xgb")) {
      stop("test_class_xgb not found. Run the classification-test-performance chunk first.")
    }
    pred_prob <- as.numeric(
      predict(m_xgb_c, newdata = test_class_xgb, type = "prob")[, pos_lab]
    )

  } else if (best_class_name == "KAN") {

    if (!exists("kan_pred_test") || is.null(kan_pred_test)) {
      stop("Best model is KAN but kan_pred_test is unavailable.")
    }
    pred_prob <- as.numeric(kan_pred_test)

  } else if (best_class_name == "GAM") {

    if (!exists("gam_num_cols_used_c")) {
      stop("gam_num_cols_used_c not found. Run the GAM chunk first.")
    }

    needed_gam_cols <- c(gam_num_cols_used_c, "is_superhost")
    missing_gam_cols <- setdiff(needed_gam_cols, names(test_ref))
    if (length(missing_gam_cols) > 0) {
      stop("Missing GAM test columns: ", paste(missing_gam_cols, collapse = ", "))
    }

    gam_test_c_local <- test_ref[, needed_gam_cols, drop = FALSE]
    pred_prob <- as.numeric(predict(m_gam_c, newdata = gam_test_c_local, type = "response"))

  } else if (best_class_name == "Logistic Regression") {

    pred_prob <- as.numeric(
      predict(m_glm, newdata = test_ref, type = "prob")[, pos_lab]
    )

  } else if (best_class_name == "Elastic Net") {

    pred_prob <- as.numeric(
      predict(m_enet_c, newdata = test_ref, type = "prob")[, pos_lab]
    )

  } else if (best_class_name == "Random Forest") {

    pred_prob <- as.numeric(
      predict(m_rf_c, newdata = test_ref, type = "prob")[, pos_lab]
    )

  } else if (best_class_name == "Neural Network") {

    if (!exists("test_class_nn")) {
      stop("test_class_nn not found. Run the classification-test-performance chunk first.")
    }
    pred_prob <- as.numeric(
      predict(m_nn_c, newdata = test_class_nn, type = "prob")[, pos_lab]
    )

  } else if (best_class_name == "SVM") {
    pred_prob <- as.numeric(
      predict(m_svm_c, newdata = test_ref, type = "prob")[, pos_lab]
    )

  } else if (best_class_name == "LDA") {
    pred_prob <- as.numeric(
      predict(m_lda_c, newdata = test_ref, type = "prob")[, pos_lab]
    )

  } else if (best_class_name %in% c("QDA/RDA", "QDA")) {
    pred_prob <- as.numeric(
      predict(m_qda_c, newdata = test_ref, type = "prob")[, pos_lab]
    )

  } else if (best_class_name == "Decision Tree") {
    pred_prob <- predict(m_tree_pruned, newdata = test_ref, type = "prob")[, pos_lab]

  } else if (best_class_name == "KNN") {
    pred_prob <- as.numeric(
      predict(m_knn_c, newdata = test_ref, type = "prob")[, pos_lab]
    )

  } else if (best_class_name == "Naive Bayes") {
    pred_prob <- as.numeric(
      predict(m_nb_c, newdata = test_ref, type = "prob")[, pos_lab]
    )

  } else {
    stop("Unsupported best_class_name: ", best_class_name)
  }

  # Safety checks
  if (length(pred_prob) != nrow(test_ref)) {
    stop("Prediction length mismatch: expected ", nrow(test_ref), ", got ", length(pred_prob))
  }
  if (any(!is.finite(pred_prob))) {
    stop("Predicted probabilities contain non-finite values (NA/NaN/Inf).")
  }

  pred_prob <- pmin(pmax(pred_prob, 0), 1)

  factor(
    dplyr::if_else(pred_prob >= opt_thr, pos_lab, neg_lab),
    levels = c(neg_lab, pos_lab)
  )
}

pred_best_class <- get_best_pred_class()

cm_best <- caret::confusionMatrix(
  data = pred_best_class,
  reference = test_ref$is_superhost,
  positive = levels(test_ref$is_superhost)[2]
)

cat("Best classification model:", best_class_name, "\n")
cat("Using optimal threshold:", round(opt_thr, 3), "\n\n")
print(cm_best)

# Extract confusion matrix counts for narrative
cm_tbl <- cm_best$table
tp <- cm_tbl[2, 2]; tn <- cm_tbl[1, 1]
fp <- cm_tbl[2, 1]; fn <- cm_tbl[1, 2]
fpr <- round(fp / (fp + tn) * 100, 1)
fnr <- round(fn / (fn + tp) * 100, 1)

cat("\n--- Confusion Matrix Interpretation ---\n")
cat("At the Youden-optimal threshold (", round(opt_thr, 3), "):\n", sep = "")
cat("  True Positives  (", tp, "): Correctly identified Superhosts\n")
cat("  True Negatives  (", tn, "): Correctly identified non-Superhosts\n")
cat("  False Positives (", fp, ", ", fpr, "%): Non-Superhosts incorrectly flagged as Superhosts.\n")
cat("    These hosts may have strong predictive features (high response rate, good reviews)\n")
cat("    but lack the formal designation — potentially 'almost-Superhosts' worth targeting.\n")
cat("  False Negatives (", fn, ", ", fnr, "%): Actual Superhosts the model missed.\n")
cat("    These may represent hosts whose quality is driven by unmeasured factors\n")
cat("    (photo quality, personal interaction style, micro-location appeal).\n\n")
cat("For platform designers, the FN rate is arguably the more concerning error:\n")
cat("  failing to recognise a deserving Superhost undermines host motivation.\n")
cat("  A lower threshold would reduce FNs at the cost of more FPs.\n")

# Confusion matrix heatmap
cm_plot_df <- as.data.frame(cm_tbl) |>
  dplyr::rename(Predicted = Prediction, Actual = Reference, Count = Freq) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    Label = dplyr::case_when(
      Actual == "Yes" & Predicted == "Yes" ~ paste0("TP\n", Count),
      Actual == "No"  & Predicted == "No"  ~ paste0("TN\n", Count),
      Actual == "No"  & Predicted == "Yes" ~ paste0("FP\n", Count),
      Actual == "Yes" & Predicted == "No"  ~ paste0("FN\n", Count)
    )
  )

ggplot(cm_plot_df, aes(x = Actual, y = Predicted, fill = Count)) +
  geom_tile(colour = "white", linewidth = 3) +
  geom_text(aes(label = Label), size = 8, fontface = "bold", colour = "white",
            lineheight = 0.85) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#5DADE2", high = "#1A5276") +
  scale_x_discrete(expand = c(0, 0)) +
  scale_y_discrete(expand = c(0, 0)) +
  labs(
    title    = paste("Confusion Matrix —", best_class_name),
    subtitle = paste0("Youden-optimal threshold = ", round(opt_thr, 3),
                      " · TP/TN on diagonal, FP/FN off-diagonal"),
    x = "Actual Class", y = "Predicted Class"
  ) +
  theme_airbnb() +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        panel.grid = element_blank(),
        axis.text  = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold"),
        aspect.ratio = 1)
```

## 7.23 SHAP Values — XGBoost

SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) provides theoretically grounded, per-observation feature importance. Unlike global VIP scores, SHAP reveals *how* each feature contributed to *each individual prediction*.

```{r shap-class, fig.width=20, fig.height=11}
# SHAP on a sample of training data (full matrix can be very large)
set.seed(42)
shap_idx_c <- sample(nrow(x_train_xgb_c), min(5000, nrow(x_train_xgb_c)))
sv_c <- shapviz(m_xgb_c$finalModel, X_pred = x_train_xgb_c[shap_idx_c, ])

# Global beeswarm importance
p_shap_bee <- sv_importance(sv_c, kind = "beeswarm", max_display = 20) +
  labs(title = "SHAP Beeswarm — XGBoost (Classification)",
       subtitle = "Each dot = one observation · colour = feature value (low → high) · x = SHAP impact") +
  theme_airbnb() +
  theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
        legend.position    = "right",
        legend.key.height  = unit(3.0, "cm"),
        legend.key.width   = unit(0.6, "cm"),
        legend.text   = element_text(size = 14),
        legend.title  = element_text(size = 15, angle = 0),
        legend.margin = margin(t = 8, b = 8, l = 12, r = 8))
print(p_shap_bee)

# Bar chart of mean |SHAP|
p_shap_bar <- sv_importance(sv_c, kind = "bar", max_display = 15) +
  labs(title = "Mean |SHAP| — Feature Importance (Classification)",
       subtitle = "Average absolute SHAP value across all observations · higher = more influential") +
  theme_airbnb() +
  theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_blank())
print(p_shap_bar)
```

The SHAP beeswarm confirms what the other models suggested: **host behaviour is what matters most**. Response rate and acceptance rate sit well above everything else. Higher rates consistently push predictions toward Superhost, which makes sense since Airbnb's own criteria explicitly require above 90% response rate and above 88% acceptance rate. The model has basically learned the platform's rules from the data.

`reviews_per_month` and `host_tenure_days` form a second tier. More active and experienced hosts are more likely to be Superhosts, though the wider spread of SHAP values suggests these effects interact with other variables.

Neighbourhood and property features sit in the middle. `neigh_superhost_rate` captures geographic clustering (hosts in neighbourhoods with many Superhosts tend to be Superhosts themselves), which is probably a selection effect rather than a causal neighbourhood influence. Price shows a non-linear SHAP pattern: moderately priced listings are associated with higher Superhost probability, while very cheap and very expensive listings show negative contributions, suggesting a pricing sweet spot.

NLP keywords, amenity counts, and room type contribute relatively little once the host behaviour features are accounted for. The takeaway is that Superhost status is mostly about *host operations*, not *listing characteristics*.

```{r shap-dependence, fig.width=22, fig.height=10}
# SHAP dependence plots for top predictors
top_shap_vars <- names(sort(colMeans(abs(sv_c$S)), decreasing = TRUE))[1:3]

shap_dep_plots <- map(top_shap_vars, ~{
  sv_dependence(sv_c, v = .x) +
    labs(title = .x,
         subtitle = NULL) +
    theme_airbnb(base_size = 14) +
    theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 15, face = "bold"),
          legend.key.height = unit(0.3, "cm"),
          legend.key.width  = unit(1.8, "cm"),
          legend.text  = element_text(size = 9),
          legend.title = element_text(size = 10),
          legend.direction = "horizontal",
          legend.position = "bottom") +
    guides(colour = guide_colourbar(barwidth = 8, barheight = 0.5,
                                     title.position = "left", title.vjust = 0.8,
                                     label.theme = element_text(size = 9)))
})
wrap_plots(shap_dep_plots, ncol = 3) +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "SHAP Dependence — Top 3 Classification Predictors",
    subtitle = "Colour = interaction variable · each point = one observation",
    theme = theme(plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", colour = "#1B4F72", size = 18),
                  plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "#5D6D7E", size = 13))
  )
```

The dependence plots show *how* each feature affects predictions. For `host_response_rate`, there is a clear threshold: SHAP contributions are near zero below about 70%, climb steeply between 80% and 95%, and level off above 95%. The model has basically learned Airbnb's 90% requirement from the data. The colour gradient suggests this effect is strongest for hosts with longer tenure, so responsiveness combined with experience is a particularly strong signal.

For `host_acceptance_rate`, a similar threshold appears around 85-90%, but with more scatter, suggesting stronger interactions with other features. For `reviews_per_month`, the pattern is different: the steepest gains happen between 0 and 2 reviews per month, with diminishing returns after about 4. This is where the project gets interesting, at least to me, because it points to a minimum engagement threshold rather than "more is always better."

## 7.24 Classification Interpretation

```{r classification-odds-ratios}
glm_or <- tidy(m_glm$finalModel, conf.int = TRUE, exponentiate = TRUE) |>
  filter(term != "(Intercept)") |>
  mutate(
    Significant = if_else(p.value < 0.05, "Yes", "No"),
    abs_log_or  = abs(log(estimate)),
    Variable    = term |>
      str_replace("^city", "City: ") |>
      str_replace("^room_type", "Room type: ") |>
      str_replace("^property_type_simple", "Property type: ") |>
      str_replace_all("_", " ") |> str_squish()
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(abs_log_or)) |>
  slice_head(n = 15) |>
  dplyr::select(Variable, Odds_Ratio = estimate, CI_lower = conf.low,
                CI_upper = conf.high, p_value = p.value, Significant)

glm_or |> styled_kable(digits = 3, caption = "Top 15 Odds Ratios — Logistic Regression (Reference: city = Amsterdam; room_type = Entire home/apt; property_type = first alphabetical level)")
```

Odds ratios show how much each predictor multiplies the odds of being a Superhost, holding everything else constant. An odds ratio of 1.0 means no effect; above 1.0 means higher odds, below 1.0 means lower.

`host_response_rate` has the largest odds ratio: each percentage-point increase is associated with a substantial boost, which matches what the SHAP analysis showed. `host_acceptance_rate` follows a similar pattern. The city indicators show that Madrid and Barcelona hosts face different baseline odds compared to Amsterdam (the reference category), reflecting city-specific Superhost distributions and competition levels.

Features with odds ratios close to 1.0 and wide confidence intervals that cross 1.0 are not statistically significant in this linear model, though they may still contribute non-linearly in tree-based methods. The narrow confidence intervals around the top predictors confirm that these associations are reliable (p < 0.05).

```{r classification-vip}
bt <- m_rf_c$bestTune
rf_final_c <- ranger(
  is_superhost ~ ., data = train_class_balanced,
  num.trees = 200, mtry = bt$mtry,
  min.node.size = bt$min.node.size,
  splitrule = as.character(bt$splitrule),
  importance = "impurity", probability = TRUE, seed = 42
)

vi_c    <- sort(rf_final_c$variable.importance, decreasing = TRUE)
vi_df_c <- tibble(Variable = names(vi_c), Importance = as.numeric(vi_c)) |>
  slice_head(n = 20)

ggplot(vi_df_c, aes(x = reorder(Variable, Importance), y = Importance, fill = Importance)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE, width = 0.75) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#D6EAF8", high = "#1B4F72") +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))) +
  labs(title = "Variable Importance — Random Forest (Classification)",
       subtitle = "Gini impurity reduction · top 20 predictors",
       x = NULL, y = "Importance (impurity decrease)") +
  theme_airbnb() +
  theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_blank())
```

PDPs show the *average* marginal effect but mask individual heterogeneity. **ICE (Individual Conditional Expectation)** plots add one curve per observation, so you can see whether the average effect is uniform or driven by a subset of the data.

```{r classification-pdp-ice, fig.width=21, fig.height=16}
top_feats_c <- c("host_response_rate","host_acceptance_rate",
                 "neigh_superhost_rate","reviews_per_month")
set.seed(42)
train_class_samp <- train_class_balanced |>
  slice_sample(n = min(2000, nrow(train_class_balanced)))

ice_class <- map(top_feats_c, ~{
  # ICE + centered PDP
  pdp_ice <- partial(m_rf_c, pred.var = .x, train = train_class_samp,
                     prob = TRUE, which.class = "Yes",
                     ice = TRUE, center = TRUE)
  # Average curve (centered PDP)
  pdp_avg <- pdp_ice |>
    group_by(across(all_of(.x))) |>
    summarise(yhat = mean(yhat), .groups = "drop")

  ggplot() +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "#EAECEE", linewidth = 0.6) +
    geom_line(data = pdp_ice,
              aes(x = .data[[.x]], y = yhat, group = yhat.id),
              colour = "#1B4F72", alpha = 0.04, linewidth = 0.35) +
    geom_line(data = pdp_avg,
              aes(x = .data[[.x]], y = yhat),
              colour = "#E74C3C", linewidth = 2.2) +
    labs(title = paste("ICE + PDP:", .x),
         subtitle = "Faint lines = individual ICE · red bold = PDP average (centered)",
         y = "ΔP(Superhost)") +
    theme_airbnb()
})

grid.arrange(grobs = ice_class, ncol = 2,
             top = textGrob("Centered ICE + PDP — Top 4 Predictors (Random Forest)",
                            gp = gpar(fontface = "bold", fontsize = 22, col = "#1B4F72")))
```

## 7.25 Model Stacking (Meta-Ensemble)

Model stacking trains a "meta-learner" on out-of-fold (OOF) predictions from multiple classifiers. Each base model's OOF probability becomes a feature, and a logistic regression learns how to weight them. I excluded KNN and Naive Bayes because their lower CV-AUC would add noise rather than useful diversity.

```{r model-stacking-class}

# Stacked ensemble (classification) ---------------------------------------------
# Uses caret OOF predictions where available. GAM (mgcv::bam/glm fallback) is not
# a caret model, so it has no OOF predictions and cannot be trained into the
# meta-learner without leakage. We still include GAM test prediction separately if needed.

# Use aligned test set if available
if (exists("test_class_aligned")) {
  test_ref <- test_class_aligned
} else {
  test_ref <- test_class
}

# Positive class label (robust)
if (!is.factor(test_ref$is_superhost) || length(levels(test_ref$is_superhost)) != 2) {
  stop("test_ref$is_superhost must be a binary factor.")
}
pos_lab_stack <- levels(test_ref$is_superhost)[2]

# Extract OOF positive-class probabilities from caret::train objects
get_oof_probs <- function(model_obj, pos_lab = "Yes") {
  if (is.null(model_obj) || is.null(model_obj$pred)) return(NULL)
  oof <- model_obj$pred
  
  # caret stores class-prob columns with class names; detect robustly
  if (!pos_lab %in% names(oof)) {
    # fallback: use second factor level if present in obs/pred
    prob_cols <- setdiff(names(oof), c("pred", "obs", "rowIndex", "Resample"))
    if (length(prob_cols) == 0) return(NULL)
    if (length(prob_cols) == 1) {
      pos_col <- prob_cols[1]
    } else {
      # try exact match against observed factor levels
      levs <- tryCatch(levels(oof$obs), error = function(e) NULL)
      pos_col <- if (!is.null(levs) && length(levs) == 2 && levs[2] %in% prob_cols) levs[2] else prob_cols[1]
    }
  } else {
    pos_col <- pos_lab
  }
  
  # savePredictions = "final" should already restrict to best tune;
  # sort back to original row order
  oof <- oof[order(oof$rowIndex), , drop = FALSE]
  as.numeric(oof[[pos_col]])
}

# OOF probs for caret models only
oof_glm   <- get_oof_probs(m_glm,   pos_lab_stack)
oof_enet  <- get_oof_probs(m_enet_c, pos_lab_stack)
oof_rf    <- get_oof_probs(m_rf_c,   pos_lab_stack)
oof_xgb_c <- get_oof_probs(m_xgb_c,  pos_lab_stack)
oof_nn    <- get_oof_probs(m_nn_c,   pos_lab_stack)

# GAM is mgcv::bam/gam -> no caret OOF preds (exclude from meta-training)
oof_gam <- NULL

# Build meta-feature matrix from available OOF preds
oof_list <- list(glm = oof_glm, enet = oof_enet, rf = oof_rf, xgb = oof_xgb_c, nn = oof_nn)
oof_lengths <- vapply(oof_list, function(x) if (is.null(x)) 0L else length(x), integer(1))

if (all(oof_lengths == 0)) {
  cat("OOF predictions not available — retrain caret models with savePredictions='final'.\n")
  stack_auc <- NA_real_
  
} else {
  
  oof_n <- min(oof_lengths[oof_lengths > 0])

  # Verify all OOF vectors have identical length (critical for correct alignment)
  valid_oof_lens <- oof_lengths[oof_lengths > 0]
  if (length(unique(valid_oof_lens)) > 1) {
    stop("OOF prediction lengths differ across models: ",
         paste(names(valid_oof_lens), "=", valid_oof_lens, collapse=", "),
         ". Cannot safely build stacking meta-features.")
  }

  # IMPORTANT: target rows must match OOF row order assumptions. This is acceptable only
  # if your caret OOF preds are aligned by rowIndex to train_class_balanced row order.
  meta_df <- data.frame(
    glm  = if (!is.null(oof_glm))   oof_glm[1:oof_n]   else rep(0.5, oof_n),
    enet = if (!is.null(oof_enet))  oof_enet[1:oof_n]  else rep(0.5, oof_n),
    rf   = if (!is.null(oof_rf))    oof_rf[1:oof_n]    else rep(0.5, oof_n),
    xgb  = if (!is.null(oof_xgb_c)) oof_xgb_c[1:oof_n] else rep(0.5, oof_n),
    nn   = if (!is.null(oof_nn))    oof_nn[1:oof_n]    else rep(0.5, oof_n),
    y    = train_class_balanced$is_superhost[1:oof_n]
  )
  
  # Drop rows with any non-finite values (rare but safer)
  keep_meta <- stats::complete.cases(meta_df) &
    apply(meta_df[, c("glm","enet","rf","xgb","nn"), drop = FALSE], 1, function(z) all(is.finite(z)))
  meta_df <- meta_df[keep_meta, , drop = FALSE]
  
  if (nrow(meta_df) < 50) {
    warning("Very few valid rows in meta_df after filtering; stacked model may be unstable.")
  }
  
  meta_model <- stats::glm(y ~ ., data = meta_df, family = "binomial")
  
  # ---- Test-set base predictions ----
  # XGBoost test matrix should already exist from classification-test-performance chunk
  if (!exists("test_class_xgb")) {
    stop("test_class_xgb not found. Run classification-test-performance chunk first.")
  }
  
  # Neural-network test matrix should already exist (required to avoid factor-level errors)
  if (!exists("test_class_nn")) {
    stop("test_class_nn not found. Run classification-test-performance chunk first.")
  }
  
  # GAM probabilities (optional for reporting, not used in stacked meta-features)
  # If already computed, reuse; otherwise compute safely.
  if (exists("p_gam_test")) {
    p_gam_stack <- as.numeric(p_gam_test)
  } else {
    needed_gam_cols <- c(gam_num_cols_used_c, "is_superhost")
    missing_gam_cols <- setdiff(needed_gam_cols, names(test_ref))
    if (length(missing_gam_cols) > 0) {
      stop("Missing GAM test columns: ", paste(missing_gam_cols, collapse = ", "))
    }
    gam_test_c_local <- test_ref[, needed_gam_cols, drop = FALSE]
    p_gam_stack <- as.numeric(predict(m_gam_c, newdata = gam_test_c_local, type = "response"))
  }
  p_gam_stack <- pmin(pmax(p_gam_stack, 0), 1)  # not used in stack, but kept for consistency
  
  # Meta-features must match training meta_df columns exactly (GAM excluded)
  test_meta_df <- data.frame(
    glm  = as.numeric(predict(m_glm,    newdata = test_ref,      type = "prob")[, pos_lab_stack]),
    enet = as.numeric(predict(m_enet_c, newdata = test_ref,      type = "prob")[, pos_lab_stack]),
    rf   = as.numeric(predict(m_rf_c,   newdata = test_ref,      type = "prob")[, pos_lab_stack]),
    xgb  = as.numeric(predict(m_xgb_c,  newdata = test_class_xgb, type = "prob")[, pos_lab_stack]),
    nn   = as.numeric(predict(m_nn_c,   newdata = test_class_nn,  type = "prob")[, pos_lab_stack])
  )
  
  # Safety clamp
  test_meta_df[] <- lapply(test_meta_df, function(z) pmin(pmax(as.numeric(z), 0), 1))
  
  stack_prob <- as.numeric(predict(meta_model, newdata = test_meta_df, type = "response"))
  stack_prob <- pmin(pmax(stack_prob, 0), 1)
  
  stack_auc <- as.numeric(
    pROC::auc(
      pROC::roc(
        response  = test_ref$is_superhost,
        predictor = stack_prob,
        levels    = levels(test_ref$is_superhost),
        direction = "<",
        quiet     = TRUE
      )
    )
  )
  
  cat("Stacked Ensemble Test AUC:", round(stack_auc, 4), "\n")
  cat("Meta-learner coefficients (log-odds scale):\n")
  print(round(stats::coef(meta_model), 4))
  cat("Note: GAM is excluded from stack training because mgcv::bam/gam has no caret OOF predictions.\n")

  # Consolidated leaderboard with stacking ensemble included
  stack_row <- tibble::tibble(
    Model = "**Stacked Ensemble**", AUC = stack_auc,
    Accuracy = NA_real_, Precision = NA_real_,
    Recall = NA_real_, F1 = NA_real_, Sig = "---"
  )

  # Normalized stacking weights
  raw_coefs <- stats::coef(meta_model)[-1]  # exclude intercept
  norm_weights <- exp(raw_coefs) / sum(exp(raw_coefs))
  cat("\nNormalized meta-learner weights (probability space):\n")
  print(round(norm_weights, 3))
}

```

<div class="callout-result">
The stacked ensemble learns the optimal way to combine the base models' predictions, following the workflow from the course's ensemble methods lecture. The meta-learner is a logistic regression whose coefficients (on the log-odds scale) show how much the ensemble trusts each model. A high weight means that model contributes unique predictive signal, catching patterns the other models miss. A near-zero or negative weight means the model is mostly redundant given its peers. The normalised weights (printed above) make this easier to read: they show each model's share of the ensemble's "budget" on a 0-to-1 scale. If the intercept is far from zero, the meta-learner is correcting a systematic bias in the base models' average predicted probability.
</div>

## 7.26 Ensemble Prediction Diversity

Stacking only helps when the base models make **different kinds of errors**. If all five models agree on who is a Superhost, combining them adds nothing. To measure that diversity, I compute the Spearman correlation between each pair of models' predicted probabilities on the test set. Low correlation means the models disagree on the hard cases, which is exactly what the meta-learner needs.

```{r ensemble-diversity, fig.width=16, fig.height=10}

# Ensemble diversity: correlation of predicted probabilities on test set ---------

# Use aligned test set if available (safer for caret models with factors)
if (exists("test_class_aligned")) {
  test_ref <- test_class_aligned
} else {
  test_ref <- test_class
}

# Basic checks
if (!exists("test_class_xgb")) stop("test_class_xgb not found. Run classification-test-performance chunk first.")
if (!exists("test_class_nn"))  stop("test_class_nn not found. Run classification-test-performance chunk first.")
if (!exists("gam_num_cols_used_c")) stop("gam_num_cols_used_c not found. Run GAM chunk first.")

# Positive class label
if (!is.factor(test_ref$is_superhost) || length(levels(test_ref$is_superhost)) != 2) {
  stop("test_ref$is_superhost must be a binary factor.")
}
pos_lab <- levels(test_ref$is_superhost)[2]

# GAM-safe test frame
needed_gam_cols <- c(gam_num_cols_used_c, "is_superhost")
missing_gam_cols <- setdiff(needed_gam_cols, names(test_ref))
if (length(missing_gam_cols) > 0) {
  stop("Missing GAM test columns: ", paste(missing_gam_cols, collapse = ", "))
}
gam_test_c <- test_ref[, needed_gam_cols, drop = FALSE]

# Predicted probabilities (all as numeric vectors)
pred_div_df <- data.frame(
  GLM  = as.numeric(predict(m_glm,    newdata = test_ref,      type = "prob")[, pos_lab]),
  ENet = as.numeric(predict(m_enet_c, newdata = test_ref,      type = "prob")[, pos_lab]),
  RF   = as.numeric(predict(m_rf_c,   newdata = test_ref,      type = "prob")[, pos_lab]),
  XGB  = as.numeric(predict(m_xgb_c,  newdata = test_class_xgb, type = "prob")[, pos_lab]),
  GAM  = as.numeric(predict(m_gam_c,  newdata = gam_test_c,    type = "response")),
  NN   = as.numeric(predict(m_nn_c,   newdata = test_class_nn, type = "prob")[, pos_lab]),
  KNN  = as.numeric(predict(m_knn_c,  newdata = test_ref,      type = "prob")[, pos_lab]),
  NB   = as.numeric(predict(m_nb_c,   newdata = test_ref,      type = "prob")[, pos_lab]),
  SVM  = as.numeric(predict(m_svm_c,  newdata = test_ref,      type = "prob")[, pos_lab]),
  DTree = predict(m_tree_pruned, newdata = test_ref, type = "prob")[, pos_lab]
)

# Add LDA/QDA if available
if (!is.null(m_lda_c)) {
  pred_div_df$LDA <- as.numeric(predict(m_lda_c, newdata = test_ref, type = "prob")[, pos_lab])
}
if (!is.null(m_qda_c)) {
  pred_div_df$QDA <- as.numeric(predict(m_qda_c, newdata = test_ref, type = "prob")[, pos_lab])
}

# Add KAN if available
if (exists("kan_available") && isTRUE(kan_available) &&
    exists("kan_pred_test") && !is.null(kan_pred_test)) {
  pred_div_df$KAN <- as.numeric(kan_pred_test)
}

# Safety checks
if (nrow(pred_div_df) != nrow(test_ref)) {
  stop("Prediction matrix row mismatch.")
}
if (any(!is.finite(as.matrix(pred_div_df)))) {
  stop("Non-finite values found in predicted probabilities.")
}

# Clamp to [0,1] just in case
pred_div_df[] <- lapply(pred_div_df, function(z) pmin(pmax(as.numeric(z), 0), 1))

# Spearman correlation of predicted probabilities
cor_div <- stats::cor(pred_div_df, method = "spearman")

corrplot::corrplot(
  cor_div,
  method      = "color",
  type        = "upper",
  addCoef.col = "#2C3E50",
  number.cex  = 1.4,
  number.font = 2,
  tl.col      = "#2C3E50",
  tl.cex      = 1.3,
  tl.srt      = 45,
  cl.cex      = 1.0,
  col         = colorRampPalette(c("#2E86C1", "#EBF5FB", "white", "#FDEDEC", "#E74C3C"))(200),
  title       = "Spearman Correlation of Predicted Probabilities — Test Set",
  mar         = c(0, 0, 2.5, 0)
)

```

<div class="callout-insight">
Pairs below 0.90 are the interesting ones: they disagree on different listings, which is what gives the ensemble its edge. Pairs near 1.0 are essentially voting the same way every time, so they add redundancy rather than diversity. The diversity pattern here should roughly align with the stacking weights above: models that are most different from the rest tend to get higher meta-learner weight.
</div>

---

# 8. Advanced Regression — Predicting Nightly Price

Next, I flip the question: instead of predicting who a host *is*, I predict what a listing *costs*. Eight regression models target `log(price + 1)`, with results back-transformed to the dollar scale for interpretation. I chose the log transformation because it corrects the heavy right skew of raw prices and makes residuals approximately normal, as Professor Morala emphasised in the regression diagnostics lecture. The pipeline follows the same structure as classification: 5-fold CV on training data, final evaluation on the test set, plus residual diagnostics, calibration, and price tier segmentation.

## 8.1 Training Control & Data Prep

```{r regression-control}
ctrl_reg <- trainControl(
  method = "cv", number = 5,
  savePredictions = "final",
  verboseIter = FALSE
)

train_reg <- train_df |> select(all_of(reg_features), log_price) |> na.omit()
test_reg  <- test_df  |> select(all_of(reg_features), log_price) |> na.omit()

cat("Regression data after na.omit():\n")
cat("  Train:", format(nrow(train_reg), big.mark=","), "rows (dropped",
```
