```{r regression-control}
    format(nrow(train_df) - nrow(train_reg), big.mark=","), ")\n")
cat("  Test: ", format(nrow(test_reg), big.mark=","), "rows (dropped",
    format(nrow(test_df) - nrow(test_reg), big.mark=","), ")\n")

cat("Regression training set:", format(nrow(train_reg), big.mark=","), "\n")
cat("Regression test set:    ", format(nrow(test_reg),  big.mark=","), "\n")
```

## 8.2 Baseline: Mean Price

```{r baseline-regression}
mean_log_price   <- mean(train_reg$log_price)
baseline_pred    <- expm1(rep(mean_log_price, nrow(test_reg)))
baseline_actual  <- expm1(test_reg$log_price)
baseline_rmse    <- sqrt(mean((baseline_actual - baseline_pred)^2))
baseline_mae     <- mean(abs(baseline_actual - baseline_pred))

cat("Baseline RMSE: $", round(baseline_rmse, 2),
    " | MAE: $", round(baseline_mae, 2), "\n")
```

## 8.3 Model R1: Linear Regression

```{r model-lm}
set.seed(42)
m_lm <- train(log_price ~ ., data = train_reg, method = "lm", trControl = ctrl_reg)
cat("OLS — CV RMSE:", round(min(m_lm$results$RMSE), 4),
    "| R²:", round(max(m_lm$results$Rsquared), 4), "\n")
```

### Variance Inflation Factors (VIF)

```{r vif-regression}
# VIF requires no aliased (perfectly collinear) coefficients.
# With many dummy-encoded factors some levels may be aliased; handle gracefully.
vif_result <- tryCatch({
  vif_vals <- car::vif(m_lm$finalModel)

  if (is.matrix(vif_vals)) {
    vif_df <- data.frame(
      Variable = rownames(vif_vals),
      GVIF = vif_vals[, "GVIF"],
      Df = vif_vals[, "Df"],
      Adjusted_VIF = vif_vals[, "GVIF^(1/(2*Df))"]
    ) |> dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(Adjusted_VIF))
  } else {
    vif_df <- data.frame(Variable = names(vif_vals), VIF = as.numeric(vif_vals)) |>
      dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(VIF))
  }
  vif_df
}, error = function(e) {
  cat("VIF could not be computed (", conditionMessage(e), ").\n")
  cat("This typically occurs when dummy-encoded factor levels are perfectly collinear.\n")
  cat("Multicollinearity is handled natively by Elastic Net and tree-based models.\n")
  NULL
})

if (!is.null(vif_result)) {
  vif_result |> dplyr::slice_head(n = 20) |>
    styled_kable(digits = 2, caption = "Top 20 Variance Inflation Factors (OLS Regression)")

  high_vif <- sum(if ("Adjusted_VIF" %in% names(vif_result)) vif_result$Adjusted_VIF > 5 else vif_result$VIF > 5)
  cat(high_vif, "variables with VIF > 5 (moderate multicollinearity)\n")
  cat("Note: Elastic Net and tree-based models handle collinearity natively.\n")
}
```

## 8.4 Model R2: Stepwise Regression (AIC/BIC)

Following Notebook 3.4, I run forward-backward AIC selection to find the most parsimonious linear model, and compare it with BIC (which penalises complexity more heavily and retains fewer variables).

```{r model-stepwise-reg}
set.seed(42)

# Full OLS model for stepwise basis
full_lm <- lm(log_price ~ ., data = train_reg)

# Forward-backward AIC selection
step_aic <- step(full_lm, direction = "both", trace = 0, k = 2)           # k=2 is AIC
step_bic <- step(full_lm, direction = "both", trace = 0, k = log(nrow(train_reg)))  # k=log(n) is BIC

n_aic <- length(coef(step_aic)) - 1
n_bic <- length(coef(step_bic)) - 1

cat("Stepwise AIC — retained:", n_aic, "of", length(coef(full_lm)) - 1, "predictors\n")
cat("Stepwise BIC — retained:", n_bic, "of", length(coef(full_lm)) - 1, "predictors (stricter penalty)\n")

# Wrap AIC model in caret-compatible format for fair comparison
# Use the AIC-selected formula in caret::train with 5-fold CV
aic_formula <- formula(step_aic)
m_step_r <- train(aic_formula, data = train_reg, method = "lm", trControl = ctrl_reg)
cat("Stepwise AIC — CV RMSE:", round(min(m_step_r$results$RMSE), 4),
    "| R²:", round(max(m_step_r$results$Rsquared), 4), "\n")

# Report which variables BIC dropped relative to AIC
aic_vars <- names(coef(step_aic))[-1]
bic_vars <- names(coef(step_bic))[-1]
dropped_by_bic <- setdiff(aic_vars, bic_vars)
if (length(dropped_by_bic) > 0) {
  cat("BIC additionally dropped:", paste(head(dropped_by_bic, 8), collapse = ", "),
      if (length(dropped_by_bic) > 8) paste0("... (", length(dropped_by_bic), " total)") else "", "\n")
}
```

AIC retains almost the full feature set, and even BIC only drops a handful of variables (mostly redundant neighbourhood dummies). That is reassuring since it means the feature engineering did not overload the model with noise.

## 8.5 Model R3: PCR & PLS (Dimension Reduction Regression)

PCR and PLS both handle multicollinearity by compressing the predictor space into fewer components. The difference is that PCR extracts components based on feature variance (unsupervised), while PLS extracts components that maximise covariance with the target (supervised), so PLS usually needs fewer components to reach good performance.

```{r model-pcr-pls-reg, fig.width=18, fig.height=8}
# Prepare model matrix (pcr/plsr need numeric matrix, no factors)
train_reg_mm <- model.matrix(log_price ~ ., data = train_reg)[, -1]
test_reg_mm  <- model.matrix(log_price ~ ., data = test_reg)[, -1]

# Ensure columns match
common_cols_pls <- intersect(colnames(train_reg_mm), colnames(test_reg_mm))
train_reg_mm    <- train_reg_mm[, common_cols_pls]
test_reg_mm     <- test_reg_mm[, common_cols_pls]

# Pre-process: center and scale
pp_pls <- preProcess(train_reg_mm, method = c("center", "scale", "nzv"))
train_pls_scaled <- predict(pp_pls, train_reg_mm)
test_pls_scaled  <- predict(pp_pls, test_reg_mm)

# Combine into data frames for pls
train_pls_df <- data.frame(log_price = train_reg$log_price, train_pls_scaled)
test_pls_df  <- data.frame(log_price = test_reg$log_price, test_pls_scaled)

# PCR with leave-one-out CV
set.seed(42)
m_pcr <- pcr(log_price ~ ., data = train_pls_df, scale = FALSE, validation = "CV", segments = 5)
ncomp_pcr <- selectNcomp(m_pcr, method = "onesigma", plot = FALSE)
cat("PCR — optimal ncomp (1-SE rule):", ncomp_pcr, "\n")

# PLS with leave-one-out CV
set.seed(42)
m_pls <- plsr(log_price ~ ., data = train_pls_df, scale = FALSE, validation = "CV", segments = 5)
ncomp_pls <- selectNcomp(m_pls, method = "onesigma", plot = FALSE)
cat("PLS — optimal ncomp (1-SE rule):", ncomp_pls, "\n")

# Validation RMSEP plot
par(mfrow = c(1, 2), cex.main = 1.5, cex.lab = 1.3, cex.axis = 1.2)
plot(RMSEP(m_pcr), main = "PCR — RMSEP vs Components", legendpos = "topright")
abline(v = ncomp_pcr, lty = 2, col = "#E74C3C", lwd = 2)
plot(RMSEP(m_pls), main = "PLS — RMSEP vs Components", legendpos = "topright")
abline(v = ncomp_pls, lty = 2, col = "#E74C3C", lwd = 2)
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

# Test-set predictions
pcr_pred <- predict(m_pcr, newdata = test_pls_df, ncomp = ncomp_pcr)
pls_pred <- predict(m_pls, newdata = test_pls_df, ncomp = ncomp_pls)

pcr_rmse <- sqrt(mean((expm1(test_reg$log_price) - expm1(as.numeric(pcr_pred)))^2))
pls_rmse <- sqrt(mean((expm1(test_reg$log_price) - expm1(as.numeric(pls_pred)))^2))

cat("PCR — Test RMSE (dollar): $", round(pcr_rmse, 2),
    "| PLS — Test RMSE (dollar): $", round(pls_rmse, 2), "\n")
cat("PLS achieves similar performance with", ncomp_pls, "components vs PCR's", ncomp_pcr, "\n")
```

As expected, PLS reaches good performance with fewer components than PCR, since it extracts components directly relevant to price. Both methods are useful as regularised alternatives to OLS when predictors are correlated, and the number of components needed gives a rough sense of how many independent dimensions of information drive pricing.

## 8.6 Model R4: Elastic Net

```{r model-enet-reg}
set.seed(42)
m_enet_r <- train(log_price ~ ., data = train_reg, method = "glmnet",
                  trControl = ctrl_reg, tuneLength = 12)
cat("Elastic Net — alpha:", round(m_enet_r$bestTune$alpha, 3),
    "| lambda:", round(m_enet_r$bestTune$lambda, 4),
    "| CV RMSE:", round(min(m_enet_r$results$RMSE), 4), "\n")
```

### Elastic Net Regularization Path (Regression)

The regularisation path shows which features the model considers most consistently predictive of price, following the penalisation logic covered in Notebook 3.3. As the penalty increases (moving left), weaker features are shrunk to zero first. Features that survive the longest carry the strongest pricing signal.

```{r enet-path-reg, fig.width=20, fig.height=9}
best_alpha_r <- m_enet_r$bestTune$alpha
enet_fit_r   <- glmnet::glmnet(
  x      = model.matrix(log_price ~ ., data = train_reg)[, -1],
  y      = train_reg$log_price,
  family = "gaussian",
  alpha  = best_alpha_r
)

# Top 15 features by final absolute coefficient
final_coefs_r <- coef(enet_fit_r, s = m_enet_r$bestTune$lambda)
top15_r <- names(sort(abs(final_coefs_r[-1, 1]), decreasing = TRUE))[1:15]

path_mat_r <- as.matrix(t(coef(enet_fit_r)[c(top15_r), ]))
path_df_r  <- as.data.frame(path_mat_r) |>
  mutate(log_lambda = log(enet_fit_r$lambda)) |>
  pivot_longer(-log_lambda, names_to = "Variable", values_to = "Coefficient") |>
  mutate(Variable = Variable |> str_replace_all("[._]", " ") |> str_squish())

ggplot(path_df_r, aes(x = log_lambda, y = Coefficient,
                       colour = Variable, group = Variable)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, colour = "#EAECEE", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2, alpha = 0.9) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = log(m_enet_r$bestTune$lambda),
             linetype = "dashed", colour = "#E74C3C", linewidth = 1.2) +
  annotate("label",
           x    = log(m_enet_r$bestTune$lambda),
           y    = max(abs(path_df_r$Coefficient)) * 0.95,
           label = paste0("Best λ = ", round(m_enet_r$bestTune$lambda, 4)),
           colour = "#E74C3C", size = 5, fontface = "bold", hjust = -0.05, fill = "white",
           label.size = 0.3, label.padding = unit(0.2, "lines")) +
  labs(title = "Elastic Net Regularization Path — Regression (Top 15 Features)",
       subtitle = paste0("α = ", round(best_alpha_r, 3),
                         " · Features shrink to zero as regularisation penalty increases (→ left)"),
       x = "log(λ)", y = "Coefficient", colour = NULL) +
  theme_airbnb() +
  theme(legend.position = "right",
        legend.text = element_text(size = 11),
        legend.key.height = unit(1.0, "lines"),
        legend.key.width  = unit(1.2, "lines"))
```

For the regression task, cross-validation selects α = `r round(m_enet_r$bestTune$alpha, 3)` and λ = `r round(m_enet_r$bestTune$lambda, 4)`, retaining `r sum(coef(m_enet_r$finalModel, m_enet_r$bestTune$lambda) != 0) - 1` features with non-zero coefficients. In the regularisation path above, trace each feature's trajectory from right (no penalty) to left (heavy penalty). The features that hold on longest (keeping non-zero coefficients even under strong penalisation) are the most reliably predictive of price. Those that flatten to zero early are either redundant given other features or too weakly associated with price to justify their inclusion.

## 8.7 Model R5: Random Forest

```{r model-rf-reg}
set.seed(42)
m_rf_r <- train(log_price ~ ., data = train_reg, method = "ranger",
                trControl = ctrl_reg, tuneLength = 3,
                importance = "impurity", num.trees = 200)
cat("Random Forest — CV RMSE:", round(min(m_rf_r$results$RMSE), 4),
    "| R²:", round(max(m_rf_r$results$Rsquared), 4), "\n")
```

## 8.8 Model R6: XGBoost

```{r model-xgb-reg}
dummies_reg    <- dummyVars(log_price ~ ., data = train_reg)
train_reg_xgb  <- predict(dummies_reg, newdata = train_reg) |> as.data.frame()

# Keep only finite columns (mirrors classification pipeline's good_cols_c pattern)
good_cols_r <- sapply(train_reg_xgb, function(col) all(is.finite(col)))
train_reg_xgb <- train_reg_xgb[, good_cols_r, drop = FALSE]

train_reg_xgb$log_price <- train_reg$log_price

xgb_grid_r <- expand.grid(
  nrounds          = c(100, 200, 300),
  max_depth        = c(3, 5, 7),
  eta              = c(0.01, 0.1),
  gamma            = c(0),
  colsample_bytree = c(0.8),
  min_child_weight = c(1),
  subsample        = c(0.8)
)

set.seed(42)
m_xgb_r <- train(log_price ~ ., data = train_reg_xgb, method = "xgbTree",
                 trControl = ctrl_reg, tuneGrid = xgb_grid_r)
cat("XGBoost — CV RMSE:", round(min(m_xgb_r$results$RMSE), 4),
    "| R²:", round(max(m_xgb_r$results$Rsquared), 4), "\n")
```

## 8.9 Model R7: Generalized Additive Model (GAM)

I spent a lot of time debugging the GAM for regression. The mgcv package kept crashing during knit, which is why I switched to `bam()`. The GAM fits smooth curves to each numeric predictor, which is useful here because the relationship between distance-to-centre and price is unlikely to be perfectly linear.

```{r model-gam-reg}
# Same numeric-only subsetting as classification GAM — see model-gam-class comments.
gam_num_cols_r <- setdiff(
  names(train_reg)[sapply(train_reg, is.numeric)],
  "log_price")
gam_train_r <- train_reg[, c(gam_num_cols_r, "log_price")]

set.seed(42)
m_gam_r <- tryCatch({
  model <- train(log_price ~ ., data = gam_train_r, method = "gam",
                 trControl = ctrl_reg)
  cat("GAM (mgcv, numeric features) — CV RMSE:",
      round(min(model$results$RMSE, na.rm = TRUE), 4),
      "| R²:", round(max(model$results$Rsquared, na.rm = TRUE), 4), "\n")
  model
}, error = function(e) {
  message("mgcv GAM failed (", conditionMessage(e), ") — using LM on numeric features.")
  model <- train(log_price ~ ., data = gam_train_r, method = "lm",
                 trControl = ctrl_reg)
  cat("LM (numeric fallback) — CV RMSE:",
      round(min(model$results$RMSE, na.rm = TRUE), 4),
      "| R²:", round(max(model$results$Rsquared, na.rm = TRUE), 4), "\n")
  model
})
```

### GAM Smooth Terms — Regression

```{r gam-smooth-reg, fig.width=20, fig.height=14}
if (inherits(m_gam_r$finalModel, "gam")) {
  
  gam_obj_r <- m_gam_r$finalModel
  gam_sum_r <- summary(gam_obj_r)
  
  if (is.null(gam_sum_r$s.table)) {
    cat("No smooth table found in regression GAM summary.\n")
    
  } else {
    
    s_tab_r <- as.data.frame(gam_sum_r$s.table)
    s_tab_r$term <- rownames(gam_sum_r$s.table)
    
    # robust edf column detection
    edf_col_r <- grep("^edf", names(s_tab_r), value = TRUE)
    if (length(edf_col_r) == 0) stop("No edf column found in regression GAM smooth table.")
    edf_col_r <- edf_col_r[1]
    
    s_tab_r$edf_value <- s_tab_r[[edf_col_r]]
    
    top_r <- s_tab_r |>
      dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(edf_value)) |>
      dplyr::slice_head(n = min(6, nrow(s_tab_r))) |>
      dplyr::pull(term)
    
    # baseline row: means of numeric predictors used in GAM regression
    # assumes gam_train_r contains predictors + outcome in last col or named log_price
    pred_cols_r <- setdiff(names(gam_train_r), "log_price")
    baseline_row_r <- gam_train_r |>
      dplyr::select(dplyr::all_of(pred_cols_r)) |>
      dplyr::summarise(dplyr::across(dplyr::everything(), ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE)))
    
    smooth_df_r <- purrr::map_df(top_r, function(term_label) {
      
      # parse "s(var)" safely (no regex escaping headaches)
      clean <- term_label
      if (startsWith(clean, "s(") && substring(clean, nchar(clean), nchar(clean)) == ")") {
        clean <- substring(clean, 3, nchar(clean) - 1)
      }
      
      if (!clean %in% names(baseline_row_r)) return(NULL)
      
      x_vec <- gam_train_r[[clean]]
      if (all(is.na(x_vec))) return(NULL)
      
      x_min <- min(x_vec, na.rm = TRUE)
      x_max <- max(x_vec, na.rm = TRUE)
      if (!is.finite(x_min) || !is.finite(x_max) || x_min == x_max) return(NULL)
      
      grid_vals <- seq(x_min, x_max, length.out = 200)
      
      # replicate 1-row baseline to 200 rows, then vary focal predictor
      nd <- baseline_row_r[rep(1, length(grid_vals)), , drop = FALSE]
      nd[[clean]] <- grid_vals
      
      pred <- predict(gam_obj_r, newdata = nd, type = "link", se.fit = TRUE)
      
      edf_here <- s_tab_r |>
        dplyr::filter(term == term_label) |>
        dplyr::pull(edf_value)
      edf_here <- if (length(edf_here) == 0) NA_real_ else edf_here[1]
      
      tibble::tibble(
        x    = grid_vals,
        fit  = as.numeric(pred$fit),
        se   = as.numeric(pred$se.fit),
        term = paste0(clean, " (edf = ", round(edf_here, 2), ")")
      )
    })
    
    if (!is.null(smooth_df_r) && nrow(smooth_df_r) > 0) {
      ggplot2::ggplot(smooth_df_r, ggplot2::aes(x = x, y = fit)) +
        ggplot2::geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "#EAECEE",
                            linewidth = 0.8) +
        ggplot2::geom_ribbon(
          ggplot2::aes(ymin = fit - 2 * se, ymax = fit + 2 * se),
          fill = "#2E86C1", alpha = 0.15
        ) +
        ggplot2::geom_line(colour = "#2E86C1", linewidth = 1.6) +
        ggplot2::facet_wrap(~term, scales = "free_x", ncol = 3) +
        ggplot2::labs(
          title = "GAM Smooth Terms — Regression",
          subtitle = "Partial effect on log(price) · shaded band = ±2 SE · edf > 1 = non-linear",
          x = NULL,
          y = "Partial Effect (log-price)"
        ) +
        theme_airbnb()
    } else {
      cat("No valid regression smooth terms could be plotted.\n")
    }
  }
  
} else {
  cat("GAM fell back to LM on this machine — smooth term plot not applicable.\n")
}


```

## 8.10 Model R8: Kolmogorov-Arnold Network — Regression

<div class="callout-kan">
**KAN for regression** uses the same B-spline architecture as classification, now with MSE loss for continuous log-price prediction (`[n_features, 16, 8, 1]` architecture). Running it on both tasks lets me compare where KAN's flexible function-learning helps more.
</div>

```{r kan-reg-export-preflight }
# Preflight: only check if we actually need to export
if (isTRUE(run_kan_reg_export)) {
  stopifnot("train_reg must exist" = exists("train_reg", inherits = TRUE))
  stopifnot("test_reg must exist"  = exists("test_reg",  inherits = TRUE))
  cat("Preflight OK: train_reg", nrow(train_reg), "rows |",
      "test_reg", nrow(test_reg), "rows\n")
} else {
  cat("KAN regression export disabled — preflight skipped.\n")
}
```


```{r kan-reg-export-for-python}

# Export KAN-ready regression matrices for external Python training
# (stable alternative to reticulate + pykan inside knit)

if (!exists("run_kan_reg_export")) run_kan_reg_export <- FALSE

if (!isTRUE(run_kan_reg_export)) {
  
  cat("KAN regression export skipped (run_kan_reg_export = FALSE).\n")
  cat("Set run_kan_reg_export <- TRUE in setup to regenerate x_kan_reg_train.csv / y_kan_reg_train.csv / x_kan_reg_test.csv.\n")
  
} else {
  
  # Safety checks
  if (!exists("train_reg") || !exists("test_reg")) {
    stop("train_reg/test_reg not found. Run regression setup/prep chunks first.")
  }
  if (!("log_price" %in% names(train_reg)) || !("log_price" %in% names(test_reg))) {
    stop("train_reg/test_reg must contain 'log_price'.")
  }
  
  # 1) Dummy-encode predictors (exclude outcome)
  dum_kan_reg <- caret::dummyVars(
    log_price ~ .,
    data = train_reg,
    fullRank = TRUE,
    na.action = na.pass
  )
  
  x_kan_reg_train <- predict(dum_kan_reg, newdata = train_reg)
  x_kan_reg_test  <- predict(dum_kan_reg, newdata = test_reg)
  y_kan_reg_train <- train_reg$log_price
  
  # 2) Keep only finite columns based on TRAIN (reuse mask for test)
  good_cols_reg <- apply(x_kan_reg_train, 2, function(col) all(is.finite(col)))
  x_kan_reg_train <- x_kan_reg_train[, good_cols_reg, drop = FALSE]
  x_kan_reg_test  <- x_kan_reg_test[,  good_cols_reg, drop = FALSE]
  
  if (ncol(x_kan_reg_train) == 0) {
    stop("KAN regression export matrix has zero valid columns after finite-value filtering.")
  }
  
  # 3) Remove near-zero variance columns (safer for neural nets)
  nzv_idx_reg <- caret::nearZeroVar(as.data.frame(x_kan_reg_train))
  if (length(nzv_idx_reg) > 0) {
    x_kan_reg_train <- x_kan_reg_train[, -nzv_idx_reg, drop = FALSE]
    x_kan_reg_test  <- x_kan_reg_test[,  -nzv_idx_reg, drop = FALSE]
  }
  
  if (ncol(x_kan_reg_train) == 0) {
    stop("KAN regression export matrix has zero columns after near-zero variance filtering.")
  }
  
  # 4) Scale using TRAIN parameters only
  pp_kan_reg <- caret::preProcess(as.data.frame(x_kan_reg_train), method = c("center", "scale"))
  x_kan_reg_train <- predict(pp_kan_reg, as.data.frame(x_kan_reg_train)) |> as.matrix()
  x_kan_reg_test  <- predict(pp_kan_reg, as.data.frame(x_kan_reg_test))  |> as.matrix()
  
  # 5) Post-scaling finite check
  finite_after_scale_reg <- apply(x_kan_reg_train, 2, function(col) all(is.finite(col)))
  if (!all(finite_after_scale_reg)) {
    bad_cols <- colnames(x_kan_reg_train)[!finite_after_scale_reg]
    x_kan_reg_train <- x_kan_reg_train[, finite_after_scale_reg, drop = FALSE]
    x_kan_reg_test  <- x_kan_reg_test[,  finite_after_scale_reg, drop = FALSE]
    cat("Dropped non-finite columns after scaling:", paste(bad_cols, collapse = ", "), "\n")
  }
  
  if (ncol(x_kan_reg_train) == 0) {
    stop("KAN regression export matrix has zero columns after post-scaling finite check.")
  }
  
  # 6) Save CSVs for Python
  readr::write_csv(as.data.frame(x_kan_reg_train), "x_kan_reg_train.csv")
  readr::write_csv(tibble::tibble(y = as.numeric(y_kan_reg_train)), "y_kan_reg_train.csv")
  readr::write_csv(as.data.frame(x_kan_reg_test),  "x_kan_reg_test.csv")
  
  # Optional reproducibility files
  readr::write_csv(
    tibble::tibble(feature = colnames(x_kan_reg_train)),
    "x_kan_reg_feature_names.csv"
  )
  
  kan_reg_meta <- tibble::tibble(
    key = c("n_train", "n_test", "n_features", "target"),
    value = c(
      nrow(x_kan_reg_train),
      nrow(x_kan_reg_test),
      ncol(x_kan_reg_train),
      "log_price"
    )
  )
  readr::write_csv(kan_reg_meta, "kan_reg_export_meta.csv")
  
  cat("KAN regression export files written to:", normalizePath(".", winslash = "/"), "\n")
  cat(" - x_kan_reg_train.csv (", nrow(x_kan_reg_train), " x ", ncol(x_kan_reg_train), ")\n", sep = "")
  cat(" - y_kan_reg_train.csv (", length(y_kan_reg_train), " rows)\n", sep = "")
  cat(" - x_kan_reg_test.csv  (", nrow(x_kan_reg_test), " x ", ncol(x_kan_reg_train), ")\n", sep = "")
  cat(" - x_kan_reg_feature_names.csv (", ncol(x_kan_reg_train), " features)\n", sep = "")
}

```



```{r kan-reg-import-preds}
# Import external KAN regression predictions generated by kan_reg_train.py
# Expected: kan_reg_pred_test.csv with column 'kan_reg_pred_test' on LOG-PRICE scale

kan_reg_pred_path <- "kan_reg_pred_test.csv"
kan_reg_pred_test <- NULL
kan_reg_available <- FALSE
kan_reg_rmse_test <- NA_real_
kan_reg_r2_test   <- NA_real_
kan_reg_mape_test <- NA_real_

if (file.exists(kan_reg_pred_path) && exists("test_reg")) {
  kan_reg_df <- readr::read_csv(kan_reg_pred_path, show_col_types = FALSE)

  if ("kan_reg_pred_test" %in% names(kan_reg_df)) {
    pred_log <- as.numeric(kan_reg_df$kan_reg_pred_test)

    if (length(pred_log) == nrow(test_reg) && all(is.finite(pred_log))) {
      kan_reg_pred_test <- pmax(expm1(pred_log), 0)
      actual_reg        <- expm1(test_reg$log_price)
      keep_mape         <- is.finite(actual_reg) & actual_reg > 0

      kan_reg_rmse_test <- sqrt(mean((actual_reg - kan_reg_pred_test)^2))
      kan_reg_r2_test   <- 1 - sum((actual_reg - kan_reg_pred_test)^2) /
                               sum((actual_reg - mean(actual_reg))^2)
      kan_reg_mape_test <- mean(abs((actual_reg[keep_mape] - kan_reg_pred_test[keep_mape]) /
                                     actual_reg[keep_mape])) * 100
      kan_reg_available <- TRUE

      cat("KAN regression imported:", length(pred_log), "predictions\n")
      cat("  RMSE: $", round(kan_reg_rmse_test, 2),
          " | R²: ", round(kan_reg_r2_test, 4),
          " | MAPE: ", round(kan_reg_mape_test, 1), "%\n", sep = "")
    } else {
      cat("KAN regression skipped: length mismatch (expected", nrow(test_reg),
          "got", length(pred_log), ") or non-finite values.\n")
      cat("Re-run kan_reg_train.py after fresh CSV export to fix.\n")
    }
  }
} else {
  cat("KAN regression predictions not available. Skipping.\n")
}
```


## 8.11 Regression Model Comparison

```{r regression-cv-comparison}
reg_models <- list(
  "Linear Regression"  = m_lm,
  "Stepwise AIC"       = m_step_r,
  "Elastic Net"        = m_enet_r,
  "Random Forest"      = m_rf_r,
  "XGBoost"            = m_xgb_r,
  "GAM"                = m_gam_r
)

reg_resamples <- resamples(reg_models)

summary(reg_resamples)$statistics$RMSE |>
  as.data.frame() |> rownames_to_column("Model") |> arrange(Mean) |>
  styled_kable(digits = 4, caption = "5-Fold CV RMSE (log-price scale): Regression Models",
               col.names = c("Model","Min","Q1","Median","Mean","Q3","Max","NA's"))

summary(reg_resamples)$statistics$Rsquared |>
  as.data.frame() |> rownames_to_column("Model") |> arrange(desc(Mean)) |>
  styled_kable(digits = 4, caption = "5-Fold CV R²: Regression Models",
               col.names = c("Model","Min","Q1","Median","Mean","Q3","Max","NA's"))

as.data.frame(reg_resamples$values) |>
  tidyr::pivot_longer(
    cols = -dplyr::all_of("Resample"),
    names_to = "model_metric",
    values_to = "value"
  ) |>
  tidyr::separate(model_metric, into = c("Model", "Metric"), sep = "~") |>
  dplyr::filter(Metric == "RMSE") |>
  ggplot2::ggplot(ggplot2::aes(
    x = reorder(Model, value, FUN = median),
    y = value,
    fill = Model
  )) +
  ggplot2::geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.8, outlier.alpha = 0.35, outlier.size = 2.5,
                        colour = "#5D6D7E", width = 0.65, linewidth = 0.7) +
  ggplot2::coord_flip() +
  ggplot2::scale_fill_manual(values = pal_blue) +
  ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::number_format(accuracy = 0.0001)) +
  ggplot2::labs(
    title = "5-Fold CV RMSE Distribution by Model",
    subtitle = "Caret-trained models · box = IQR · line = median · ordered by median RMSE (lower = better)",
    x = NULL,
    y = "RMSE (log-price scale, lower is better)"
  ) +
  theme_airbnb() +
  ggplot2::theme(legend.position = "none",
                 panel.grid.major.y = element_blank())
```

### Pairwise Significance Tests — Regression CV

As with classification, we test whether the RMSE differences between regression models are statistically significant or just fold-to-fold noise. Paired t-tests with Holm correction tell us which models are genuinely different.

```{r reg-cv-significance}
# Pairwise significance tests — Regression CV (version-robust)
# Uses paired t-tests on fold-level RMSE values from reg_resamples$values

cv_vals_reg <- as.data.frame(reg_resamples$values)
rmse_cols <- grep("~RMSE$", names(cv_vals_reg), value = TRUE)

if (length(rmse_cols) < 2) {
  stop("Need at least two RMSE columns in reg_resamples$values to run pairwise tests.")
}

rmse_df <- cv_vals_reg[, c("Resample", rmse_cols), drop = FALSE]
clean_model_names_reg <- sub("~RMSE$", "", rmse_cols)
names(rmse_df)[match(rmse_cols, names(rmse_df))] <- clean_model_names_reg

pair_idx_reg <- utils::combn(clean_model_names_reg, 2, simplify = FALSE)

pairwise_reg_tbl <- purrr::map_dfr(pair_idx_reg, function(pair) {
  m1 <- pair[1]
  m2 <- pair[2]
  
  x <- rmse_df[[m1]]
  y <- rmse_df[[m2]]
  
  keep <- is.finite(x) & is.finite(y)
  x <- x[keep]
  y <- y[keep]
  
  if (length(x) < 2) {
    return(tibble::tibble(
      Comparison = paste0(m1, " - ", m2),
      Mean_Diff  = NA_real_,
      p_value    = NA_real_
    ))
  }
  
  tt <- stats::t.test(x, y, paired = TRUE)
  
  tibble::tibble(
    Comparison = paste0(m1, " - ", m2),
    Mean_Diff  = mean(x - y),   # negative => first model has lower RMSE (better)
    p_value    = tt$p.value
  )
})

pairwise_reg_tbl |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    p_value_holm = stats::p.adjust(p_value, method = "holm"),
    Direction    = dplyr::if_else(Mean_Diff < 0, "Row better", "Col better"),
    Significant  = dplyr::if_else(!is.na(p_value_holm) & p_value_holm < 0.05, "Yes", ""),
    Mean_Diff    = round(Mean_Diff, 6),
    p_value      = round(p_value, 4),
    p_value_holm = round(p_value_holm, 4)
  ) |>
  dplyr::arrange(p_value_holm, p_value) |>
  dplyr::select(Comparison, Mean_Diff, Direction, p_value, p_value_holm, Significant) |>
  styled_kable(
    caption = "Pairwise paired t-test on 5-Fold CV RMSE: Regression Models (Holm-adjusted p-values; negative ΔRMSE = row model better)",
    col.names = c("Comparison (row − col)", "Mean ΔRMSE", "Direction", "p-value", "Holm p-value", "Holm p < 0.05")
  )
```

## 8.12 Test Set Performance (Dollar Scale)

```{r regression-test-performance}
eval_reg_model <- function(model, test_data, model_name) {
  pred_log    <- as.numeric(predict(model, newdata = test_data))
  actual_log  <- as.numeric(test_data$log_price)

  pred_orig   <- pmax(expm1(pred_log), 0)   # clamp negative back-transformed values
  actual_orig <- pmax(expm1(actual_log), 0)

  mae  <- mean(abs(actual_orig - pred_orig))
  rmse <- sqrt(mean((actual_orig - pred_orig)^2))
  r2   <- 1 - sum((actual_orig - pred_orig)^2) / sum((actual_orig - mean(actual_orig))^2)

  # Safe MAPE (avoid divide-by-zero)
  keep_mape <- is.finite(actual_orig) & actual_orig > 0
  mape <- mean(abs((actual_orig[keep_mape] - pred_orig[keep_mape]) / actual_orig[keep_mape])) * 100

  list(
    model = model_name,
    mae = mae, rmse = rmse, r2 = r2, mape = mape,
    predictions = pred_orig, actual = actual_orig
  )
}

# XGBoost test matrix (apply same good_cols_r mask as training)
test_reg_xgb <- predict(dummies_reg, newdata = test_reg) |> as.data.frame()
test_reg_xgb <- test_reg_xgb[, good_cols_r, drop = FALSE]
test_reg_xgb$log_price <- test_reg$log_price

# Core models
test_results_reg <- list(
  eval_reg_model(m_lm,     test_reg,     "Linear Regression"),
  eval_reg_model(m_step_r, test_reg,     "Stepwise (AIC)"),
  eval_reg_model(m_enet_r, test_reg,     "Elastic Net"),
  eval_reg_model(m_rf_r,   test_reg,     "Random Forest"),
  eval_reg_model(m_xgb_r,  test_reg_xgb, "XGBoost"),
  eval_reg_model(m_gam_r,  test_reg[, c(gam_num_cols_r, "log_price")], "GAM")
)
# Add PCR and PLS (use test_pls_df prepared in model-pcr-pls-reg chunk)
pcr_pred_log  <- as.numeric(predict(m_pcr, newdata = test_pls_df, ncomp = ncomp_pcr))
pcr_pred_orig <- pmax(expm1(pcr_pred_log), 0)
pcr_actual    <- pmax(expm1(test_reg$log_price), 0)
keep_pcr      <- is.finite(pcr_actual) & pcr_actual > 0
test_results_reg[[length(test_results_reg) + 1]] <- list(
  model       = "PCR",
  mae         = mean(abs(pcr_actual - pcr_pred_orig)),
  rmse        = sqrt(mean((pcr_actual - pcr_pred_orig)^2)),
  r2          = 1 - sum((pcr_actual - pcr_pred_orig)^2) / sum((pcr_actual - mean(pcr_actual))^2),
  mape        = mean(abs((pcr_actual[keep_pcr] - pcr_pred_orig[keep_pcr]) / pcr_actual[keep_pcr])) * 100,
  predictions = pcr_pred_orig,
  actual      = pcr_actual
)
# PLS predictions
pls_pred_log  <- as.numeric(predict(m_pls, newdata = test_pls_df, ncomp = ncomp_pls))
pls_pred_orig <- pmax(expm1(pls_pred_log), 0)
test_results_reg[[length(test_results_reg) + 1]] <- list(
  model       = "PLS",
  mae         = mean(abs(pcr_actual - pls_pred_orig)),
  rmse        = sqrt(mean((pcr_actual - pls_pred_orig)^2)),
  r2          = 1 - sum((pcr_actual - pls_pred_orig)^2) / sum((pcr_actual - mean(pcr_actual))^2),
  mape        = mean(abs((pcr_actual[keep_pcr] - pls_pred_orig[keep_pcr]) / pcr_actual[keep_pcr])) * 100,
  predictions = pls_pred_orig,
  actual      = pcr_actual
)
# Add KAN regression if available (already on dollar scale from your import chunk)
if (exists("kan_reg_available") && isTRUE(kan_reg_available) &&
    exists("kan_reg_pred_test") && !is.null(kan_reg_pred_test)) {
  
  act_r_orig <- pmax(expm1(test_reg$log_price), 0)

  keep_mape <- is.finite(act_r_orig) & act_r_orig > 0
  
  test_results_reg[[length(test_results_reg) + 1]] <- list(
    model       = "KAN",
    mae         = mean(abs(act_r_orig - kan_reg_pred_test)),
    rmse        = sqrt(mean((act_r_orig - kan_reg_pred_test)^2)),
    r2          = 1 - sum((act_r_orig - kan_reg_pred_test)^2) /
                      sum((act_r_orig - mean(act_r_orig))^2),
    mape        = mean(abs((act_r_orig[keep_mape] - kan_reg_pred_test[keep_mape]) / act_r_orig[keep_mape])) * 100,
    predictions = kan_reg_pred_test,
    actual      = act_r_orig
  )
}

# Final leaderboard table
test_perf_reg <- purrr::map_df(test_results_reg, ~{
  tibble::tibble(
    Model = .x$model,
    MAE   = .x$mae,
    RMSE  = .x$rmse,
    R2    = .x$r2,
    MAPE  = .x$mape
  )
}) |>
  dplyr::arrange(RMSE)

# Bold best regression model
best_reg_model_name <- test_perf_reg$Model[1]  # sorted by RMSE ascending

test_perf_reg |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    dplyr::across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 2)),
    Model = dplyr::if_else(Model == best_reg_model_name,
                           paste0("**", Model, "**"), Model),
    MAE  = paste0("$", MAE),
    RMSE = paste0("$", RMSE),
    MAPE = paste0(MAPE, "%")
  ) |>
  styled_kable(caption = "Test Set Performance — Regression Models (Original Dollar Scale; best model bolded; bootstrap 95% CI for best RMSE reported below)")

# Bootstrap 95% CI for best model RMSE
best_reg_idx  <- which.min(purrr::map_dbl(test_results_reg, "rmse"))
best_reg_name <- test_results_reg[[best_reg_idx]]$model
b_preds       <- test_results_reg[[best_reg_idx]]$predictions
b_acts        <- test_results_reg[[best_reg_idx]]$actual

set.seed(42)
boot_rmse_r <- replicate(2000, {
  idx_b <- sample(seq_along(b_preds), replace = TRUE)
  sqrt(mean((b_acts[idx_b] - b_preds[idx_b])^2))
})

cat("\nBootstrap 95% CI — Best Regressor RMSE (", best_reg_name, "):\n",
    "  [$", round(stats::quantile(boot_rmse_r, 0.025), 2), ", $",
    round(stats::quantile(boot_rmse_r, 0.975), 2), "]\n", sep = "")

# Pairwise bootstrap test on test-set squared errors (best vs all others)
best_reg_se <- (b_acts - b_preds)^2
reg_pairwise_pvals <- sapply(seq_along(test_results_reg), function(i) {
  if (i == best_reg_idx) return(NA_real_)
  other_preds <- test_results_reg[[i]]$predictions
  other_acts  <- test_results_reg[[i]]$actual
  other_se    <- (other_acts - other_preds)^2
  tryCatch({
    tt <- stats::t.test(other_se, best_reg_se, paired = TRUE)
    tt$p.value
  }, error = function(e) NA_real_)
})
names(reg_pairwise_pvals) <- sapply(test_results_reg, function(x) x$model)
reg_pairwise_holm <- stats::p.adjust(reg_pairwise_pvals, method = "holm")

test_perf_reg_sig <- test_perf_reg |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    Sig = dplyr::case_when(
      Model == best_reg_model_name ~ "---",
      !is.na(reg_pairwise_holm[Model]) & reg_pairwise_holm[Model] < 0.01 ~ "**",
      !is.na(reg_pairwise_holm[Model]) & reg_pairwise_holm[Model] < 0.05 ~ "*",
      TRUE ~ ""
    )
  )

cat("\nPairwise paired t-test on test-set squared errors (Holm-adjusted, vs best):\n")
for (m in names(reg_pairwise_holm)) {
  if (!is.na(reg_pairwise_holm[m])) {
    cat("  ", m, ": p =", round(reg_pairwise_holm[m], 4), "\n")
  }
}
```

### Interpretation — What Drives Nightly Price?

The best regression model explains roughly 57% of price variation using listing characteristics alone. Given that we have no demand-side data (no booking rates, seasonal calendars, or competitor pricing), I think that is a reasonable result. The remaining 43% probably comes from demand shocks, host-specific pricing strategies, seasonal patterns, and quality factors we cannot measure (interior design, photo quality, etc.).

A few patterns show up across all the models:

1. **Location is the strongest price driver.** City, neighbourhood median price, and distance to the centre collectively explain the largest share of price variation. Amsterdam listings command a clear premium over Madrid and Barcelona, which makes sense given the higher cost of living and tighter rental regulations. Within each city, proximity to tourist centres amplifies prices non-linearly.

2. **Property size defines the price tiers.** The number of guests a listing accommodates, bedroom count, and room type (entire home vs. private room) create the broad pricing bands. The GAM smooth terms show that each additional bedroom adds a diminishing percentage premium rather than a fixed dollar amount, which is my main reason for modelling prices on the log scale, and the smooth terms confirm it was the right call.

3. **Listing presentation adds marginal value.** Description quality, name length, and luxury keywords contribute to pricing beyond physical attributes. Hosts who invest in their listing copy charge noticeably more, though causality runs both ways since more sophisticated hosts likely write better descriptions *and* price more strategically.

4. **The gap between linear and non-linear models is informative.** OLS achieves roughly R² = 0.45, and Random Forest lifts this to about 0.57. That 12-point gain quantifies the value of capturing interactions and non-linearities. The fact that the gain is more modest than in classification suggests that much of the pricing structure is genuinely linear on the log scale.

```{r regression-actual-vs-predicted}
best_reg_idx  <- which.min(purrr::map_dbl(test_results_reg, "rmse"))
best_reg_name <- test_results_reg[[best_reg_idx]]$model

plot_data <- tibble::tibble(
  Actual    = as.numeric(test_results_reg[[best_reg_idx]]$actual),
  Predicted = as.numeric(test_results_reg[[best_reg_idx]]$predictions)
) |>
  dplyr::filter(is.finite(Actual), is.finite(Predicted)) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    # log10 scales cannot take 0 or negatives
    Actual_plot    = pmax(Actual, 1e-6),
    Predicted_plot = pmax(Predicted, 1e-6)
  )

ggplot2::ggplot(plot_data, ggplot2::aes(x = Actual_plot, y = Predicted_plot)) +
  ggplot2::geom_abline(
    slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed",
    colour = "#95A5A6", linewidth = 1.0
  ) +
  ggplot2::geom_point(alpha = 0.15, colour = "#2E86C1", size = 2.0, shape = 16) +
  ggplot2::geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, colour = "#E74C3C", linewidth = 1.6) +
  ggplot2::scale_x_log10(labels = scales::dollar) +
  ggplot2::scale_y_log10(labels = scales::dollar) +
  ggplot2::coord_fixed() +
  ggplot2::labs(
    title = paste("Actual vs Predicted Price —", best_reg_name),
    subtitle = paste0(
      "Test R² = ", round(test_results_reg[[best_reg_idx]]$r2, 3),
      " | RMSE = $", round(test_results_reg[[best_reg_idx]]$rmse, 2),
      " | MAPE = ", round(test_results_reg[[best_reg_idx]]$mape, 1), "%",
      " · grey dashed = perfect prediction · red = linear fit"
    ),
    x = "Actual Price (USD, log scale)",
    y = "Predicted Price (USD, log scale)"
  ) +
  theme_airbnb() +
  ggplot2::theme(aspect.ratio = 1)

 
```

### 8.12.1 Regression Calibration — Predicted vs Actual (Binned)

```{r regression-calibration, fig.width=18, fig.height=11}
# Binned calibration: do predicted prices match actual prices on average?
best_reg_cal_preds <- test_results_reg[[best_reg_idx]]$predictions
best_reg_cal_acts  <- test_results_reg[[best_reg_idx]]$actual

cal_reg_df <- tibble::tibble(
  predicted = best_reg_cal_preds,
  actual    = best_reg_cal_acts
) |>
  dplyr::mutate(decile = dplyr::ntile(predicted, 10)) |>
  dplyr::group_by(decile) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    mean_predicted = mean(predicted),
    mean_actual    = mean(actual),
    sd_actual      = sd(actual),
    n              = dplyr::n(),
    .groups        = "drop"
  ) |>
  dplyr::mutate(se = sd_actual / sqrt(n))

ggplot(cal_reg_df, aes(x = mean_predicted, y = mean_actual)) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "#BDC3C7", linewidth = 1.0) +
  annotate("text", x = max(cal_reg_df$mean_predicted) * 0.85,
           y = max(cal_reg_df$mean_predicted) * 0.78,
           label = "Perfect\ncalibration", colour = "#BDC3C7", size = 5,
           fontface = "italic", angle = 38) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = mean_actual - 1.96 * se, ymax = mean_actual + 1.96 * se),
                width = 0, colour = "#2E86C1", linewidth = 0.7, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_point(size = 6.5, colour = "#1B4F72", stroke = 0.8) +
  geom_line(colour = "#1B4F72", linewidth = 1.3, alpha = 0.6) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::dollar) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::dollar) +
  labs(
    title    = paste("Regression Calibration —", best_reg_name),
    subtitle = "Decile-binned: mean predicted vs mean actual price · error bars = 95% CI",
    x = "Mean Predicted Price (USD)", y = "Mean Actual Price (USD)"
  ) +
  theme_airbnb() +
  theme(aspect.ratio = 1)
```

## 8.13 SHAP Values — XGBoost Regression

```{r shap-reg, fig.width=20, fig.height=12}


set.seed(42)

# Predictor matrix used for xgboost training (exclude outcome)
X_train_r <- as.matrix(train_reg_xgb[, setdiff(names(train_reg_xgb), "log_price"), drop = FALSE])

# Sample rows
shap_idx_r <- sample(seq_len(nrow(X_train_r)), min(5000, nrow(X_train_r)))
X_pred_shap_r <- X_train_r[shap_idx_r, , drop = FALSE]

# Force column names to match booster expectations
xgb_feat_names_r <- m_xgb_r$finalModel$feature_names

if (is.null(xgb_feat_names_r)) {
  stop("m_xgb_r$finalModel has no feature_names.")
}

if (length(xgb_feat_names_r) != ncol(X_pred_shap_r)) {
  stop("Feature count mismatch: booster has ", length(xgb_feat_names_r),
       " names, but X_pred has ", ncol(X_pred_shap_r), " columns.")
}

colnames(X_pred_shap_r) <- xgb_feat_names_r

sv_r <- shapviz::shapviz(
  m_xgb_r$finalModel,
  X_pred = X_pred_shap_r
)

shapviz::sv_importance(sv_r, kind = "beeswarm", max_display = 20) +
  ggplot2::labs(
    title = "SHAP Beeswarm — XGBoost (Regression)",
    subtitle = "Each dot = one observation · colour = feature value (low → high) · x = SHAP impact on log-price"
  ) +
  theme_airbnb() +
  ggplot2::theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
                 legend.position    = "right",
                 legend.key.height  = unit(3.0, "cm"),
                 legend.key.width   = unit(0.6, "cm"),
                 legend.text   = element_text(size = 14),
                 legend.title  = element_text(size = 15, angle = 0),
                 legend.margin = margin(t = 8, b = 8, l = 12, r = 8))




```

The regression SHAP beeswarm looks very different from the classification one. Here, **physical capacity dominates**: `accommodates` and `bedrooms` are the top drivers, with high values consistently pushing predicted price upward. Bigger listings cost more, and that is the single strongest signal.

**Location is next.** `neigh_median_price` ranks near the top because listings in expensive neighbourhoods are priced accordingly. `dist_to_center` shows the classic urban rent gradient, with closer to the centre meaning higher SHAP contributions.

**Room type captures the market's basic segmentation.** Private rooms and shared rooms get large negative SHAP values relative to entire homes (the reference category), quantifying the discount that guests expect.

Looking at these results, my main takeaway is what *does not* appear at the top. Host behaviour features (`host_response_rate`, `host_acceptance_rate`), which dominated the classification task, are near the bottom here. Price depends on what the listing is and where it is, not on how the host behaves. This contrast between the two tasks is one of the project's main findings.

```{r shap-reg-dependence, fig.width=22, fig.height=10}
top_shap_r <- names(sort(colMeans(abs(sv_r$S)), decreasing = TRUE))[1:3]
shap_dep_r <- map(top_shap_r, ~{
  sv_dependence(sv_r, v = .x) +
    labs(title = .x, subtitle = NULL) +
    theme_airbnb(base_size = 14) +
    theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 15, face = "bold"),
          legend.key.height = unit(0.3, "cm"),
          legend.key.width  = unit(1.8, "cm"),
          legend.text  = element_text(size = 9),
          legend.title = element_text(size = 10),
          legend.direction = "horizontal",
          legend.position = "bottom") +
    guides(colour = guide_colourbar(barwidth = 8, barheight = 0.5,
                                     title.position = "left", title.vjust = 0.8,
                                     label.theme = element_text(size = 9)))
})
wrap_plots(shap_dep_r, ncol = 3) +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "SHAP Dependence — Top 3 Regression Predictors",
    subtitle = "Colour = interaction variable · each point = one observation",
    theme = theme(plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", colour = "#1B4F72", size = 18),
                  plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "#5D6D7E", size = 13))
  )
```

## 8.14 Regression Model Stacking

```{r stacking-regression}
get_oof_reg <- function(model_obj) {
  oof <- model_obj$pred
  if (is.null(oof) || !"pred" %in% names(oof)) return(NULL)
  oof[order(oof$rowIndex), "pred", drop = TRUE]
}

oof_lm_r  <- get_oof_reg(m_lm)
oof_en_r  <- get_oof_reg(m_enet_r)
oof_rf_r  <- get_oof_reg(m_rf_r)
oof_xgb_r <- get_oof_reg(m_xgb_r)
oof_gam_r <- get_oof_reg(m_gam_r)

oof_lengths_r <- c(length(oof_lm_r), length(oof_en_r),
                   length(oof_rf_r), length(oof_xgb_r), length(oof_gam_r))
valid_r <- oof_lengths_r[oof_lengths_r > 0]
oof_n_r <- if (length(valid_r) > 0) min(valid_r) else 0L

# Verify all OOF vectors have identical length (critical for correct alignment)
if (length(unique(valid_r)) > 1) {
  stop("OOF prediction lengths differ across models: ",
       paste(names(valid_r), "=", valid_r, collapse=", "),
       ". Cannot safely build stacking meta-features.")
}

if (oof_n_r > 0) {
  mu_lp <- mean(train_reg$log_price)
  meta_reg_df <- data.frame(
    lm  = if (!is.null(oof_lm_r))  oof_lm_r[1:oof_n_r]  else rep(mu_lp, oof_n_r),
    en  = if (!is.null(oof_en_r))  oof_en_r[1:oof_n_r]  else rep(mu_lp, oof_n_r),
    rf  = if (!is.null(oof_rf_r))  oof_rf_r[1:oof_n_r]  else rep(mu_lp, oof_n_r),
    xgb = if (!is.null(oof_xgb_r)) oof_xgb_r[1:oof_n_r] else rep(mu_lp, oof_n_r),
    gam = if (!is.null(oof_gam_r)) oof_gam_r[1:oof_n_r] else rep(mu_lp, oof_n_r),
    y   = train_reg$log_price[1:oof_n_r]
  )
  meta_reg_model <- lm(y ~ ., data = meta_reg_df)

  # Test predictions (same column order as meta_reg_df)
  test_meta_reg <- data.frame(
    lm  = predict(m_lm,     test_reg),
    en  = predict(m_enet_r, test_reg),
    rf  = predict(m_rf_r,   test_reg),
    xgb = predict(m_xgb_r,  test_reg_xgb),
    gam = predict(m_gam_r,  test_reg[, gam_num_cols_r])
  )
  stack_pred_log  <- predict(meta_reg_model, test_meta_reg)
  stack_pred_orig <- expm1(stack_pred_log)
  actual_orig_r   <- expm1(test_reg$log_price)
  stack_rmse <- sqrt(mean((actual_orig_r - stack_pred_orig)^2))
  stack_r2   <- 1 - sum((actual_orig_r - stack_pred_orig)^2)/
                    sum((actual_orig_r - mean(actual_orig_r))^2)
  cat("Stacked Ensemble — Test RMSE: $", round(stack_rmse, 2),
      " | R²:", round(stack_r2, 4), "\n")
  cat("Meta-learner weights (OLS coefficients):\n"); print(round(coef(meta_reg_model), 4))

  # Normalize regression stacking weights for interpretation
  raw_w <- coef(meta_reg_model)[-1]  # exclude intercept
  norm_w <- raw_w / sum(abs(raw_w))
  cat("\nNormalized regression stacking weights (proportion of total allocation):\n")
  print(round(norm_w, 3))

  cat("\nThe OLS meta-learner's coefficients show how much weight each base model receives.\n")
  cat("Higher absolute weight = more unique predictive signal. A negative weight means\n")
  cat("the model's errors offset the others' — it acts as a correction term.\n")
  cat("A non-zero intercept means the meta-learner corrects systematic bias in the base predictions.\n")
} else {
  cat("OOF not available for stacking.\n")
  stack_rmse <- NA_real_; stack_r2 <- NA_real_
}
```

## 8.15 Regression Interpretation

```{r regression-coefficients}
lm_coef <- summary(m_lm$finalModel)$coefficients |>
  as.data.frame() |>
  rownames_to_column("Variable") |>
  rename(SE = `Std. Error`, T_stat = `t value`, P = `Pr(>|t|)`) |>
  filter(Variable != "(Intercept)") |>
  mutate(Pct_effect = (exp(Estimate) - 1) * 100,
         Significant = if_else(P < 0.05, "***", "")) |>
  arrange(desc(abs(Estimate))) |>
  slice_head(n = 15) |>
  dplyr::select(Variable, Estimate, Pct_effect, SE, P, Significant)

lm_coef |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~round(., 4)),
         Pct_effect = paste0(round(Pct_effect, 1), "%")) |>
  styled_kable(caption = "Top 15 Coefficients — Linear Regression (Log Price Scale)",
               col.names = c("Variable","Estimate","~% Effect on Price","SE","p-value","Sig."))
```

Because the target is `log(price + 1)`, each OLS coefficient translates to an approximate percentage change in nightly price for a one-unit increase in the predictor, computed as `(exp(beta) - 1) * 100`. The model explains roughly `r round(max(m_lm$results$Rsquared) * 100)`% of log-price variance (CV R² = `r round(max(m_lm$results$Rsquared), 3)`).

The largest coefficients belong to `room_type` dummies: private rooms and shared rooms are priced well below entire homes (the reference category), reflecting the platform's basic market segmentation. Among continuous features, `accommodates` has the strongest positive effect: each additional guest capacity is associated with roughly `r round((exp(coef(m_lm$finalModel)["accommodates"]) - 1) * 100, 1)`% higher nightly price. `neigh_median_price` also ranks highly, confirming that a listing's price is anchored to what nearby competitors charge.

```{r regression-vip}

# Refit ranger directly using best hyperparameters to guarantee variable importance extraction
bt_r <- m_rf_r$bestTune

rf_final_r <- ranger::ranger(
  log_price ~ ., data = train_reg,
  num.trees = 200,
  mtry = bt_r$mtry,
  min.node.size = bt_r$min.node.size,
  splitrule = as.character(bt_r$splitrule),
  importance = "impurity",
  seed = 42
)

vi_r <- sort(rf_final_r$variable.importance, decreasing = TRUE)

vi_df_r <- tibble::tibble(
  Variable = names(vi_r),
  Importance = as.numeric(vi_r)
) |>
  dplyr::slice_head(n = 20)

ggplot2::ggplot(vi_df_r, ggplot2::aes(x = reorder(Variable, Importance), y = Importance, fill = Importance)) +
  ggplot2::geom_col(show.legend = FALSE, width = 0.75) +
  ggplot2::coord_flip() +
  ggplot2::scale_fill_gradient(low = "#D6EAF8", high = "#1B4F72") +
  ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))) +
  ggplot2::labs(
    title = "Variable Importance — Random Forest (Regression)",
    subtitle = "Variance reduction · top 20 predictors of log(Price)",
    x = NULL, y = "Importance (variance reduction)"
  ) +
  theme_airbnb() +
  ggplot2::theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_blank())

```

```{r regression-pdp-ice, fig.width=20, fig.height=14}
top_reg_feats  <- c("accommodates","dist_to_center","bedrooms","neigh_median_price")
set.seed(42)
train_reg_samp <- train_reg |> slice_sample(n = min(2000, nrow(train_reg)))

ice_reg <- map(top_reg_feats, ~{
  pdp_ice_r <- partial(m_rf_r, pred.var = .x, train = train_reg_samp,
                       ice = TRUE, center = TRUE)
  pdp_avg_r <- pdp_ice_r |>
    group_by(across(all_of(.x))) |>
    summarise(yhat = mean(yhat), .groups = "drop")

  ggplot() +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "#EAECEE", linewidth = 1.0) +
    geom_line(data = pdp_ice_r,
              aes(x = .data[[.x]], y = yhat, group = yhat.id),
              colour = "#2E86C1", alpha = 0.04, linewidth = 0.35) +
    geom_line(data = pdp_avg_r,
              aes(x = .data[[.x]], y = yhat),
              colour = "#E74C3C", linewidth = 2.2) +
    labs(title = paste("ICE + PDP:", .x),
         subtitle = "Faint lines = individual ICE · red bold = PDP average (centered)",
         y = "Δ log(Price)") +
    theme_airbnb()
})

grid.arrange(grobs = ice_reg, ncol = 2,
             top = textGrob("Centered ICE + PDP — Top 4 Price Predictors (Random Forest)",
                            gp = gpar(fontface = "bold", fontsize = 22, col = "#1B4F72")))
```

## 8.16 Residual Diagnostics

```{r regression-residuals, fig.width=20, fig.height=14}
best_res   <- test_results_reg[[best_reg_idx]]$actual - test_results_reg[[best_reg_idx]]$predictions
best_fit   <- test_results_reg[[best_reg_idx]]$predictions
std_resid  <- best_res / sd(best_res)

p_r1 <- ggplot(data.frame(Fitted = best_fit, Residuals = best_res),
               aes(x = Fitted, y = Residuals)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, colour = "#1B4F72", linewidth = 1.0, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.18, colour = "#2E86C1", size = 2.0, shape = 16) +
  geom_smooth(colour = "#E74C3C", se = TRUE, fill = "#E74C3C", alpha = 0.08, linewidth = 1.4) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = dollar) +
  labs(title = "Residuals vs Fitted", subtitle = "Red = LOESS trend · should be flat at zero",
       x = "Fitted (USD, log)", y = "Residuals (USD)") +
  theme_airbnb()

p_r2 <- ggplot(data.frame(Residuals = best_res), aes(x = Residuals)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), bins = 60, fill = "#2E86C1",
                 alpha = 0.7, colour = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
  geom_density(colour = "#E74C3C", linewidth = 1.4, adjust = 1.5) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, colour = "#1B4F72", linewidth = 1.0, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(title = "Residual Distribution", subtitle = "Red = kernel density estimate",
       x = "Residuals (USD)", y = "Density") +
  theme_airbnb()

p_r3 <- ggplot(data.frame(Residuals = best_res), aes(sample = Residuals)) +
  stat_qq(colour = "#2E86C1", alpha = 0.3, size = 2.0, shape = 16) +
  stat_qq_line(colour = "#E74C3C", linewidth = 1.4) +
  labs(title = "Normal Q-Q Plot", subtitle = "Deviations in tails indicate heavy-tailed residuals",
       x = "Theoretical Quantiles", y = "Sample Quantiles") +
  theme_airbnb()

p_r4 <- ggplot(data.frame(Fitted = best_fit, Sqrt_Std = sqrt(abs(std_resid))),
               aes(x = Fitted, y = Sqrt_Std)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.18, colour = "#2E86C1", size = 2.0, shape = 16) +
  geom_smooth(colour = "#E74C3C", se = TRUE, fill = "#E74C3C", alpha = 0.08, linewidth = 1.4) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = dollar) +
  labs(title = "Scale-Location", subtitle = "Flat LOESS = constant variance (homoscedasticity)",
       x = "Fitted (USD, log)", y = "√|Standardised Residuals|") +
  theme_airbnb()

(p_r1 + p_r2) / (p_r3 + p_r4) +
  plot_annotation(
    title = paste("Residual Diagnostics —", best_reg_name),
    theme = theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", colour = "#1B4F72", size = 20))
  )

```

These four panels give a health check on the model's assumptions. The **Residuals vs Fitted** plot should look like a random cloud centred at zero. Any systematic curve would mean the model's functional form is wrong, and a fan shape would mean prediction errors grow with price. The **Q-Q plot** checks whether residuals are approximately normal: the central values should follow the line closely, with tails deviating where the model struggles with extreme listings. The **Scale-Location** panel tests whether variance is constant. A flat LOESS line means the log transformation has done its job stabilising variance; an upward slope at the right edge would indicate that expensive listings are harder to predict. Finally, the **histogram** shows the overall shape of prediction errors. Any right skew points to systematic underprediction of a subset of listings, most likely premium properties that depend on features my data does not capture (interior design, photo quality, unique character).

## 8.17 Price Tier Analysis

I wanted to check whether the model performs equally well across different price ranges. Listings are segmented into four tiers (Budget, Mid-Range, Premium, and Luxury) based on actual price quartiles, and I evaluate the best regression model within each tier separately.

```{r price-tier-analysis, fig.width=20, fig.height=14}
price_quantiles <- quantile(test_reg$log_price |> expm1(),
                            probs = c(0, 0.25, 0.60, 0.85, 1))

tier_df <- data.frame(
  actual    = test_results_reg[[best_reg_idx]]$actual,
  predicted = test_results_reg[[best_reg_idx]]$predictions
) |>
  mutate(
    tier = cut(actual,
               breaks = price_quantiles,
               labels = c("Budget","Mid-Range","Premium","Luxury"),
               include.lowest = TRUE),
    error    = predicted - actual,
    abs_err  = abs(error),
    pct_err  = abs(error) / actual * 100
  )

# Per-tier error summary
tier_summary <- tier_df |>
  group_by(tier) |>
  summarise(
    n         = n(),
    Median_Price = median(actual),
    MAE       = mean(abs_err),
    RMSE      = sqrt(mean(error^2)),
    MAPE      = mean(pct_err),
    Bias      = mean(error),
    .groups   = "drop"
  )

tier_summary |>
  mutate(across(c(Median_Price, MAE, RMSE, Bias), ~paste0("$", round(., 0))),
         MAPE = paste0(round(MAPE, 1), "%"),
         n    = format(n, big.mark = ",")) |>
  styled_kable(caption = paste("Prediction Error by Price Tier —", best_reg_name))

# Actual vs Predicted by tier
p_tier1 <- ggplot(tier_df, aes(x = actual, y = predicted, colour = tier)) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "#BDC3C7",
              linewidth = 1.0) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.2, size = 2.0, shape = 16) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = dollar) + scale_y_log10(labels = dollar) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("Budget"="#2E86C1","Mid-Range"="#F39C12",
                                  "Premium"="#1B4F72","Luxury"="#8E44AD")) +
  facet_wrap(~tier, scales = "free") +
  labs(title = "Actual vs Predicted by Price Tier",
       subtitle = "Dashed line = perfect prediction · each panel on its own scale",
       x = "Actual Price (USD)", y = "Predicted Price (USD)") +
  theme_airbnb() + theme(legend.position = "none")

p_tier2 <- ggplot(tier_df, aes(x = tier, y = abs_err, fill = tier)) +
  geom_violin(alpha = 0.55, colour = NA, trim = FALSE, scale = "width") +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.1, outlier.alpha = 0.15, outlier.size = 1.5,
               colour = "#2C3E50", fill = "white", alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_y_log10(labels = dollar) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Budget"="#2E86C1","Mid-Range"="#F39C12",
                                "Premium"="#1B4F72","Luxury"="#8E44AD")) +
  labs(title = "Absolute Error Distribution by Price Tier",
       subtitle = "Violin = density shape · boxplot = IQR + median",
       x = NULL, y = "Absolute Error (USD, log scale)") +
  theme_airbnb() +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        panel.grid.major.x = element_blank())

p_tier1 / p_tier2
```

<div class="callout-insight">
Luxury listings (the top 15% by price) are consistently the hardest to predict. This is not surprising since at the high end, pricing depends on things our data cannot measure: interior design, photo quality, micro-location prestige, and the host's personal brand. For budget and mid-range listings, the predictions are much more accurate and practically useful.
</div>

---

# 9. Advanced Analysis

With the core classification and regression pipelines complete, I now push the analysis further. The questions here are: do models generalise across cities? Which listings are anomalies? Would more data help? Do different model families agree on which features matter? And can I construct counterfactual scenarios that translate predictions into actionable guidance?

## 9.1 City-Stratified Model Generalization

My models were trained on listings from all three cities pooled together. But does that shared model work equally well everywhere, or does it favour one city's patterns over another's? To answer this, I compute performance metrics separately for each city's test-set listings.

```{r city-stratified}
cities <- c("Madrid","Barcelona","Amsterdam")

city_perf <- map_df(cities, function(city_name) {
  test_city   <- test_class |> filter(city == city_name)
  test_city_r <- test_reg   |> filter(city == city_name)

  if (nrow(test_city) < 50 || nrow(test_city_r) < 50) {
    return(data.frame(City = city_name, AUC = NA, RMSE = NA, R2 = NA, n_class = nrow(test_city), n_reg = nrow(test_city_r)))
  }

  # Classification AUC
  prob_c  <- predict(m_rf_c, test_city, type = "prob")[,"Yes"]
  auc_c   <- suppressMessages(auc(roc(test_city$is_superhost, prob_c, levels=c("No","Yes"), direction="<")))

  # Regression RMSE + R²
  pred_r  <- expm1(predict(m_rf_r, test_city_r))
  act_r   <- expm1(test_city_r$log_price)
  rmse_r  <- sqrt(mean((act_r - pred_r)^2))
  r2_r    <- 1 - sum((act_r - pred_r)^2) / sum((act_r - mean(act_r))^2)

  data.frame(City = city_name, AUC = round(as.numeric(auc_c), 4),
             RMSE = round(rmse_r, 2), R2 = round(r2_r, 4),
             n_class = nrow(test_city), n_reg = nrow(test_city_r))
})

city_perf |>
  mutate(RMSE = paste0("$", RMSE)) |>
  styled_kable(caption = "City-Stratified Test Set Performance — Random Forest",
               col.names = c("City","AUC (Class)","RMSE (Reg)","R² (Reg)","n (class)","n (reg)"))

# Visual comparison
city_perf_long <- city_perf |>
  select(City, AUC, R2) |>
  pivot_longer(c(AUC, R2), names_to = "Metric", values_to = "Value")

ggplot(city_perf_long, aes(x = City, y = Value, fill = City)) +
  geom_col(alpha = 0.9, width = 0.5, colour = "white", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(Value, 3)), vjust = -0.35, size = 6.5,
            fontface = "bold", colour = "#2C3E50") +
  facet_wrap(~Metric, scales = "free_y",
             labeller = labeller(Metric = c(AUC = "Classification AUC", R2 = "Regression R²"))) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = pal_city) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.18))) +
  labs(title = "Model Generalization Across Cities",
       subtitle = "Random Forest performance on city-specific test subsets · trained on combined data",
       x = NULL, y = "Metric Value") +
  theme_airbnb() +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        panel.grid.major.x = element_blank())
```

These results reveal that model performance is not uniform across cities. Amsterdam tends to show the highest classification AUC but the widest regression errors, while Madrid and Barcelona cluster closer together. This makes sense: Amsterdam's rental market is more tightly regulated and expensive, which creates a more predictable distribution of host quality but a more volatile pricing landscape. Barcelona and Madrid, with somewhat more relaxed short-term rental environments at the time of data collection, show the opposite pattern. If this model were deployed in production, city-specific recalibration (or even separate models per market) would likely improve performance.

## 9.2 Anomaly Detection — Suspiciously Priced Listings

When a listing's actual price is very different from what the model predicts, that is worth investigating. The listing might have unmeasured luxury features, a stale placeholder price, or a genuinely unusual market position. I flag listings whose prediction error exceeds 2x the interquartile range to see where the model struggles most and what that tells us about the data's limits.

```{r anomaly-detection, fig.height=7}
# Define anomaly as |prediction error| > 2 IQR of all errors
all_preds   <- test_results_reg[[best_reg_idx]]$predictions
all_actuals <- test_results_reg[[best_reg_idx]]$actual
all_errors  <- all_actuals - all_preds

iqr_err     <- IQR(all_errors)
anomaly_thresh <- 2 * iqr_err

anomaly_df <- test_reg |>
  mutate(
    predicted_price = all_preds,
    actual_price    = all_actuals,
    error           = all_errors,
    abs_error       = abs(all_errors),
    is_anomaly      = abs_error > anomaly_thresh,
    anomaly_type    = case_when(
      error > anomaly_thresh  ~ "Overpriced",
      error < -anomaly_thresh ~ "Underpriced",
      TRUE                    ~ "Normal"
    )
  )

cat("Anomaly threshold (2×IQR): $", round(anomaly_thresh, 2), "\n")
cat("Anomalies detected:", sum(anomaly_df$is_anomaly),
    "(", round(mean(anomaly_df$is_anomaly)*100, 1), "% of listings)\n\n")

anomaly_df |>
  filter(is_anomaly) |>
  group_by(anomaly_type, city) |>
  summarise(n = n(), median_error = median(abs(error)),
            median_actual = median(actual_price),
            median_predicted = median(predicted_price), .groups = "drop") |>
  styled_kable(digits = 2, caption = "Anomalous Listings by Type and City")

ggplot(anomaly_df, aes(x = actual_price, y = predicted_price, colour = anomaly_type)) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "#BDC3C7",
              linewidth = 1.0) +
  geom_point(data = ~filter(., anomaly_type == "Normal"),
             alpha = 0.20, size = 2.0, shape = 16) +
  geom_point(data = ~filter(., anomaly_type != "Normal"),
             alpha = 0.70, size = 3.5, shape = 16) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = dollar) + scale_y_log10(labels = dollar) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("Normal"="#BDC3C7","Overpriced"="#C0392B","Underpriced"="#2471A3")) +
  labs(title = "Price Anomaly Detection",
       subtitle = paste0("Anomaly = |prediction error| > $", round(anomaly_thresh,0),
                         " (2×IQR) — detected ", sum(anomaly_df$is_anomaly),
                         " anomalies out of ", nrow(anomaly_df), " listings"),
       x = "Actual Price (USD, log scale)", y = "Predicted Price (USD, log scale)",
       colour = NULL) +
  theme_airbnb()
```

Most flagged listings fall into two interpretable categories. **Overpriced anomalies** (actual price far above what the model predicts) tend to cluster in premium neighbourhoods and likely reflect luxury features the data cannot capture: penthouse views, designer interiors, or event-capable spaces. **Underpriced anomalies** (actual price below predicted) often correspond to stale or inactive listings with placeholder prices, or promotional rates during low-demand periods. The fact that anomalies represent a small fraction of the test set is reassuring. It confirms that the model's learned pricing structure broadly matches the actual market. For a platform, these flagged listings could feed into a "pricing health check" recommendation system.

## 9.3 Learning Curves

Learning curves answer a practical question: would collecting more data actually improve my model? I train on progressively larger subsets and plot both training-set and cross-validation performance. If both curves converge, the model has learned what it can from the available features and more data would not help much. If a gap persists at full training size, the model is still overfitting, and more data or stronger regularisation could close it. My expectation was that ~50,000 listings would be plenty for the tree-based models, and the curves below confirm this.

```{r learning-curves, fig.width=20, fig.height=12}
set.seed(42)
lc_fracs  <- c(0.05, 0.10, 0.20, 0.35, 0.55, 0.80, 1.00)
ctrl_lc   <- trainControl(method = "cv", number = 3, classProbs = TRUE,
                           summaryFunction = twoClassSummary, verboseIter = FALSE)
ctrl_lc_r <- trainControl(method = "cv", number = 3, verboseIter = FALSE)

# ── Classification LC — Random Forest ──────────────────────────────
lc_class_df <- map_df(lc_fracs, function(frac) {
  n_sub  <- max(80L, round(nrow(train_class_balanced) * frac))
  s_idx  <- sample(nrow(train_class_balanced), n_sub)
  s_data <- train_class_balanced[s_idx, ]

  m_tmp <- suppressWarnings(
    train(is_superhost ~ ., data = s_data, method = "ranger",
          trControl = ctrl_lc, metric = "ROC", tuneLength = 1,
          num.trees = 100, importance = "none")
  )
  tr_prob <- predict(m_tmp, s_data, type = "prob")[,"Yes"]
  tr_auc  <- suppressMessages(
    as.numeric(auc(roc(s_data$is_superhost, tr_prob,
                       levels = c("No","Yes"), direction = "<"))))
  cv_auc  <- max(m_tmp$results$ROC, na.rm = TRUE)
  data.frame(n = n_sub, frac = frac, train_auc = tr_auc, cv_auc = cv_auc)
})

p_lc_c <- lc_class_df |>
  pivot_longer(c(train_auc, cv_auc), names_to = "set", values_to = "auc") |>
  mutate(set = case_match(set,
                      "train_auc" ~ "Training AUC",
                      "cv_auc"    ~ "CV AUC (3-fold)")) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = n, y = auc, colour = set, group = set)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.7) +
  geom_point(size = 5.0, shape = 21, fill = "white", stroke = 1.3) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("Training AUC" = "#1B4F72",
                                  "CV AUC (3-fold)" = "#E74C3C")) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = comma) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0.5, 1.0),
                     labels = number_format(accuracy = 0.01)) +
  labs(title = "Classification — Learning Curve (RF)",
       subtitle = "ROC-AUC vs training set size · gap = overfitting",
       x = "Training Set Size (n)", y = "ROC-AUC", colour = NULL) +
  theme_airbnb()

# ── Regression LC — Random Forest ──────────────────────────────────
lc_reg_df <- map_df(lc_fracs, function(frac) {
  n_sub  <- max(80L, round(nrow(train_reg) * frac))
  s_idx  <- sample(nrow(train_reg), n_sub)
  s_data <- train_reg[s_idx, ]

  m_tmp_r <- suppressWarnings(
    train(log_price ~ ., data = s_data, method = "ranger",
          trControl = ctrl_lc_r, tuneLength = 1,
          num.trees = 100, importance = "none")
  )
  tr_rmse <- sqrt(mean((s_data$log_price - predict(m_tmp_r, s_data))^2))
  cv_rmse <- min(m_tmp_r$results$RMSE, na.rm = TRUE)
  data.frame(n = n_sub, frac = frac, train_rmse = tr_rmse, cv_rmse = cv_rmse)
})

p_lc_r <- lc_reg_df |>
  pivot_longer(c(train_rmse, cv_rmse), names_to = "set", values_to = "rmse") |>
  mutate(set = case_match(set,
                      "train_rmse" ~ "Training RMSE",
                      "cv_rmse"    ~ "CV RMSE (3-fold)")) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = n, y = rmse, colour = set, group = set)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.7) +
  geom_point(size = 5.0, shape = 21, fill = "white", stroke = 1.3) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("Training RMSE" = "#1B4F72",
                                  "CV RMSE (3-fold)" = "#E74C3C")) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(title = "Regression — Learning Curve (RF)",
       subtitle = "RMSE (log-price scale) vs training set size · gap = overfitting",
       x = "Training Set Size (n)", y = "RMSE (log-price scale)", colour = NULL) +
  theme_airbnb()

(p_lc_c + p_lc_r) +
  plot_layout(guides = "collect") & theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

<div class="callout-insight">
When the training and cross-validation curves converge, the model has reached its data-efficiency limit: adding more of the same type would give diminishing returns. Any remaining gap represents overfitting that could be addressed through stronger regularisation, feature selection, or collecting different types of data (like photos or demand signals).
</div>

---

## 9.4 Cross-Model Feature Importance Comparison

If a feature ranks highly across very different model types (linear, regularised, tree-based, and SHAP), that is a good sign it is a real signal rather than an artefact of one particular algorithm. This section consolidates importance estimates from four classifiers into a single view.

```{r cross-model-importance, fig.width=20, fig.height=14}
# 1. Logistic Regression: absolute z-statistic, collapsed by factor group
glm_imp_df <- tidy(m_glm$finalModel) |>
  filter(term != "(Intercept)") |>
  mutate(
    Variable   = term |>
      str_replace("^city[A-Z].+$",              "city")            |>
      str_replace("^room_type.+$",              "room_type")        |>
      str_replace("^property_type_simple.+$",   "property_type")    |>
      str_replace_all("[._]", " ") |> str_squish(),
    importance = abs(statistic),
    model      = "Logistic Regression"
  ) |>
  group_by(Variable, model) |>
  summarise(importance = sum(importance), .groups = "drop")

# 2. Elastic Net: absolute coefficient at best lambda, collapsed
enet_coef_mat <- coef(m_enet_c$finalModel, s = m_enet_c$bestTune$lambda)
enet_imp_df <- tibble(
    term     = rownames(enet_coef_mat),
    coef_val = as.numeric(enet_coef_mat)
  ) |>
  filter(term != "(Intercept)", coef_val != 0) |>
  mutate(
    Variable   = term |>
      str_replace("^city[A-Z].+$",              "city")            |>
      str_replace("^room_type.+$",              "room_type")        |>
      str_replace("^property_type_simple.+$",   "property_type")    |>
      str_replace_all("[._]", " ") |> str_squish(),
    importance = abs(coef_val),
    model      = "Elastic Net"
  ) |>
  group_by(Variable, model) |>
  summarise(importance = sum(importance), .groups = "drop")

# 3. Random Forest: impurity importance
rf_imp_df <- tibble(
    Variable   = names(vi_c),
    importance = as.numeric(vi_c),
    model      = "Random Forest"
  ) |>
  mutate(Variable = Variable |> str_replace_all("[._]", " ") |> str_squish())

# 4. XGBoost SHAP: mean |SHAP|, dummy groups collapsed to factor name
shap_raw <- colMeans(abs(sv_c$S))
xgb_imp_df <- tibble(
    raw_name   = names(shap_raw),
    importance = as.numeric(shap_raw)
  ) |>
  mutate(
    Variable = raw_name |>
      str_replace("^city[A-Z].+$",              "city")            |>
      str_replace("^room_type.+$",              "room_type")        |>
      str_replace("^property_type_simple.+$",   "property_type")    |>
      str_replace_all("[._]", " ") |> str_squish(),
    model = "XGBoost (SHAP)"
  ) |>
  group_by(Variable, model) |>
  summarise(importance = sum(importance), .groups = "drop")

# Normalise each model's importance to [0, 1] and combine
norm_01 <- function(df) df |>
  mutate(importance = importance / max(importance, na.rm = TRUE))

all_imp_df <- bind_rows(
  norm_01(glm_imp_df),
  norm_01(enet_imp_df),
  norm_01(rf_imp_df),
  norm_01(xgb_imp_df)
)

# Keep features appearing in ≥2 model families, ranked by mean importance
top_vars_cross <- all_imp_df |>
  group_by(Variable) |>
  summarise(mean_imp = mean(importance, na.rm = TRUE),
            n_models = n(), .groups = "drop") |>
  filter(n_models >= 2) |>
  slice_max(mean_imp, n = 20) |>
  pull(Variable)

all_imp_df |>
  filter(Variable %in% top_vars_cross) |>
  mutate(Variable = fct_reorder(Variable, importance,
                                 .fun = function(x) mean(x, na.rm = TRUE))) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Variable, y = importance, fill = model)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), alpha = 0.88, width = 0.75) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Logistic Regression" = "#1B4F72",
    "Elastic Net"         = "#F39C12",
    "Random Forest"       = "#2E86C1",
    "XGBoost (SHAP)"      = "#8E44AD"
  )) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05)),
                     labels = number_format(accuracy = 0.01)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Cross-Model Feature Importance — Classification",
    subtitle = "Normalised importance (0–1) across 4 paradigms · features in ≥2 models · ordered by mean",
    x = NULL, y = "Normalised Importance (0–1)", fill = "Model"
  ) +
  theme_airbnb() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom",
        panel.grid.major.y = element_blank())
```

<div class="callout-insight">
Features that rank highly across all four methods (linear, regularised, tree ensemble, and SHAP) are the most trustworthy signals. They survive very different modelling assumptions. Features that only appear in one model's top rankings could be artefacts of that particular method rather than real predictive signals.
</div>

---

## 9.5 Results Summary Dashboard

<div class="stat-grid">
<div class="stat-card">
<div class="stat-number">`r round(test_results_class[[best_class_idx]]$auc, 3)`</div>
<div class="stat-label">Best Class AUC</div>
</div>
<div class="stat-card">
<div class="stat-number">`r best_class_name`</div>
<div class="stat-label">Best Classifier</div>
</div>
<div class="stat-card">
<div class="stat-number">$`r round(test_results_reg[[best_reg_idx]]$rmse, 0)`</div>
<div class="stat-label">Best Reg RMSE</div>
</div>
<div class="stat-card">
<div class="stat-number">`r round(test_results_reg[[best_reg_idx]]$r2, 3)`</div>
<div class="stat-label">Best Reg R²</div>
</div>
<div class="stat-card">
<div class="stat-number">`r best_reg_name`</div>
<div class="stat-label">Best Regressor</div>
</div>
<div class="stat-card">
<div class="stat-number">`r if (!is.na(stack_auc)) round(stack_auc, 3) else "—"`</div>
<div class="stat-label">Stack Class AUC</div>
</div>
</div>

---

## 9.6 Counterfactual Analysis — Pathway to Superhost

This is the part of the project I found most interesting. I take a typical non-Superhost, constructed from the median feature values of all non-Superhosts in the test set, and ask: what sequence of improvements would the model predict to push this host across the classification boundary? Each step is cumulative, building on the previous one. These are model-based projections under a ceteris paribus assumption, not guaranteed outcomes (see the caveat below).

```{r counterfactual-analysis, fig.width=20, fig.height=14}
# Construct a "typical non-Superhost" from test-set medians
typical_no <- test_class |>
  filter(is_superhost == "No") |>
  summarise(across(where(is.numeric), ~median(., na.rm = TRUE))) |>
  mutate(
    room_type            = "Entire home/apt",
    property_type_simple = as.factor(names(which.max(
      table(test_class$property_type_simple)))),
    city                 = factor("Madrid", levels = levels(test_class$city)),
    is_superhost         = factor("No", levels = c("No","Yes"))
  )

# Cumulative improvement scenarios
step_defs <- list(
  "1. Baseline\n(typical non-Superhost)"          = list(),
  "2. Response Rate -> 100%"                       = list(host_response_rate  = 100),
  "3. Acceptance Rate -> 95%"                      = list(host_response_rate  = 100,
                                                          host_acceptance_rate = 95),
  "4. +3 Years Experience"                         = list(host_response_rate  = 100,
                                                          host_acceptance_rate = 95,
                                                          host_tenure_days     = typical_no$host_tenure_days + 1095),
  "5. Detailed Description\n(desc_quality = 3)"    = list(host_response_rate  = 100,
                                                          host_acceptance_rate = 95,
                                                          host_tenure_days     = typical_no$host_tenure_days + 1095,
                                                          desc_quality         = 3L,
                                                          desc_length          = 600),
  "6. +Amenities (top 25%)"                        = list(host_response_rate  = 100,
                                                          host_acceptance_rate = 95,
                                                          host_tenure_days     = typical_no$host_tenure_days + 1095,
                                                          desc_quality         = 3L,
                                                          desc_length          = 600,
                                                          n_amenities          = quantile(test_class$n_amenities, 0.75)),
  "7. Fully Optimised\n(all improvements)"         = list(host_response_rate   = 100,
                                                          host_acceptance_rate  = 100,
                                                          host_tenure_days      = typical_no$host_tenure_days + 1825,
                                                          desc_quality          = 3L,
                                                          desc_length           = 800,
                                                          n_amenities           = quantile(test_class$n_amenities, 0.9),
                                                          neigh_superhost_rate  = quantile(test_class$neigh_superhost_rate, 0.75),  # NOT host-actionable
                                                          instant_bookable      = 1L)
)

cf_results <- map_df(seq_along(step_defs), function(i) {
  scen <- typical_no
  mods <- step_defs[[i]]
  for (nm in names(mods)) scen[[nm]] <- mods[[nm]]
  scen$is_superhost <- factor("No", levels = c("No","Yes"))
  prob <- predict(m_rf_c, scen, type = "prob")[,"Yes"]
  data.frame(step     = i,
             scenario = names(step_defs)[i],
             prob     = as.numeric(prob))
})

# Threshold lines
opt_thr_val <- opt_thr

# Pre-build threshold annotations (avoid overlap when thresholds are close)
thr_close <- abs(opt_thr_val - 0.5) < 0.06
thr_annotations <- if (thr_close) {
  list(
    annotate("label", x = 7.4, y = 0.50,
             label = paste0("Decision / Youden threshold (", round(opt_thr_val, 2), ")"),
             colour = "#2E86C1", size = 5.5, fontface = "bold", hjust = 1,
```
