```{r counterfactual-analysis, fig.width=20, fig.height=14}
             fill = "white", label.size = 0.3, label.padding = unit(0.4, "lines"))
  )
} else {
  list(
    annotate("label", x = 7.4, y = 0.50,
             label = "0.50 decision boundary", colour = "#2E86C1",
             size = 5, fontface = "bold", hjust = 1, fill = "white", label.size = 0.3,
             label.padding = unit(0.4, "lines")),
    annotate("label", x = 7.4, y = opt_thr_val,
             label = paste0("Youden threshold (", round(opt_thr_val, 2), ")"),
             colour = "#C88A00", size = 5, fontface = "bold", hjust = 1,
             fill = "white", label.size = 0.3, label.padding = unit(0.4, "lines"))
  )
}

cf_results |>
  mutate(
    above_opt  = prob >= opt_thr_val,
    above_half = prob >= 0.5
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(scenario, step), y = prob, group = 1)) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = reorder(scenario, step), xend = reorder(scenario, step),
                   y = 0, yend = prob), colour = "#EAECEE", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_line(colour = "#1B4F72", linewidth = 1.8) +
  geom_point(aes(colour = above_opt), size = 8, stroke = 1.2) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.5,        linetype = "dashed",
             colour = "#2E86C1", linewidth = 1.0) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = opt_thr_val, linetype = "dotted",
             colour = "#F39C12", linewidth = 1.0) +
  geom_text(aes(label = percent(prob, accuracy = 1)), hjust = -0.3,
            size = 5.5, fontface = "bold", colour = "#2C3E50") +
  thr_annotations +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("FALSE" = "#E74C3C", "TRUE" = "#27AE60"),
                      labels = c("FALSE" = "Below threshold",
                                 "TRUE"  = "Above threshold")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent, limits = c(0, 1),
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.08))) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title    = "Counterfactual Analysis — Pathway to Superhost",
    subtitle = "Cumulative improvements · predicted P(Superhost) at each step · % labels on points",
    x = NULL, y = "Predicted P(Superhost)", colour = NULL
  ) +
  theme_airbnb() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

```{r counterfactual-table}
cf_results |>
  mutate(
    prob      = round(prob, 4),
    Change    = round(prob - lag(prob, default = prob[1]), 4),
    Threshold = case_when(
      prob >= opt_thr_val ~ paste0("✓ Above Youden (", round(opt_thr_val, 2), ")"),
      prob >= 0.50        ~ "✓ Above 0.50",
      TRUE                ~ "✗ Below both"
    )
  ) |>
  select(Step = step, Scenario = scenario, `P(Superhost)` = prob,
         `ΔP(Superhost) vs prev` = Change, `Decision Status` = Threshold) |>
  mutate(Scenario = str_replace_all(Scenario, "\n", " "),
         Scenario = str_replace(Scenario, "^\\d+\\.\\s*", "")) |>
  styled_kable(caption = "Counterfactual Pathway: Cumulative Impact of Each Improvement")
```

<div class="callout-caution">
**Interpretation caveat**: This analysis translates *predictive associations* into guidance, but it does not establish causation. The step that produces the largest probability jump is the highest-ROI action according to the model, but real-world outcomes may differ because improvements interact in ways a ceteris paribus projection cannot capture. Also note that Step 7 includes `neigh_superhost_rate`, which is not something a host can change. It is included to show the model's full sensitivity, not as a recommendation.
</div>

---

# 10. Conclusions

## 10.1 Summary of Findings

In this project I applied 13 classification and 8 regression models to **`r format(nrow(df_clean), big.mark=",")`** Airbnb listings across Madrid, Barcelona, and Amsterdam. The two prediction tasks, Superhost status and nightly price, ended up revealing quite different things about the short-term rental market.

### Classification — Superhost Status

**Best model**: **`r best_class_name`** · Test AUC = **`r round(test_results_class[[best_class_idx]]$auc, 4)`**

| Finding | Detail |
|---------|--------|
| Behaviour drives Superhost status | Response rate, acceptance rate, and host tenure are the strongest predictors across all model families |
| Quality clusters geographically | Neighbourhood Superhost density is a useful feature; good hosts tend to concentrate in certain areas |
| Text signals add value | Listings with keywords like "luxury" or "modern" in their names show measurably different Superhost rates |
| Balancing the training data helped | Downsampling improved recall for the minority class without sacrificing overall AUC |
| Every model beat the baseline | Baseline accuracy: `r round(baseline_acc,4)`. All 13 models outperformed this substantially |

### Regression — Nightly Price

**Best model**: **`r best_reg_name`** · Test RMSE = **$`r round(test_results_reg[[best_reg_idx]]$rmse, 2)`** · R² = **`r round(test_results_reg[[best_reg_idx]]$r2, 4)`**

| Finding | Detail |
|---------|--------|
| Capacity is the top price driver | Number of guests and bedrooms are the dominant predictors of nightly price |
| Location commands a premium | Proximity to the city centre adds significant value, and Amsterdam listings are priced substantially higher than Madrid or Barcelona |
| Neighbourhood context matters | The neighbourhood's median price is one of the strongest features, so local market positioning is key |
| Luxury listings are hardest to predict | Model error increases sharply for listings in the top 15% price range, where unmeasured quality factors dominate |

### The key asymmetry

One of the most interesting findings is the contrast between the two tasks: **Superhost status is a behaviour story** (how a host operates), while **pricing is a property story** (what is offered and where). Host behaviour features that dominate the classification task are only marginally important for price prediction, and vice versa.

## 10.2 Methodological Contributions

Beyond applying the methods from Professor Morala's course, I made several analytical choices that extend the standard pipeline:

1. **Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KAN)**, a 2024 architecture not yet widely used in applied social science, benchmarked against established methods on both tasks
2. **Neighbourhood intelligence features**, engineered from local market context (median price, Superhost density, listing count) and recomputed on training data only to prevent leakage
3. **NLP feature engineering**: keyword indicators and description quality scores extracted from listing text, adding a signal dimension that purely numeric features miss
4. **Full calibration analysis**, going beyond accuracy and AUC to check whether predicted probabilities actually match observed rates
5. **SHAP-based explanations**: per-observation feature attributions for both classification and regression, which enabled the behaviour-vs.-property insight discussed below
6. **Price tier segmentation**: diagnosing where and why models fail by breaking the test set into price bands
7. **City-stratified evaluation**: testing whether a pooled model generalises fairly across markets with different regulatory and cultural contexts

## 10.3 Practical Recommendations

> **Important caveat**: These recommendations come from a predictive model trained on observational data, not from a causal experiment. Features that predict an outcome do not necessarily cause it. Each recommendation is tagged to distinguish plausible causal mechanisms from purely predictive associations.

**For hosts aiming for Superhost status:**

1. **Respond to every message.** Response rate is the single strongest predictor, and Airbnb's own criteria explicitly require above 90%. The mechanism is intuitive: faster responses lead to better guest experiences and higher ratings. `[Plausibly causal]`
2. **Accept most booking requests.** Acceptance rate is strongly associated with Superhost status, and the platform rewards non-selective hosts. `[Plausibly causal]`
3. **Stay on the platform.** Host tenure predicts Superhost probability, though this likely reflects accumulated reputation and learning rather than being something a host can directly accelerate. `[Predictive association]`
4. **Invest in your listing description.** Longer, more detailed descriptions are associated with higher Superhost probability. They signal commitment and reduce guest uncertainty. `[Plausibly causal]`
5. **Price competitively.** Listings priced near or below the neighbourhood median are more likely to be Superhosts, though the causal direction is unclear since experienced hosts may simply price more strategically. `[Predictive association]`

**For pricing strategy:**

1. **Capacity is the strongest price lever.** Listings that accommodate more guests and have more bedrooms command substantially higher rates. `[Plausibly causal]`
2. **Location sets the baseline.** Neighbourhood median price is one of the strongest features, and a listing's price is heavily influenced by what nearby competitors charge. `[Market-structural]`
3. **Amenities add value.** Listings with kitchen, air conditioning, and wifi are priced higher. `[Plausibly causal]`
4. **Superhost status carries a premium.** Superhosts tend to charge more, though whether the badge enables higher pricing or whether better listings independently achieve both is unclear. `[Predictive association]`

**For platform design:**

1. The Superhost programme seems to be working as intended: it identifies hosts with strong operational behaviours, not just hosts in desirable locations. `[Platform-structural]`
2. Model performance varies across cities, suggesting that city-specific thresholds or recalibration could improve the programme's accuracy in different markets. `[Predictive association]`
3. The anomaly detection framework (Section 9.2) could power a "pricing health check" to flag potentially mispriced or fraudulent listings. `[Platform-structural]`

## 10.4 Limitations

1. **Cross-sectional data.** The dataset is a single snapshot in time. It cannot capture seasonality, demand shocks, regulatory changes, or how individual hosts evolve. Conclusions about "what predicts Superhost status" are associational, not causal.
2. **Unmeasured quality.** Listing photos, interior design, cleanliness, and hyper-local factors (floor level, noise, view) are invisible to my models. This is likely the main reason luxury listings are hard to predict.
3. **Neighbourhood statistics in production.** I recompute neighbourhood features on training data only (Section 6), which is correct for modelling. In a live deployment, these would need to be pre-computed from historical data to avoid look-ahead bias at inference time.
4. **KAN maturity.** The KAN architecture (2024) is still under rapid development. My results are a snapshot of an evolving method, and performance may change as the `pykan` library matures.
5. **European focus.** Madrid, Barcelona, and Amsterdam share certain regulatory and cultural characteristics. These models may not transfer to markets with very different short-term rental norms (e.g., Tokyo, New York, or markets in the Global South).

## 10.5 Future Directions

- **Computer vision.** Listing photos are the most obvious missing signal. Pre-trained image models could extract quality proxies (brightness, style, cleanliness) that my text-based features cannot capture.
- **Text embeddings.** Replacing keyword indicators with sentence-transformer embeddings on full listing descriptions would capture richer semantic information about what hosts are offering.
- **Panel modelling.** Tracking individual listings over time would enable difference-in-differences or event-study designs to estimate the causal effect of earning Superhost status on pricing power.
- **Survival analysis.** Modelling time-to-Superhost as a hazard process could reveal whether the path to the badge follows a predictable trajectory or depends on early-career decisions.
- **Fairness audit.** Investigating whether model predictions or Superhost classification rates differ systematically by host demographics or neighbourhood income would address growing concerns about algorithmic equity in platform governance.
- **KAN interpretability.** As the `pykan` ecosystem matures, extracting the learned spline functions as symbolic rules could make KAN a uniquely interpretable non-linear method.

---

# 11. References

1. Inside Airbnb (2024). *Inside Airbnb: Adding data to the debate*. [http://insideairbnb.com/](http://insideairbnb.com/)

2. Liu, Z., Wang, Y., Vaidya, S., Ruehle, F., Halverson, J., Soljačić, M., ... & Tegmark, M. (2024). KAN: Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks. *arXiv preprint arXiv:2404.19756*. [https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.19756](https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.19756)

3. Lundberg, S. M., & Lee, S. I. (2017). A unified approach to interpreting model predictions. *Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems*, 30. [https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.1705.07874](https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.1705.07874)

4. Kuhn, M. (2008). Building Predictive Models in R Using the caret Package. *Journal of Statistical Software*, 28(5), 1–26. [doi:10.18637/jss.v028.i05](https://doi.org/10.18637/jss.v028.i05)

5. Friedman, J., Hastie, T., & Tibshirani, R. (2010). Regularization Paths for Generalized Linear Models via Coordinate Descent. *Journal of Statistical Software*, 33(1), 1–22. [doi:10.18637/jss.v033.i01](https://doi.org/10.18637/jss.v033.i01)

6. Wright, M. N., & Ziegler, A. (2017). ranger: A Fast Implementation of Random Forests for High Dimensional Data in C++ and R. *Journal of Statistical Software*, 77(1), 1–17. [doi:10.18637/jss.v077.i01](https://doi.org/10.18637/jss.v077.i01)

7. Chen, T., & Guestrin, C. (2016). XGBoost: A Scalable Tree Boosting System. *Proc. 22nd ACM SIGKDD*, 785–794. [doi:10.1145/2939672.2939785](https://doi.org/10.1145/2939672.2939785)

8. He, H., & Garcia, E. A. (2009). Learning from Imbalanced Data. *IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering*, 21(9), 1263–1284. [doi:10.1109/TKDE.2008.239](https://doi.org/10.1109/TKDE.2008.239)

9. Wood, S. N. (2017). *Generalized Additive Models: An Introduction with R* (2nd ed.). Chapman & Hall/CRC. [doi:10.1201/9781315370279](https://doi.org/10.1201/9781315370279)

10. Mayer, M., Caner, S., & Gunn, R. (2023). shapviz: SHAP Visualizations. *R package version 0.9.0*. [https://CRAN.R-project.org/package=shapviz](https://CRAN.R-project.org/package=shapviz)

11. Hastie, T., Tibshirani, R., & Friedman, J. (2009). *The Elements of Statistical Learning* (2nd ed.). Springer. [https://hastie.su.domains/ElemStatLearn/](https://hastie.su.domains/ElemStatLearn/)

12. James, G., Witten, D., Hastie, T., & Tibshirani, R. (2023). *An Introduction to Statistical Learning with Applications in R* (2nd ed.). Springer. [https://www.statlearning.com/](https://www.statlearning.com/)

13. Breiman, L. (2001). Random Forests. *Machine Learning*, 45(1), 5–32. [doi:10.1023/A:1010933404324](https://doi.org/10.1023/A:1010933404324)

14. Zou, H., & Hastie, T. (2005). Regularization and variable selection via the elastic net. *Journal of the Royal Statistical Society: Series B (Statistical Methodology)*, 67(2), 301–320. [doi:10.1111/j.1467-9868.2005.00503.x](https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-9868.2005.00503.x)

15. Ribeiro, M. T., Singh, S., & Guestrin, C. (2016). "Why should I trust you?": Explaining the predictions of any classifier. *Proceedings of the 22nd ACM SIGKDD International Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining*, 1135-1144. [doi:10.1145/2939672.2939778](https://doi.org/10.1145/2939672.2939778)

16. Kalehbasti, P. R., Nikolenko, L., & Rezaei, H. (2021). Airbnb price prediction using machine learning and sentiment analysis. In *Cross-Cultural Design* (pp. 173-184). Springer.

17. Zhang, Z., Chen, R. J., Han, L. D., & Yang, L. (2017). Key factors affecting the price of Airbnb listings: A geographically weighted approach. *Sustainability*, 9(9), 1635. [doi:10.3390/su9091635](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9091635)

18. Liang, S., Schuckert, M., Law, R., & Chen, C. C. (2020). The importance of marketer-generated content to peer-to-peer property rental platforms. *International Journal of Hospitality Management*, 84, 102329.

19. Grinsztajn, L., Oyallon, E., & Varoquaux, G. (2022). Why do tree-based models still outperform deep learning on typical tabular data? *Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems*, 35, 507-520.

20. Molnar, C. (2022). *Interpretable Machine Learning: A Guide for Making Black Box Models Explainable* (2nd ed.). [https://christophm.github.io/interpretable-ml-book/](https://christophm.github.io/interpretable-ml-book/)

---

# Session Information

```{r session-info}
sessionInfo()
```

---

*This report was prepared as a final project for the Advanced Modelling course, UC3M, Master in Computational Social Science, 2025–2026. Data from Inside Airbnb (public domain). All analysis is fully reproducible by knitting this document from source.*

**End of Report**
